title: Build the deliverable gate map separating evidence from open interview gates
what: Map every required deliverable (PDR, scope, technical requirements, stack, assets, platforms, subscriptions, crowdfunding plan, tools, skills) against evidence already in hand versus decisions still gated.
why: Keeps unsupported assumptions out of the roadmap so nobody builds on claims that were never verified.
doneWhen: The gate map exists with all ten deliverable rows filled and unresolved matters routed into documents 12-21 as explicit release gates.

title: Assemble the final planning package in the eleven required copy-ready sections
what: Produce the final document package structured as PDR, Project Scope, Technical Requirements, Tech Stack, Required Assets, Platforms, Subscriptions and Services, Crowdfunding Plan, Tools and Setup, Skills and Training, and the Traceability Matrix.
why: Gives the whole team one trustworthy, navigable source of truth instead of scattered notes.
doneWhen: All eleven sections exist as separate copy-ready documents in the deliverables set.

title: Enforce the traceability rule across the final package
what: Verify every final requirement or recommendation cites a reviewed asset, a confirmed interview answer, or an explicitly commissioned professional determination; anything else is excluded or marked as an open option.
why: Protects the team and future backers from decisions built on invented facts.
doneWhen: The traceability and consistency matrix links every requirement to an approved evidence source, with unresolved items marked as options requiring a decision.

title: Complete the crowdfunding evidence and asset readiness audit
what: Audit every existing campaign-capable asset, generated visual, and unsupported claim, and define the release gates, cost model, reward gate, and proof ladder for the future campaign.
why: Ensures the eventual campaign asks for money with honest evidence, not borrowed confidence.
doneWhen: The audit document exists covering asset register, forbidden claims, decision-dependent assets, cost model, reward gate, proof ladder, and release gate.

title: Confirm the operating entity or fiscal sponsor
what: Decide and legally confirm whether Worth Keeping operates as its own entity or under a fiscal sponsor, producing written confirmation usable in campaign materials.
why: Every promise to participants, partners, and backers needs a real, accountable entity behind it.
doneWhen: Entity formation or fiscal-sponsor agreement documents exist and the entity is eligible to run the chosen campaign.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

title: Produce a print proof of the armadillo hero sticker
what: Get the armadillo hero sticker physically printed as a proof and verify material, color, and QR scan quality.
why: The hero collectible must exist and scan in the real world before it anchors any public story.
doneWhen: A physical armadillo sticker proof exists and passes visual and scan inspection, with the result recorded.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Armadillo hero sticker

title: Produce print proof plus durability and scan test evidence
what: Run durability and scan testing on manufactured sample stickers and document the evidence for campaign and pilot use.
why: Recipients depend on stickers that survive real streets and real weather and still scan.
doneWhen: Documented durability and scan test results exist for manufactured samples, suitable for publication as proof.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

title: Capture real prototype photographs and QR scan footage
what: Photograph actual physical prototypes and film real QR scans to replace rendered mockups in campaign materials.
why: Backers deserve to see the real thing working, not a rendering pretending to be one.
doneWhen: A library of real prototype photos and scan footage exists and is cleared for campaign use.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

title: Generate, collision-check, print, and destination-test real QR codes
what: Replace all decorative QR marks with controlled test or production codes that have passed generation, collision, print, and destination testing.
why: A QR code shown as functional before testing is a promise the system cannot yet keep.
doneWhen: No published visual contains an untested QR code, and controlled codes have passed generation, collision, print, and destination tests.

source.section: Existing material that must not be presented as fact

title: Select the honest proof level the campaign will reach before launch
what: Choose which proof-ladder level (concept, physical, workflow, partner, or pilot proof) the campaign will truthfully claim, based on what evidence will exist at launch.
why: The campaign must never visually imply more proof than the evidence supports.
doneWhen: A proof level is selected, approved, and recorded as the standard every campaign page claim is checked against.

title: Obtain final stakeholder approval of the hero line
what: Get the approved three-party hero line signed off by stakeholders for use as the campaign headline or video opening.
why: The first sentence backers read must be one the whole team stands behind.
doneWhen: Written stakeholder approval of the hero line exists for campaign use.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Approved hero line

title: Run trademark/name review and brand-owner approval on the tagline
what: Complete a trademark and name-conflict review of the tagline and obtain brand-owner approval before public use on end cards, packaging, social, and the page footer.
why: Protects the project from a forced rename after the community has bonded with the name.
doneWhen: Trademark/name review is documented and brand-owner approval is recorded.

title: Verify brand palette accessibility contrast and final brand file ownership
what: Run accessibility contrast checks on the brand palette and type direction, and confirm ownership of the final brand files.
why: A dignity-first brand has to be readable by everyone, including people with low vision.
doneWhen: Contrast ratios pass accessibility standards and brand file ownership is documented.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Brand palette and type direction

title: Clear the eight story illustrations for public use
what: Verify generation/provider usage rights, run likeness review, and confirm final-resolution/print suitability for the eight story illustrations.
why: The visual journey must not accidentally trade on anyone's likeness or unlicensed work.
doneWhen: Rights, likeness, and resolution checks are documented for all eight illustrations.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Eight story illustrations

title: Confirm armadillo sticker ownership, edition rules, and artist/AI disclosure
what: Document final ownership of the armadillo hero sticker art, define its edition rules, and prepare honest artist/AI-generation disclosure for campaign use.
why: Collectors deserve to know exactly what they hold and who made it.
doneWhen: Ownership, edition, and disclosure statements are approved and attached to the asset.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Armadillo hero sticker

title: Vet the print-shop scene against real production agreements
what: Before using the print-shop scene publicly, ensure it does not imply a real printer or production agreement unless one actually exists.
why: Implied partnerships that do not exist would betray the trust the project runs on.
doneWhen: The scene is either backed by a signed print agreement or edited/captioned so no agreement is implied.

title: Vet the partner handoff scene against written partner authorization
what: Before public use, confirm the partner handoff scene does not imply a confirmed community partner without written authorization.
why: Community partners must consent to how they are represented before they appear in the story.
doneWhen: The scene is backed by written partner authorization or revised so no confirmed partner is implied.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Partner handoff scene

title: Align pass/scan/redeem scenes with the approved state machine and reward rules
what: Review the pass, scan, and redeem illustrations against the approved redemption state machine and reward rules, and correct any mismatch.
why: The how-it-works story must match how it actually works, or recipients get confused at the worst moment.
doneWhen: Each scene matches the approved state machine and reward rules, confirmed in a documented review.

title: Replace 'grant' language and all figures in the ledger/grant scene
what: After the legal/accounting classification decision, replace the word 'grant' and every dollar figure in the ledger scene with approved terms and amounts.
why: Money-flow language carries legal weight; the wrong word could mislead recipients and regulators alike.
doneWhen: The scene contains only counsel/CPA-approved terminology and figures.

title: Keep the city-ripple vision image framed as aspirational
what: Ensure captions and surrounding copy for the city-ripple scene present community-scale impact as intended future vision, not achieved outcome, until evidence exists.
why: Hope is honest when it is labeled as hope.
doneWhen: The image's placement and copy explicitly frame impact as aspirational, verified in the pre-publish review.

title: Strip illustrative metrics, partner-like logos, and unsupported commitments from the static explainer
what: Edit the static explainer content so no fabricated metric, partner-look logo, or commitment the project cannot keep remains.
why: The explainer teaches the story; it must not teach fiction as fact.
doneWhen: A reviewed version of the explainer exists with all flagged elements removed or labeled illustrative.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Static explainer concept

title: Complete privacy, AI, accessibility, uptime, and claim review of the story prototype
what: Before the story prototype accompanies a public campaign, review its production privacy posture, AI usage, accessibility, uptime expectations, and every claim it makes.
why: A live demo becomes a public promise the moment backers can touch it.
doneWhen: Each review area is documented with pass/fix outcomes and the prototype is approved for its chosen use (private review or public companion).

title: Rebuild the money-flow diagram with only approved formula, custody, overhead, and timing
what: Insert into the money-flow explainer only the formula, custody arrangement, overhead figure, and timing that counsel and accounting have approved.
why: The accountability diagram is the campaign's trust centerpiece; every number on it must be real.
doneWhen: The published diagram contains only approved values with a documented approver for each.

source.section: Existing campaign-capable assets — Money-flow diagram direction

title: Ground the passpack product mockup in approved production facts
what: Before campaign use, replace the fully rendered passpack mockup's unapproved vendor, dieline, dimensions, material, cost, QR behavior, label, and 'micro-grants' copy with confirmed or clearly-illustrative content.
why: A rendered product shown as real sets expectations no vendor has agreed to meet.
doneWhen: The mockup is either rebuilt from confirmed production specs or explicitly labeled a concept rendering, with 'micro-grants' copy removed.

title: Correct the platform-sticker-collection visual for rights, editions, QR, and contrast
what: Remove or replace decorative QR marks, and confirm artwork rights, edition rules, print separations, cut paths, and accessible contrast before using the collection visual.
why: Collectibility claims and fake codes must not stand in for verified editions and working scans.
doneWhen: The visual passes the generated-visual release check with rights, edition, print, and contrast items confirmed.

title: Review sticker-collection-expanded for implied people, places, rarity, and edition promises
what: Audit the expanded collage for images implying real people, locations, rarity tiers, or edition promises that do not exist, and correct or caption them before use.
why: A mood board must not quietly become a product promise.
doneWhen: The collage is cleared or corrected, and it is used only as mood/direction, never as a SKU sheet.

title: Rebuild the pledge-tracking-engine visual without fabricated metrics, portraits, or instant-grant claims
what: Recreate the scan-to-impact lifecycle graphic so it contains no exact-looking live metrics, no real-person-style portraits, and no 'micro-grant instantly' claims that conflict with manual review and unresolved classification.
why: Recipients and backers must never be shown an automated promise the team fulfills by hand.
doneWhen: A rebuilt visual exists that matches the manual-review reality and approved terminology, and the old version is retired from campaign use.

title: Replace see-the-math-circle icon labels with interview-approved terms
what: Rework the accountability graphic so its icons no longer imply completed controls, amounts, custody, verification, or accounting rules that are not approved.
why: 'See the math' only works if the math shown is the real, approved math.
doneWhen: The graphic carries only approved labels and no implied-but-unbuilt controls.

title: Resolve the four byte-identical community-circle variants into distinct assets or one composition
what: Decide whether community-circle, community-distributors, no-disruption-circle, and stickerpass-hero-bridge remain one composition used once, or are intentionally recropped/redesigned into genuinely distinct visuals.
why: Counting one image four times inflates the asset inventory the campaign plan depends on.
doneWhen: A decision is recorded and the asset register reflects either one composition or newly created distinct designs.

source.section: Recovered generated-visual asset register — community-circle and byte-identical variants

title: Exclude optimized JPEG derivatives from the campaign asset count
what: Mark the static explainer's optimized JPEGs as derivatives of the PNG set in the asset register so they do not inflate the creative inventory.
why: Planning honestly starts with counting honestly.
doneWhen: The asset register lists the JPEGs as derivatives, not additional creative concepts.

title: Run the human release check on every generated visual before publication
what: For each generated visual, a named human owner verifies visible words, numbers, QR codes, logos, apparent people, locations, rights, and implied operations; decorative QRs are removed or replaced and metrics labeled illustrative.
why: One unnoticed fake detail in one image can undo the honesty of the whole campaign.
doneWhen: Every published generated visual has a completed, owner-signed release check on file.

title: Sweep all materials for figures and claims that must not be presented as fact
what: Remove or clearly label as illustrative every unsupported item: the $2 pledge, $1.60/$0.40 split, 500-sticker drop, 50-sticker packs, 70/20/8/2 rarity, 20% overhead, seven-day review, 90-day retention, participant/business/sticker counts, 'instant' disbursement, persona stories, partner-like names/logos/quotes, invented metrics, 'micro-grant'/'payout'/tax-deductibility language, specific Austin launch details, and any uncosted production or delivery date.
why: Every one of these numbers looks like a promise to someone who needs it to be true.
doneWhen: A documented sweep confirms no listed item appears as fact anywhere in public-facing material.

source.section: Existing material that must not be presented as fact

title: Produce authorized founder/team biographies and portraits

why: Backers fund people; the people shown must have said yes to being shown.
doneWhen: Approved bios and portraits exist with written authorization for campaign use.

doneWhen: Each published name has a written publication approval on file, or the name is omitted.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

why: People experiencing homelessness especially deserve control over how their image and story are used.
doneWhen: A permissions file documents a signed release for every person, organization, quote, image, and logo appearing in campaign materials.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust; Campaign release gate

what: Build and approve the campaign financial model and the use-of-funds budget backing every money claim on the page.
why: Backers' money deserves a plan that was checked before it was promised.
doneWhen: An approved financial model and use-of-funds budget exist with a named approver.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

title: Write the risk and limitations disclosure

why: Telling backers what could go wrong is how a trust-first project treats its supporters.
doneWhen: An approved risk and limitations disclosure is ready for the campaign page.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

title: Produce privacy policy, terms, participant safeguards, and campaign FAQ
what: Draft and approve the privacy policy, terms of service, participant safeguarding commitments, and the campaign FAQ.

doneWhen: Approved privacy, terms, safeguards, and FAQ documents exist and are linked from campaign materials.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Proof and trust

doneWhen: Title, short description, and campaign promise are approved and locked for page and video use.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

doneWhen: Goal and minimum viable raise are documented with every input traced to a sourced cost row.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page; Campaign release gate

title: Choose the funding model and confirm platform eligibility
what: Select the funding model, choose the campaign platform, and produce the platform eligibility statement for the operating entity.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

what: Create the campaign's use-of-funds chart with platform/payment fees, taxes, contingency, and fulfillment costs explicitly included.
why: Hiding the unglamorous costs would make every other number on the page a little bit false.
doneWhen: The chart is published-ready with all four often-omitted cost classes visibly included.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

what: Draft the campaign milestone timeline showing dependencies between milestones and the named owner of each decision.
why: Backers can only hold the project accountable if they can see who decides what, and when.
doneWhen: The timeline exists with every milestone carrying its dependencies and a named decision owner.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

what: Decide between a reward tier table and an explicit no-reward/donation structure, and produce the chosen structure for the page.

doneWhen: A complete tier table or explicit no-reward structure is approved for the campaign page.

doneWhen: The fulfillment section is complete with locations, restrictions, estimates, and replacement policy approved.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

what: Draft the section describing the team, roles, and governance of the operating entity for the campaign page.
why: People fund projects they can see are run by accountable humans.
doneWhen: The team and governance section is drafted, fact-checked, and approved.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

what: Draft the campaign page section explaining what impact will be measured, how, and what evidence currently exists.

doneWhen: The impact and measurement section is complete with every claim tied to evidence or clearly framed as intended.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Campaign page

why: Answering the hard questions up front is cheaper than losing trust later.
doneWhen: FAQ and risks/challenges sections are approved and ready to publish.

doneWhen: An accessibility review is documented showing captions, alt text, transcripts, contrast, and mobile layouts all pass.

what: Complete video post-production: captions, full transcript, final audio mix, thumbnail, end card, and short cuts for social channels.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Video and photography

what: Plan and produce an alternate campaign video that works without on-camera participation from vulnerable people.

what: Build and publish a pre-launch landing page that captures email addresses with proper consent language and handling.

doneWhen: The landing page is live, capture works, and consent language and data handling are verified compliant.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Launch operations

what: Draft and approve the five-stage campaign email sequence covering announcement, launch, midpoint, final days, and outcome.
why: Supporters who raised their hands deserve to hear the story at every turn, not just the ask.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Launch operations

what: Build the campaign's social media calendar with approved copy and graphics for each planned post.
why: A planned voice stays honest under launch pressure; an improvised one drifts.

what: Compile the press kit: one-page brief, approved images, team bios, contact information, verified facts, and FAQ.

doneWhen: A complete press kit exists with every element approved and every fact verified.

what: Compile launch outreach lists that the team legitimately owns and has permission to contact.

what: Draft reusable templates for backer updates covering progress, delays, and decisions.
why: Templates ready in advance mean backers hear from the team even in hard weeks.

what: Produce support response scripts for expected campaign questions and an escalation matrix naming who handles what.
why: Every backer question answered well is trust compounding.
doneWhen: Scripts cover the expected question set and the escalation matrix names an owner for each severity.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Launch operations

what: Define and approve the communication rules and responses for refunds, cancellations, delays, funding shortfall, overfunding, and scope changes before launch.
why: Deciding the hard answers calmly, in advance, is how the team keeps its word under pressure.
doneWhen: Approved written responses exist for each of the six scenarios.

what: Define the final reward SKUs and a complete bill of materials for each.
why: You can only promise what you can actually build, count, and ship.
doneWhen: Every reward SKU has a complete, costed bill of materials.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

what: Collect written vendor quotes and physical production samples for every reward item.
why: A sample in hand is the only proof a reward can be delivered as promised.
doneWhen: Quotes and approved samples exist for every reward SKU.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Document quantity breaks and reorder minimums
what: Record vendor quantity price breaks and reorder minimums for each reward item.
why: Knowing the reorder math up front prevents an overfunded campaign from becoming an underdelivered one.
doneWhen: Quantity breaks and reorder minimums are documented per SKU in the fulfillment plan.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Cost packaging, postage, tax, storage, pick/pack, and replacements
what: Calculate the full fulfillment cost stack per reward: packaging, postage, taxes, storage, pick/pack labor, and replacement allowance.
why: Fulfillment costs sunk many honest campaigns; counting them first keeps this one solvent.
doneWhen: A per-SKU fulfillment cost sheet covers all six cost categories with sourced amounts.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Define address collection and retention rules
what: Set the rules for how backer addresses are collected, stored, retained, and deleted.
why: Backers hand over their home address; it must be guarded like the trust it is.
doneWhen: Documented collection and retention rules exist and are reflected in the privacy policy.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Set domestic and international shipping service boundaries
what: Decide which regions the campaign will ship to and document the domestic/international service boundaries.
why: Saying no to a region up front is kinder than failing it later.
doneWhen: Service boundaries are documented and reflected on the campaign page's fulfillment section.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Create the quality-control checklist and acceptance sampling plan
what: Define the QC checklist and acceptance sampling method for inspecting reward production runs.
why: Nobody who backed the mission should receive a reward that feels like an afterthought.
doneWhen: A QC checklist and sampling plan exist and are agreed with the vendor.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Choose the inventory and shipment tracking method
what: Select and set up the method for tracking reward inventory and outbound shipments.
why: Knowing where every package is means never having to guess when a backer asks.
doneWhen: A tracking method is in place that can report inventory counts and per-shipment status.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Name the fulfillment support owner and set the exception budget
what: Assign a named owner for fulfillment support and approve a budget for exceptions such as losses and replacements.
why: When a package goes missing, one accountable human with a budget fixes it fast.
doneWhen: A support owner is named and an exception budget is approved in the financial model.

source.section: Campaign assets that are decision-dependent — Fulfillment

title: Source entity/governance costs: formation, insurance, contracts, accounting
what: Obtain written quotes or approved budget lines for formation or fiscal-sponsor fees, insurance, contracts, and accounting.
why: The boring legal costs protect everyone the project touches; they belong in the goal.
doneWhen: Every entity/governance cost row has a sourced amount and a named owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

what: Collect printer and vendor quotes covering design, proofing, setup, per-sticker unit cost, serialization/QR, and packaging.

doneWhen: Every product cost row has a vendor-quoted amount and an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source participant-operations costs from the partner operating plan
what: Cost partner staffing, training, support, transportation, safety, and accommodations using the partner operating plan.
why: Supporting participants well costs real money; budgeting it is how the mission stays real.
doneWhen: Every participant-operations cost row has a sourced amount and an owner from the partner operating plan.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

doneWhen: Every distributor-support cost row has an amount sourced from the legal/accounting determination and financial model, with an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source reward costs: unit, packaging, postage, replacements, tax/customs
what: Collect vendor and carrier quotes for reward unit costs, packaging, postage, replacements, and tax/customs.
why: Rewards must never quietly eat the funds meant for participants.
doneWhen: Every reward cost row has a quoted amount and an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source software costs: hosting, database, AI if retained, email/SMS, monitoring, support, analytics
what: Cost the software stack from approved provider tiers and the usage model, covering hosting, database, AI (if retained), email/SMS, monitoring, support tooling, and analytics.
why: The system that keeps promises to recipients has to stay paid for.
doneWhen: Every software cost row has an amount from an approved provider tier and usage model, with an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source campaign production costs: video, photography, editing, design, copy, accessibility, events
what: Cost campaign production from internal capacity or vendor quotes across video, photography, editing, design, copywriting, accessibility work, and events.
why: Telling the story well is work, and unbudgeted work gets done badly or not at all.
doneWhen: Every campaign-production cost row has a sourced amount and an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source platform and payment costs: platform fee, card processing, failed/refunded payments
what: Cost platform fees, card processing, and failed/refunded payments from the chosen platform's terms.
why: Fees taken off the top must be counted before the goal is set, not discovered after.
doneWhen: Every platform/payment cost row has an amount sourced from the chosen platform's terms, with an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source contingency amounts for production, schedule, returns, fraud, vendor, and demand variance
what: Set contingency amounts per the approved risk policy covering production, schedule, returns, fraud, vendor, and demand variance.
why: Contingency is the honest admission that plans meet reality.
doneWhen: Every contingency row has an amount tied to the approved risk policy, with an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Source taxes and administration costs with qualified accounting guidance
what: Obtain qualified accounting guidance to cost applicable campaign, sales/use, and income taxes plus filing and bookkeeping.
why: Tax surprises after a successful raise would take money straight from the mission.
doneWhen: Every tax/administration cost row has an amount backed by qualified accounting guidance, with an owner.

source.section: Cost model that must be completed before choosing a goal

title: Pass every proposed reward through the eight-point feasibility gate
what: Before any reward appears on the page, verify: mission and sticker-ownership consistency; no diversion of beta participant inventory or funds; full unit economics (packaging, fees, postage, taxes, loss, replacement, support); vendor-confirmed quantity, quality, timing, and reprint terms; delivery date covering proofing through contingency; fulfillment without exposing participant identity; documented scarcity/rarity/artist/partner/environmental/impact claims; and entity/platform permission.
why: A reward that fails any one of these tests would quietly take from the people the beta serves.
doneWhen: Each published reward has a completed eight-point gate review on file.

title: Confirm the authorized campaign owner
what: Name and confirm the person authorized to own and publish the campaign on behalf of the operating entity.
why: Someone accountable has to be able to say 'publish' — and to answer for it afterward.
doneWhen: The authorized campaign owner is confirmed in writing by the operating entity.

title: Approve the campaign purpose and stated proof level
what: Formally approve the campaign's purpose and the proof-ladder level it will state, confirming the page never implies more than the evidence supports.
why: This approval is the promise-keeping moment for the entire campaign.
doneWhen: Campaign purpose and proof level are approved and recorded against the selected proof-ladder level.

title: Approve funds custody, distributor-support classification, accounting, and disclosures
what: Obtain counsel and accounting approval of funds custody arrangements, the distributor-support classification, the accounting treatment, and all required disclosures.
why: Handling other people's money correctly is the floor, not the ceiling, of this mission.
doneWhen: Written approvals exist for custody, classification, accounting, and disclosures.

title: Verify every public claim has evidence and a named approver
what: Build a claim ledger listing every public campaign claim with its evidence source and named approver, and close every gap before publish.
why: Trust is built one verifiable sentence at a time.
doneWhen: The claim ledger covers every public claim with evidence and approver, and no claim is unresolved.

title: Test page, checkout, video, captions, transcript, mobile layout, links, and emails
what: Run end-to-end pre-publish tests of the campaign page, checkout flow, video playback, captions, transcript, mobile layout, all links, and the email sends.
why: A broken checkout or dead link on launch day squanders the community's one first impression.
doneWhen: A documented test pass covers all eight areas with every failure fixed and retested.

title: Verify the final campaign page frames all future outcomes as intended or proposed
what: Review the entire campaign page so every future outcome in the supported narrative is framed as intended or proposed, not achieved, until pilot evidence exists.
why: The story earns its emotional power only if hope is never dressed up as a result.
doneWhen: A documented page review confirms every future-outcome statement carries intended/proposed framing, with no achieved-outcome language before pilot evidence.

source.section: Campaign story already supported by supplied assets

title: Verify rewards and fulfillment have named cost, vendor, schedule, privacy, and support owners
what: As a release-gate check, confirm every reward and fulfillment commitment has a named owner for its cost, vendor, schedule, privacy, and support dimensions.
why: A promise to backers without a named human behind it is a promise nobody is keeping.
doneWhen: A signed-off checklist names an owner for cost, vendor, schedule, privacy, and support on every reward and fulfillment item.

title: Assign launch-day roles, access, monitoring, support coverage, and update cadence
what: Before going live, assign named launch-day roles, confirm everyone's platform access, set up monitoring, schedule support coverage, and commit to a backer update cadence.
why: Launch day rewards teams that decided who does what before the adrenaline hit.
doneWhen: A launch-day roster documents roles, verified access, monitoring, support shifts, and the update cadence.

title: Accept the Pre-Design Review as the planning baseline
what: Chrix reviews and formally accepts the PDR, closing the interview and authorizing detailed design and planning.
why: Everyone downstream — participants, partners, investors — deserves a build that starts from an agreed, honest baseline.
doneWhen: The PDR document is marked approved by Chrix with a dated acceptance; the doc records the interview closed 2026-07-18.

title: Establish an authoritative repository baseline and release history
what: Create the canonical source-control baseline for the recovered main app and story app, with tagged history from which all development proceeds.
why: Without a trusted baseline, no one can prove what code the team is actually shipping to vulnerable users.
doneWhen: A single authoritative repo (or repos) exists with a tagged baseline commit and documented release history; both applications build from it.

source.section: Existing evidence and maturity / PDR exit criteria

title: Represent the exact beta slice and non-negotiable release gates in the backlog
what: Translate the approved beta scope and every non-waivable release gate into explicit backlog items before detailed build work begins.
why: Gates only protect people if they are visible in the plan, not just in a document.
doneWhen: The backlog contains the beta slice and each release gate as trackable items, reviewed against the PDR.

title: Confirm the issuing/contracting entity and entity path
what: Decide and confirm the final for-profit issuing/contracting entity, ownership, and capitalization path before any contracts, crowdfunding, data collection, or money custody.
why: Every promise to an investor, partner, or participant needs a real legal entity standing behind it.
doneWhen: The entity path is documented and the entity is formed or confirmed in writing, with ownership and cap table recorded.

title: Write the authority matrix for Chrix, Brad, and Sarah
what: Document exact financial and approval roles for all current venture collaborators, superseding the interim 'Chrix decides' baseline.
why: Clear authority protects collaborators from ambiguity and participants from unaccountable decisions.
doneWhen: A written authority matrix names each person's decision, approval, and financial authority and is accepted by all named parties.

title: Engage qualified securities counsel
what: Retain independent securities counsel to advise on the crowdfunding instrument, disclosures, and communications.
why: Investors are protected only when the raise is shaped by qualified counsel, not founder optimism.
doneWhen: An engagement letter with qualified securities counsel is signed.

title: Engage business/entity and promotions counsel
what: Retain counsel for entity formation, contracts, and promotion/offer law questions distinct from securities work.
why: Businesses and recipients rely on offers that are lawful, not just well-intentioned.
doneWhen: An engagement letter with business/entity and promotions counsel is signed.

title: Engage a tax/accounting adviser
what: Retain an independent tax and accounting adviser for participant-value classification, revenue recognition, and books.
why: Participants must never be harmed by a payment the venture misclassified.
doneWhen: An engagement letter with a qualified tax/accounting adviser is signed.

title: Engage an insurance adviser
what: Retain an insurance adviser to scope coverage for field operations, liability, and the Austin pilot.
why: Field work with vulnerable people carries real risk; insurance is part of taking that seriously.
doneWhen: An insurance adviser is engaged and has received the pilot operating description.

title: Name the community-safeguarding owner
what: Appoint the accountable owner for participant safety, consent, and incident handling, with veto authority in their domain.
why: Someone must be personally accountable for keeping the most vulnerable people in the system safe.
doneWhen: A named safeguarding owner is documented in the authority matrix with defined veto authority.

source.section: Governance baseline / Stop and change controls

title: Name the privacy/security owner
what: Appoint the accountable owner for data protection, security decisions, and incident duties, with veto authority in their domain.
why: Recipients trust the system with their data only because a named human guards it.
doneWhen: A named privacy/security owner is documented in the authority matrix with defined veto authority.

source.section: Governance baseline / Stop and change controls

title: Design segregation of duties for material money movement
what: Define roles so no one person can create, approve, execute, and reconcile a material money movement.
why: Good controls protect honest people from suspicion and participants from theft.
doneWhen: A written control describes who may create, approve, execute, and reconcile money movement, with no single person holding all four.

title: Stand up the claims register
what: Create the register in which every public numerical, partner, participant, legal, tax, return, or delivery claim gets a source and a named approver.
why: Worth Keeping's credibility rests on never publishing a claim it cannot back.
doneWhen: The claims register exists, is in active use, and every published claim to date appears in it with source and approver.

source.section: Governance baseline / PDR risks and controls

title: Stand up the decision register
what: Create the register that records project decisions with date, decider, and rationale, as required active alongside the claims register at baseline.
why: A written decision trail is how collaborators, advisers, and investors can trust why the venture did what it did.
doneWhen: The decision register exists, is in active use, and baseline decisions to date (including PDR acceptance) are recorded in it.

title: Establish the scope ceiling and weekly gate reviews
what: Adopt a scope ceiling and weekly gate cadence, with an explicit rule that no safety or financial gate is waived to hit the Christmas date.
why: Deadline pressure must never be allowed to cut corners that protect people.
doneWhen: The scope ceiling and weekly gate review are documented and the first weekly review has occurred.

title: Adopt the stop and change control process
what: Operationalize the immediate-pause, revise, and stop triggers and the change-approval rule (Chrix approves scope changes; domain owners hold vetoes).
why: Knowing exactly when to pause is how the venture keeps its promise to do no harm.
doneWhen: The pause/revise/stop triggers and change-approval path are documented and acknowledged by all named owners.

title: Produce the money/value decision brief
what: Assemble the decision brief that frames participant-value classification, custody, and disbursement questions for professional advisers.
why: Advisers can only protect participants if the venture gives them the full, honest picture.
doneWhen: A written money/value decision brief is delivered to counsel and the tax adviser.

title: Build the partner and business outreach package
what: Create the materials used to approach the Austin community partner and prospective participating businesses, with no premature public naming.
why: Partners and businesses deserve a clear, truthful pitch before being asked to stand beside the program.
doneWhen: An outreach package exists and has been reviewed against the claims rules; outreach conversations have begun.

title: Set up the chart of accounts
what: Establish the chart of accounts for the venture covering campaign proceeds, participant-value obligations, fees, and reserves.
why: Every dollar promised to a participant must be traceable from day one.
doneWhen: A chart of accounts is created in the accounting system and reviewed by the tax/accounting adviser.

title: Define the Austin pilot area envelope
what: Select one Austin pilot area (or a deliberately small set of adjacent areas) as the planning envelope, with expansion requiring change approval.
why: A bounded area keeps the pilot small enough to run safely and honestly.
doneWhen: The pilot area is documented in the plan and referenced by the change-control process.

title: Obtain the legal/tax/benefits classification of participant value
what: Get a written qualified determination of how participant value is classified before naming, promising, accruing, or executing any value; 'micro-grant' is not yet approved.
why: A misclassified payment could cost a participant their benefits — the exact opposite of the mission.
doneWhen: A written qualified determination of participant-value classification is on file before any value is named, promised, accrued, or executed.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the entity, securities eligibility, and Wefunder terms determination
what: Get counsel's determination on the issuing entity, ownership, securities eligibility, capitalization, and Wefunder terms.
why: Community investors deserve a raise structured correctly before it is offered to them.
doneWhen: A written counsel determination covers entity, ownership, eligibility, capitalization, and Wefunder terms.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the funds custody and revenue treatment determination
what: Get a qualified determination on funds custody, revenue recognition, refunds, chargebacks, restricted-use treatment, and disbursement rails.
why: Money held on behalf of participants and sponsors must sit on rails a professional has approved.
doneWhen: A written determination covering custody, revenue recognition, refunds, chargebacks, restricted use, and disbursement rails is on file.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the participant identity/KYC and benefits-interaction determination
what: Get a qualified determination on participant identity/KYC, tax reporting, sanctions screening, benefits interaction, and equitable alternatives.
why: Identity requirements must never become a wall that excludes the people the program exists to serve.
doneWhen: A written determination covering KYC, tax reporting, sanctions, benefits interaction, and equitable alternatives is on file.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the Austin legal and field-liability requirements determination
what: Get a qualified determination of Austin solicitation, promotion, prize/offer, privacy, accessibility, insurance, and field-liability requirements.
why: Field teams and partners can only operate confidently when local law is mapped, not assumed.
doneWhen: A written determination of applicable Austin requirements is on file and reflected in operating procedures.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the age and consent rules determination
what: Get a qualified determination of age and consent rules for recipients and any optional profile/personalization.
why: Consent is only real when the rules around it are settled before anyone is asked.
doneWhen: Written age and consent rules for recipients and optional personalization are approved and on file.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Obtain the data retention, processor, and consumer-rights determination
what: Get a qualified determination on data retention, processor agreements, incident duties, and consumer rights obligations.
why: Respecting recipients means knowing exactly how long their data lives and what rights they hold.
doneWhen: A written determination covering retention, processor agreements, incident duties, and consumer rights is on file.

source.section: Professional determinations required before public launch

title: Produce the printer-ready platform-owned sticker collection
what: Finalize the collectible sticker art as a printer-ready collection where platform-owned art stays visibly primary and business attribution never turns the front into an ad.
why: The art is the dignity of the exchange — a gift worth keeping, not an advertisement.
doneWhen: Printer-ready files for the collection exist, reviewed against the design principle that platform art remains primary.

title: Select the two-code or approved front/back identification model via scan testing
what: Test and approve the sticker identification model (two-code or alternative front/back scheme) based on real scan results.
why: The whole loop starts with a scan that has to work for anyone, on any phone, in the field.
doneWhen: Scan-test results are documented and one identification model is formally approved.

title: Write the physical production specification
what: Document the sticker specification — material, size, finish, code placement — that the printer must produce.
why: A precise spec is how the first sticker in a stranger's hand matches the one the team imagined.
doneWhen: A written production specification exists and is accepted by the chosen printer.

title: Produce the serialized data export and print proof
what: Generate the secure serialized code export and obtain a printer proof of serialized stickers.
why: Serialization is what makes every sticker traceable and every claim about it honest.
doneWhen: A secure serialized export has been produced and a physical print proof received and reviewed.

source.section: In scope — Physical system / Milestone plan — G2

title: Run the batch scan test and count reconciliation on printed samples
what: Scan-test a printed sample batch and reconcile counts against the serialized export.
why: Proof in hand — not a mockup — is what earns the right to put stickers in the field.
doneWhen: Batch scan-test results and a count reconciliation are logged with no unexplained discrepancy.

title: Design controlled pilot packaging and the chain-of-custody SOP
what: Define how sticker batches are packaged for the pilot and the custody-transfer procedure from print through distributor hands.
why: Chain of custody is how the team can always say, truthfully, where every sticker went.
doneWhen: Packaging design and a written custody SOP exist covering every handoff from printer to distributor.

source.section: In scope — Physical system / Workstreams — Physical production

title: Write defect, loss, theft, duplicate, expiry, recall, and unused-inventory procedures
what: Document the procedure for each physical-inventory exception: defective stickers, loss, theft, duplicates, expiry, recall, and unused stock.
why: Planning for things going wrong is how the program stays trustworthy when they do.
doneWhen: Written procedures exist for all seven exception types and are referenced by the custody SOP.

title: Demonstrate the controlled end-to-end staged workflow
what: Run the full serialized-sticker → scan → offer → authenticated redemption → obligation → reconciliation loop in a controlled, staged setting with no real participants.
why: The team must prove the loop to itself before ever asking a vulnerable person to trust it.
doneWhen: A staged end-to-end run completes every loop step and the results are documented.

title: Produce the campaign demo from the staged workflow
what: Capture a truthful demo of the working staged loop for use in the crowdfunding campaign, with no illustrative values or generated people presented as evidence.
why: Investors should see the real thing working, not a simulation dressed up as proof.
doneWhen: A campaign demo asset exists, sourced from the staged workflow, and passes claims review.

title: Confirm Wefunder Community Round feasibility and entity readiness
what: Verify that the confirmed entity is eligible and ready for a Wefunder Community Round before campaign assets are finalized.
why: Community investors should only be invited onto a platform the venture is actually cleared to use.
doneWhen: Wefunder eligibility and entity readiness are confirmed in writing.

title: Approve one security instrument with no guaranteed-return language
what: Select and get counsel and platform approval for a single security instrument and its risk/return mechanics.
why: One clean, honest instrument keeps the promise to investors simple and true.
doneWhen: One security instrument is approved by counsel and platform, with confirmed absence of guaranteed-return language.

source.section: In scope — Crowdfunding preparation / Acceptance criteria — Campaign-ready

title: Prepare financials and draft Form C inputs
what: Assemble the financial statements and draft Form C inputs required for the regulated crowdfunding filing.
why: Full, accurate disclosure is the baseline of respect for every community investor.
doneWhen: Financials and draft Form C inputs are complete and delivered to counsel for review.

title: Build the costed use-of-funds budget that reconciles
what: Produce a bottom-up budget where costed minimum, target, maximum, fees, reserve, and use of proceeds all reconcile.
why: Asking for money honestly means knowing exactly what each dollar will do.
doneWhen: Minimum, target, maximum, fees, reserve, and use of proceeds reconcile in a reviewed budget document.

source.section: Acceptance criteria — Campaign-ready / Milestone plan — G3

title: Produce the campaign page, video, and founder/team story
what: Create the Wefunder campaign page, video, and founder/team story using the approved demo and physical samples.
why: The story earns support only when it is told without pity, savior language, or invented evidence.
doneWhen: Campaign page, video, and team story are complete and every claim in them passes the claims register.

title: Write the campaign risks, FAQs, and disclosures
what: Draft the explicit risk disclosures, FAQs, and platform-required disclosures for the round, with costed milestones and no guaranteed-return language.
why: Investors deserve to see the risks as clearly as the vision.
doneWhen: Risks, FAQs, and disclosures are drafted and approved by counsel and the platform.

source.section: In scope — Crowdfunding preparation / 12-pre-design-review.md PDR risks and controls

title: Select a maximum of five investor perks from the 35-option menu
what: Choose up to five investor perks from the documented 35-option perk menu.
why: A short, deliverable perk list is a promise the team can actually keep.
doneWhen: No more than five perks are selected and documented, each traceable to the 35-option menu.

title: Cost and schedule every physical perk
what: For each physical perk, obtain quotes, proofs, landed cost, a named owner, and a delivery schedule.
why: A perk promised without a costed plan becomes a broken promise to a backer.
doneWhen: Every physical perk has documented quotes, proofs, landed cost, owner, and delivery schedule.

title: Create separate business sponsorship packages
what: Design business sponsorship packages that are clearly distinct from the securities offering and never represented as securities or charitable deductions.
why: Local businesses deserve a clean, honest way to participate without legal confusion.
doneWhen: Sponsorship packages exist, reviewed by counsel to confirm they are not represented as securities or charitable deductions.

title: Run investor warmup and build the warm audience
what: Execute the pre-launch audience warmup so a warm investor audience exists before the round opens.
why: A campaign that launches to silence puts the whole beta — and the people waiting on it — at risk.
doneWhen: A warm audience is documented (list size and engagement) and warmup communications have been sent within platform rules.

source.section: In scope — Crowdfunding preparation / Milestone plan — G3

title: Define the investor update cadence
what: Set the schedule and format for investor updates during and after the campaign.
why: Regular, honest updates are how a community round stays a community.
doneWhen: A written update cadence with owner and channel is part of the campaign plan.

title: Audit campaign content for fabricated or unapproved representations
what: Verify that no generated person, illustrative number, unconfirmed partner, or guaranteed outcome is represented as fact anywhere in campaign materials.
why: The campaign's integrity is the product's integrity — one faked image would poison both.
doneWhen: A documented audit confirms all campaign content is free of generated people, illustrative numbers as fact, unconfirmed partners, and guaranteed outcomes.

title: Clear artwork rights for the campaign and sticker collection
what: Confirm and document that the venture holds the rights to all sticker art and campaign creative, passing the artwork-rights release gate before public crowdfunding.
why: Artists and the community both deserve a campaign built only on art the venture truly has the right to use.
doneWhen: Written rights documentation covers every artwork used in the campaign and sticker collection, and the artwork-rights release gate is recorded as passed.

title: Pass the campaign release checklist
what: Assemble and pass the campaign release checklist confirming the entity, security, financial disclosures, budget, platform acceptance, claims, risks, artwork rights, and fulfillment-plan gates before the round opens.
why: One final honest checklist is what stands between the team and asking the public for money too soon.
doneWhen: Every campaign release-checklist item passes with evidence, or the launch is held; platform and legal approvals are on file.

title: Decide whether the Sticker Oracle remains a labeled campaign demonstration
what: Run the privacy, content, cost, and fallback review that decides whether the existing Oracle may appear as a clearly labeled campaign demonstration; production Oracle/AI personalization stays out of scope.
why: A demo that could be mistaken for the real product would mislead the very people being asked to invest.
doneWhen: A documented review covering privacy, content, cost, and fallback concludes with a keep-as-labeled-demo or remove decision.

source.section: Explicitly out of scope for the first beta

title: Launch the platform-approved public round
what: Open the Wefunder Community Round at the earliest responsible date after all G3 campaign-readiness gates pass.
why: This is the moment the community is invited to own a piece of the mission — it has to be done right.
doneWhen: The round is live on Wefunder with platform approval and all campaign-ready acceptance criteria met.

title: Run launch support operations
what: Operate the campaign day-to-day: monitoring, responding to questions, and executing planned launch support activity.
why: Backers who show up with questions deserve a team that shows up with answers.
doneWhen: Launch support is staffed and active for the duration of the round, with inquiries tracked to response.

title: Keep investor communications active through the round
what: Publish investor updates per the approved cadence, with every claim passing the claims register and no misleading securities communication.
why: Trust built during the raise is the foundation for everything the beta must prove.
doneWhen: Updates are published on cadence throughout the round and each is logged in the claims register.

title: Build the anonymous-first recipient scan and explanation
what: Implement the recipient scan flow that gives a clear, accessible explanation and live offer with no account required for the first scan.
why: A person's first moment with Worth Keeping should ask nothing of them and explain everything.
doneWhen: A recipient can scan a sticker and see the explanation and live offer with no account and no profile collection.

title: Build the optional consented preference profile
what: Implement optional, explicit, granular consent-based preferences for offer relevance, with no sale of personal data.
why: Relevance should be a gift the recipient chooses, never a profile built behind their back.
doneWhen: A recipient can opt in with granular consent, see and revoke it, and the system records consent in the ledger; no personal data leaves the platform.

title: Build the sticker batch and lifecycle system
what: Implement batch creation, secure code export, print QA support, activation, assignment, transfer, void, and recall states.
why: Full lifecycle control is what keeps every sticker accountable from print run to recipient.
doneWhen: Every lifecycle state (create, export, activate, assign, transfer, void, recall) works in staging with audit records.

title: Implement QR anti-copy and duplicate-redemption controls
what: Use random high-entropy codes, one-way storage where practical, state-machine enforcement, rate limits, and a review queue against copying and duplicate redemption.
why: Fraud against the system is ultimately theft from the participants it serves.
doneWhen: Codes are high-entropy, state transitions are enforced, rate limits and the review queue are active, and duplicate-redemption attempts are demonstrably blocked in staging.

title: Build distributor onboarding, packs, activity, and support workflows
what: Implement distributor onboarding/status, pack assignment, activity view, support access, and partner-assisted access paths.
why: Distributors give first — the system owes them clarity about everything that follows.
doneWhen: A distributor can be onboarded, assigned a pack, view their verified activity, and reach support, including via a partner-assisted path.

title: Build business/sponsor workflows
what: Implement business prospecting, agreement, location, staff access, offer templates, optional custom offer review, funding/status, and aggregate reporting.
why: Businesses fund the loop; they deserve a controlled, verifiable workflow instead of vague promises.
doneWhen: A business can be set up through agreement, locations, staff access, and offers, and can view funding status and aggregate reports in staging.

title: Build offer reveal through redemption, dispute, and correction
what: Implement offer reveal, claim, expiry, authenticated redemption, dispute, reversal, and correction — with no POS integration required.
why: The redemption moment is where dignity, commerce, and accountability all meet.
doneWhen: The full offer lifecycle including authenticated redemption, dispute, reversal, and correction works in staging without POS integration.

title: Implement staff verification integrity controls
what: Enforce authenticated staff, per-location authorization, idempotency keys, a correction/reversal workflow, and an audit log for redemption verification.
why: Honest business staff are protected by controls that make collusion and mistakes visible.
doneWhen: Verification requires authenticated, location-authorized staff; repeat submissions are idempotent; corrections and every action appear in the audit log.

title: Build the idempotent ledger where only verified redemption creates obligations
what: Implement ledger/obligation creation so that only a verified redemption can create a financial obligation, exactly once, under the approved formula.
why: One clean rule — verified redemption moves money — is the financial heart of participant trust.
doneWhen: Staging tests prove no scan, impression, or duplicate verification can create an obligation, and each verified redemption creates exactly one auditable obligation.

source.section: In scope — Product workflows / Acceptance criteria — Pilot-ready

title: Build the human-reviewed disbursement queue with dual approval
what: Implement the value/disbursement queue with human review, dual approval, payout batching, execution evidence, failure/reissue handling, exception/appeal workflow, and reconciliation.
why: Every disbursement touches a real person's life; two sets of eyes and a paper trail honor that.
doneWhen: No disbursement can execute without dual approval; execution evidence, failure/reissue, exceptions, and reconciliation all function in staging.

source.section: In scope — Product workflows / 12-pre-design-review.md PDR risks and controls

title: Build fraud/risk flags, case review, appeal, and closure
what: Implement fraud and risk flagging with a case-review workflow, appeal path, and documented closure.
why: A fair appeal path means no participant or distributor is judged by an algorithm alone.
doneWhen: A flagged event can be reviewed, appealed, and closed with a recorded outcome in staging.

title: Build operator, finance, partner, support, business, and auditor views
what: Implement the six role-based views so each role sees exactly what it needs and nothing more.
why: Transparency for each role — without exposing anyone's data to the wrong eyes.
doneWhen: All six role views exist in staging with role-appropriate data and least-privilege access.

title: Build aggregate impact reporting gated by claims approval
what: Implement aggregate impact reporting whose outputs require claims approval before publication.
why: Impact numbers must earn belief — approved, aggregate, and never built from a person's story.
doneWhen: Aggregate reports generate in staging and are blocked from publication until a claims approval is recorded.

title: Implement anonymous-first privacy controls
what: Enforce data minimization, a consent ledger, retention jobs, and role-based access against privacy overcollection.
why: The least the system can know about a person is the most it should ever collect.
doneWhen: Minimization rules, consent ledger, automated retention jobs, and role-based access are implemented and verified in staging.

title: Produce the threat model and data map
what: Create the security threat model and the data map covering consent, retention, access, and incident response.
why: You can only protect data you have honestly mapped and threats you have honestly named.
doneWhen: A written threat model and data map exist and are accepted in the security release review.

title: Complete WCAG accessibility QA
what: Run accessibility QA across the recipient, distributor, business, and operator surfaces.
why: The first scan often happens on a borrowed phone in hard circumstances — accessibility is the mission, not a checkbox.
doneWhen: WCAG QA is complete with results documented and exceptions formally recorded.

title: Write the role matrix with least-privilege access and account recovery
what: Document every user role, its least-privilege access rights, and its account-recovery path.
why: Least privilege protects participants; recovery paths protect the people doing the work.
doneWhen: A role matrix covers every role with least-privilege access and a tested account-recovery path.

source.section: In scope — Operations and readiness / Acceptance criteria — Pilot-ready

title: Deliver traceable automated tests and staging acceptance
what: Build the automated test suite traceable to requirements and pass staging acceptance for the production candidate.
why: Tests are how the team proves the system is safe before real people depend on it.
doneWhen: Automated tests trace to requirements and the production candidate passes technical, security, operations, and accessibility acceptance in staging.

source.section: Workstreams — Product engineering / Milestone plan — G5

title: Pass the security/privacy/accessibility release review
what: Complete the formal release review of the threat model, data map, consent, retention, access, incident response, and WCAG results, documenting any exceptions.
why: A formal review is the moment someone accountable says: this is safe enough for real people.
doneWhen: The release review is held and passed with all exceptions documented and accepted by the privacy/security owner.

title: Sign the community-partner agreement and SOPs
what: Execute the written agreement with the Austin operating partner covering recruitment, onboarding, safety, support, inventory handoff, and escalation; no public naming without written authorization.
why: The partner carries the human side of the program — the agreement is the promise made to them in return.
doneWhen: A signed partner agreement and accompanying SOPs are on file, including the public-naming authorization terms.

source.section: In scope — Operations and readiness / 12-pre-design-review.md Value exchange

title: Produce participant consent, safety, and support materials
what: Create participant consent, training, safety, support, incident, complaint, suspension, appeal, and exit materials — with no story, image, or testimonial required for access.
why: Every participant deserves to know exactly what they are joining, how they are protected, and how they can leave.
doneWhen: The full participant materials set exists, reviewed by the safeguarding owner, with documented incident, complaint, appeal, and exit paths.

source.section: In scope — Operations and readiness / 12-pre-design-review.md PDR risks and controls

title: Complete business agreements, staff training, offer rules, and dispute handling
what: Sign participating-business agreements and deliver staff training, offer rules, and the dispute-handling procedure.
why: Trained, confident business staff make the redemption moment respectful instead of awkward.
doneWhen: Agreements are signed and staff at each participating business complete training on offers, verification, and disputes.

title: Pass the three-business readiness test
what: Verify business-operations readiness with three participating businesses, or a reduced cohort explicitly approved by the launch owner.
why: Real offers from real businesses are what make the sticker worth scanning.
doneWhen: Three businesses (or the approved reduced cohort) pass the readiness test with results documented.

source.section: Workstreams — Business operations / 12-pre-design-review.md Recommended pilot planning baseline

title: Recruit and approve pilot distributors through the partner
what: Recruit and approve up to 10 community distributors via the operating partner within the pilot envelope.
why: Distributors are the human beginning of every loop — chosen with care, supported from day one.
doneWhen: An approved distributor roster (up to 10) exists with partner-led onboarding records for each.

title: Run the community-operations tabletop exercise
what: Exercise the participant policy, incident, complaint, and appeal system in a tabletop with the partner, ending in partner sign-off.
why: Rehearsing the hardest moments on paper means no one improvises them with a real person.
doneWhen: The tabletop exercise is complete and the partner has signed off on the community-operations system.

title: Complete partner staff onboarding and rehearsals
what: Partner staff complete onboarding, safety, incident, consent, inventory, and support rehearsals before any real participant use.
why: The people standing next to participants must be ready before the first sticker changes hands.
doneWhen: Every partner staff member has completed all six rehearsal areas with completion recorded.

title: Complete business staff offer and redemption rehearsals
what: Business staff rehearse the offer and redemption workflows end-to-end before the pilot opens.
why: A rehearsed redemption keeps the recipient's moment at the counter smooth and dignified.
doneWhen: Staff at every participating business complete offer and redemption rehearsals with completion recorded.

title: Exercise backup/restore, incident response, rollback, and support escalation
what: Run live drills of backup/restore, incident response, rollback, and support escalation before real participants depend on the system.
why: When something breaks mid-pilot, a practiced team protects people; an unpracticed one scrambles.
doneWhen: All four drills are executed with documented results and any gaps remediated.

title: Write the operational runbooks
what: Produce the runbooks covering pilot operations, exceptions, and escalations referenced by the readiness materials.
why: Runbooks turn tribal knowledge into something the whole team can act on at 9pm on a Friday.
doneWhen: Runbooks exist for pilot operations and are used successfully during the rehearsal drills.

title: Complete the beta launch checklist
what: Assemble and pass the launch checklist confirming partner, insurance, consent, support, inventory, business, finance, and incident readiness before real participant use.
why: The checklist is the final, honest answer to 'are we actually ready to serve people?'
doneWhen: Every launch-checklist item passes with evidence, or the launch is held.

source.section: In scope — Operations and readiness / Milestone plan — G6

title: Bind insurance coverage for the pilot
what: Put the adviser-recommended insurance coverage in force before real participant operations begin.
why: Insurance is the safety net under every field interaction the pilot creates.
doneWhen: Coverage is bound and certificates are on file before any real participant use.

source.section: Workstreams — Governance and finance / Milestone plan — G6

title: Verify every active sticker is unique, scan-tested, activated, assigned, and traceable
what: Audit the full active-sticker inventory against the pilot-ready criterion before launch.
why: Traceable inventory is the difference between a controlled pilot and stickers in the wind.
doneWhen: An inventory audit confirms every active sticker is unique, scan-tested, activated, assigned, and traceable, with the audit logged.

title: Verify least-privilege access and account recovery for every role
what: Confirm before launch that every user role has least-privilege access and a working account-recovery path.
why: Access checks protect participant data from every account the pilot creates.
doneWhen: A pre-launch verification confirms least-privilege access and tested recovery for every role.

title: Verify duplicate-obligation prevention and correction paths before launch
what: Confirm that verification cannot create duplicate financial obligations and that every obligation has an authorized correction/reversal path with immutable audit evidence.
why: Financial integrity has to be proven before the first real obligation exists, not after.
doneWhen: Pre-launch tests demonstrate duplicate prevention, correction/reversal, and immutable audit evidence, with results logged.

title: Run the controlled Austin beta within the approved envelope
what: Operate the 2–4 week live pilot: serialized stickers issued through distributors, real recipient scans, live offers, authenticated redemptions, human-reviewed disbursements, and full record reconciliation.
why: This is the whole point — real people, real dignity, real proof.
doneWhen: The beta runs within the approved envelope (≤10 distributors, ≤3 businesses, ≤1,000 stickers, one pilot area) and produces the real end-to-end proof.

source.section: Milestone plan — G7 / 12-pre-design-review.md Primary beta proof

title: Confirm each active distributor completes at least one valid end-to-end loop
what: Track that every active distributor completes at least one valid loop, or document a non-product reason why not, before any scale-up.
why: One real completed loop per distributor is the honest minimum bar for saying the service works.
doneWhen: Every active distributor has a logged valid loop or a documented non-product reason.

source.section: Acceptance criteria — Beta success / 12-pre-design-review.md Recommended pilot planning baseline

title: Reconcile all verified redemptions one-to-one with the obligation formula
what: Verify during the beta that every verified redemption reconciles one-to-one with the approved obligation formula and that no unauthorized or duplicate disbursement/value event occurs.
why: Perfect reconciliation is the proof that participant money moved exactly as promised.
doneWhen: A reconciliation report shows one-to-one matching for all verified redemptions and zero unauthorized or duplicate disbursement events.

title: Hit the 90% execution service window and track all failures
what: Measure that at least 90% of approved executions complete within the disclosed service window and that every failure has a tracked resolution.
why: People waiting on real value deserve delivery on the schedule they were told.
doneWhen: Beta metrics show ≥90% of approved executions within the disclosed window and every failure resolved with a tracked record.

title: Reconcile pilot inventory with no unexplained active-code discrepancy
what: Reconcile physical and system inventory during and at the end of the beta.
why: Every unexplained sticker is an unexplained risk to the program's integrity.
doneWhen: The end-of-beta inventory reconciliation shows zero unexplained active-code discrepancies.

title: Resolve every critical safety, privacy, financial, and security incident
what: Track all beta incidents and drive every critical safety, privacy, financial, or security incident to resolution.
why: The beta cannot be called a success while any serious harm is still open.
doneWhen: The incident log shows no unresolved critical safety, privacy, financial, or security incident at beta close.

title: Verify distributors and partner staff understand activity, value, execution, and appeals
what: Check during the beta that distributors and partner staff can understand their activity, expected value, actual execution, and how to appeal.
why: Transparency only counts if the people it is for can actually use it.
doneWhen: Documented checks confirm distributors and partner staff can explain their activity, expected value, execution status, and the appeal path.

title: Verify businesses can verify and correct transactions without POS integration
what: Confirm during live operations that business staff can verify and correct real transactions using only the no-POS workflow.
why: The no-POS promise is what makes participation possible for small local businesses.
doneWhen: Live-beta evidence shows businesses verifying and correcting transactions without any POS integration.

title: Run beta research: consent, metrics, observation, and interviews
what: Collect consented research data — metrics, observation, and interviews — during the beta per the beta-research workstream.
why: Listening carefully during the beta is how the program learns what participants actually experienced.
doneWhen: Consented metrics, observation notes, and interviews are collected per the research plan and ready for synthesis.

title: Publish the impact report with only approved aggregate evidence
what: Produce the beta impact report containing only claims-approved aggregate evidence — no individual stories, generated people, or illustrative numbers.
why: The first public proof of impact must be as honest as the program claims to be.
doneWhen: The impact report is published with every figure traced to approved aggregate evidence in the claims register.

title: Synthesize the beta evidence report
what: Synthesize the beta research and operational data into the evidence report that grounds the exit decision.
why: A decision this consequential deserves evidence, not anecdotes.
doneWhen: The evidence report is complete, covering the beta-success criteria with supporting data.

title: Compile the remediation backlog
what: Capture every defect, gap, and improvement surfaced by the beta into a prioritized remediation backlog.
why: Honestly naming what still needs fixing is part of the promise to everyone who invested trust.
doneWhen: A prioritized remediation backlog exists covering all issues surfaced during the beta.

title: Make the continue/revise/pause/stop exit decision
what: Hold the exit review — desired by December 21, 2026, fallback January 2027 — and decide continue, revise, pause, or stop based on the evidence report and remediation backlog.
why: Participants, partners, and investors all deserve a decision made on evidence rather than momentum.
doneWhen: A documented continue/revise/pause/stop decision exists, referencing the evidence report and remediation backlog.

title: Implement recipient role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build the recipient role: scan, view explanation/offer, optional consent/profile, claim, redeem, request support — with no access to distributor identity, sponsor financials, or operational records.
why: Recipients get full dignity and zero exposure to the machinery behind the gift.
doneWhen: Role tests show every listed recipient capability works and every prohibition is denied.

title: Implement distributor role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build the distributor role: view assigned inventory, verified activity, expected/actual value status, training, help, and appeal — without the ability to activate inventory, verify their own redemption, or alter accounting.
