why: Distributors see their own honest record without ever being put in a position to game it.
doneWhen: Role tests show all distributor views work and self-verification, activation, and accounting writes are denied.

title: Implement partner staff role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build partner staff functions: onboard/support distributors, issue/return packs, record accommodations and incidents, initiate cases — with no unilateral approve-and-execute money movement.
why: Partner staff can care for distributors without ever holding sole control of money.
doneWhen: Role tests show partner staff workflows function and money movement requires a second authorized actor.

title: Implement business staff role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build business staff functions: view location offers and verify/correct eligible redemptions — with no access to participant identity/value or other businesses' data.
why: Front-line staff can honor a redemption without ever seeing who the recipient is or what they're worth on paper.
doneWhen: Role tests show verification/correction works and participant identity, value, and cross-business data are denied.

title: Implement business owner/sponsor role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build sponsor functions: manage approved locations, offers, funding inputs, and view aggregate reports — with no access to recipient profiles or raw distributor records.
why: Sponsors see their impact in aggregate without turning participants into surveilled data points.
doneWhen: Role tests show sponsor management and aggregate reporting work and all individual-level access is denied.

title: Implement support role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build support functions: view minimum case context, communicate, escalate, document resolution — with no ability to alter immutable events or financial records.
why: Support can help people without becoming a back door into the ledger.
doneWhen: Role tests show support case workflows function and immutable/financial writes are denied.

title: Implement finance reviewer role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build finance reviewer functions: review obligations, approve/reject batches, record execution and reconciliation — with a rule that the reviewer cannot originate the verified redemption they approve without second review.
why: Separation of duties keeps every dollar of participant value above reproach.
doneWhen: Role tests show review/approval/reconciliation works and originator-approves-own-redemption is blocked.

title: Implement operator/admin role capabilities and prohibitions
what: Build operator functions: configure approved entities, batches, offers, roles, queues, reports — with no ability to bypass audit, idempotency, or dual-control rules.
why: Even the people running the system stay inside its accountability rails.
doneWhen: Role tests show operator configuration works and audit/idempotency/dual-control bypass attempts fail.

title: Implement auditor read-only role
what: Build an auditor role that can view approved immutable events, reconciliations, access history, and reports with zero write access.
why: Anyone checking the system's honesty can see everything and change nothing.
doneWhen: Role tests show auditor reads succeed and every write attempt is denied.

title: Require MFA on operator, finance, and organization-owner accounts
what: Enforce multi-factor authentication for all privileged account types in the platform.
why: The accounts that can move value or change rules deserve the strongest lock.
doneWhen: Privileged login without a second factor is impossible in staging and production.

title: Build account-free recipient first scan
what: Ensure a recipient's first sticker scan requires no account creation.
why: The moment someone is handed a sticker should never start with a signup wall.
doneWhen: E2E test completes scan-to-offer-view with no account and no required personal data.

title: Support partner-assisted distributor access and a documented non-digital fallback
what: Build distributor access that works through partner assistance and document a non-digital fallback path for distributors without reliable devices.
why: No distributor loses their standing or their record because they don't own a working phone.
doneWhen: Partner-assisted access works in testing and the non-digital fallback procedure is written and approved.

title: Implement the sticker inventory state machine
what: Build the draft code → minted → print_exported → print_verified → activated → packed → assigned → passed → claimed → redeemed lifecycle plus exception states (quarantined, void, lost, stolen, damaged, recalled, expired), recording actor, timestamp, reason, prior state, and correlation ID on every terminal/exception transition. Printed codes stay unusable until batch activation.
why: Every physical sticker is accounted for from mint to redemption, so nothing valuable leaks or gets lost silently.
doneWhen: State-transition tests pass for every legal transition, every illegal transition is rejected, and exception transitions log all five required fields.

title: Implement the offer lifecycle state machine
what: Build the draft → pending_approval → active → paused → retired lifecycle for offers.
why: Offers only reach recipients after deliberate approval, and can be paused the moment something is wrong.
doneWhen: Tests confirm every offer transition follows the approved lifecycle and no unapproved offer is claimable.

source.section: Required state machines — Offer and claim

title: Implement the claim state machine with atomic creation controls
what: Build revealed → claimed → redemption_pending → redeemed with expired, cancelled, disputed, and reversed as controlled outcomes; claim creation atomically enforces availability, active dates, recipient/device abuse controls, and per-offer caps.
why: Recipients can trust that a revealed offer is real, and sponsors can trust caps are never oversold.
doneWhen: Concurrency tests show caps never oversubscribe and all controlled outcome transitions behave per spec.

source.section: Required state machines — Offer and claim

title: Implement the redemption and participant-value state machine
what: Build initiated → verified → obligation_created → under_review → approved → execution_pending → executed → reconciled with exceptions (verification_rejected, disputed, reversed, execution_failed, reissued, cancelled_by_authority). One verified redemption produces exactly one obligation set under the versioned approved formula; corrections use compensating/reversal entries and financial history is never overwritten.
why: The money a distributor earned is tracked with the same rigor as a bank ledger — earned once, paid once, never erased.
doneWhen: Tests confirm the full lifecycle, single-obligation-set rule, and that corrections append rather than overwrite.

source.section: Required state machines — Redemption and participant value

title: Resolve high-entropy public sticker codes without exposing internal IDs
what: Build public code resolution so sticker URLs use high-entropy codes and never leak internal sequential identifiers.
why: No one can enumerate stickers, guess codes, or map the inventory from the outside.
doneWhen: Code-utility tests pass and no public response contains a sequential internal ID.

title: Explain the giver-first model before requesting any profile data
what: Build the recipient flow so the explanation of who gave the sticker and why comes before any optional profile or consent request.
why: The story of generosity leads; data collection never does.
doneWhen: The recipient flow shows the giver-first explanation screen before any data request in E2E tests and design review.

title: Display full offer details before claim confirmation
what: Show offer value, business, location, expiry, restrictions, accessibility info, and redemption instructions before the recipient confirms a claim.
why: Nobody claims an offer they can't actually use — informed choice is dignity.
doneWhen: E2E test verifies all seven detail fields render before the claim confirmation action.

title: Build a low-bandwidth recipient route
what: Provide a recipient route with compressed media and no required animation for constrained devices and connections.
why: A sticker works for someone on an old phone with one bar of signal, not just for people with flagships.
doneWhen: The low-bandwidth route completes scan-to-claim on a throttled low-end device profile within targets.

title: Build granular consent with skip, withdrawal, export, and deletion
what: Implement per-purpose consent for personalization and marketing, allowing skip at any point, later withdrawal, data export, and deletion where applicable.
why: Recipients own their data and their choices, always and reversibly.
doneWhen: Consent-logic unit tests pass and each of skip, withdraw, export, delete works end-to-end.

title: Never expose distributor identity or expected participant value to recipients
what: Enforce at the API and query level that recipient-facing responses contain no distributor identity and no expected-value figures.
why: The person who handed over the sticker is protected from being identified, judged, or targeted.
doneWhen: Automated tests assert recipient responses never contain distributor identity or value fields.

title: Provide approved offer templates and a custom-offer submission path
what: Build a library of approved offer templates plus a submission path for custom offers requiring approval.
why: Businesses can join quickly with safe defaults while unusual offers still get human review.
doneWhen: A business can create an offer from a template and submit a custom offer that lands in the approval queue.

title: Require authenticated staff or controlled short-lived location sessions for redemption verification
what: Build redemption verification so only authenticated location staff or a controlled short-lived location session can verify, with no shared plaintext/PIN-equivalent secret stored or exposed in sponsor-readable offer data.
why: A redemption is only ever verified by someone accountable, and no leaked PIN can fake one.
doneWhen: Verification fails without an authenticated staff account or valid location session, and no plaintext shared secret exists in sponsor-readable data.

title: Accept an idempotency key on every verification attempt
what: Require and honor an idempotency key for all redemption verification requests so retries return the original result.
why: A flaky connection at the counter never double-charges a sponsor or double-credits a distributor.
doneWhen: Replay tests with the same key return the original result without creating duplicate records.

title: Record full verification evidence with correction lineage
what: Record location, staff account, claim, timestamp, result, and correction lineage for every verification attempt.
why: Every verified moment of value has a complete, correctable, never-erased paper trail.
doneWhen: Every verification row contains all six fields and corrections link to their source record in tests.

title: Build the offline verification exception procedure
what: Provide an offline exception procedure where offline verification events remain pending until authenticated synchronization and review.
why: A dead Wi-Fi router doesn't turn a recipient away, and it doesn't create unreviewed money either.
doneWhen: An offline-recorded event stays pending, then resolves only after authenticated sync and reviewer action in tests.

title: Record distributor approval, training, consent, accommodations, and support preference
what: Build distributor records covering approval, training completion, consent version, accommodations, status, assigned partner, and support preference.
why: The platform remembers how each distributor needs to be supported, not just what they sold.
doneWhen: All seven distributor record fields persist, update, and appear in partner workflows in tests.

title: Build counted custody events for pack transfers
what: Transfer sticker packs through counted custody events requiring sender and receiver acknowledgement.
why: Inventory moves hand-to-hand with both people vouching for the count, so no one gets blamed for stickers they never received.
doneWhen: A pack transfer requires both acknowledgements and produces an immutable custody event in tests.

title: Show distributors plain-language activity and value counts
what: Display plain-language counts for assigned/passed/claimed/redeemed activity and approved/executed value to each distributor.
why: Every distributor can understand exactly where they stand without decoding jargon.
doneWhen: The distributor view renders all six counts in plain language and passes readability review.

title: Provide a human support channel and partner-assisted statements
what: Provide distributors a human support channel and a partner-assisted statement path when no reliable device exists.
why: Nobody is left talking to a form when they need a person.
doneWhen: The support channel is reachable from distributor views and a partner-assisted statement can be produced in rehearsal.

title: Segregate safety/incident details from marketing and business reporting
what: Keep safety and incident details in access-restricted records that never flow into marketing or business reporting.
why: A person's hardest moments never become someone's dashboard metric.
doneWhen: Access tests confirm incident data is invisible to marketing/business report queries and roles.

title: Version every formula, cap, fee rule, sponsor restriction, and effective date
what: Build versioned records for participant-value formulas, caps, fee/overhead rules, sponsor restrictions, and effective dates.
why: When rules change, past obligations stay computed under the rules that were in force — no retroactive surprises.
doneWhen: Tests show obligations reference a specific formula version and historical calculations are reproducible.

title: Create obligations transactionally with verified redemptions
what: Create obligations in the same database transaction as the verified redemption, or through a transactionally safe outbox worker with idempotent replay.
why: Value owed to a distributor can never silently fail to be recorded.
doneWhen: Failure-injection tests show no verified redemption ever exists without its obligation set, and outbox replays are idempotent.

title: Require two distinct authorized actors for material approval and execution
what: Enforce that material financial approval and execution evidence require two distinct authorized actors.
why: No single person — however trusted — can move participant money alone.
doneWhen: Tests confirm a single actor cannot both approve and execute a material batch.

title: Build the reconciliation workflow
what: Reconcile obligations, approved batches, provider/manual execution evidence, failures, reversals, and bank/accounting totals.
why: The platform's ledger and the bank's ledger always agree, and every discrepancy has a name.
doneWhen: A full reconciliation run matches all six record classes against bank/accounting totals with discrepancies flagged.

title: Generate participant statements and aggregate sponsor/impact reports
what: Generate participant statements and aggregate sponsor/impact reports that disclose no protected information.
why: Distributors get proof of what they earned; sponsors see impact; nobody's privacy is spent to do it.
doneWhen: Generated statements and reports pass a review confirming completeness and zero protected-information disclosure.

title: Build operator queues for all pending work types
what: Provide operator queues for pending offers, print batches, assignments, redemption exceptions, fraud flags, disputes, participant-value reviews, failed executions, incidents, consent/deletion requests, and claims approval.
why: Nothing that needs a human decision can silently pile up unseen.
doneWhen: All eleven queue types exist, populate from real state, and support operator action in tests.

title: Require a reason and append-only audit entry for every administrative action
what: Enforce that every administrative action captures a reason and lands in an append-only audit log.
why: Power in the system always leaves an honest, permanent trace.
doneWhen: Tests show admin actions without a reason are rejected and audit entries cannot be modified or deleted.

title: Decide whether to ever add safe impersonation
what: Beta ships with no impersonation; after the pilot, decide whether a safe impersonation capability is justified and, if so, under what controls.
why: Protects participants from anyone acting as them until the need and safeguards are proven.
doneWhen: A documented decision (approve with controls, or keep prohibition) is recorded with reasoning.

title: Revise the fourteen-table draft schema after professional determinations
what: Retain the useful concepts of the existing fourteen-table draft while revising names and relationships once counsel/CPA determinations land.
why: The data model speaks the approved public language, not prototype shorthand.
doneWhen: A revised schema using approved terminology is committed and mapped against the draft.

title: Add organization, membership, role, location staff, and account lifecycle tables
what: Create the tables that model organizations, memberships, roles, location staff, and account lifecycle states.
why: Access control and accountability start with knowing exactly who belongs to what.
doneWhen: Migrations for all five table groups apply cleanly with RLS and lifecycle tests passing.

title: Add partner organization and partner–distributor relationship tables
what: Create tables modeling partner organizations and their relationships to distributors.
why: Every distributor's support network is a first-class record, not an afterthought.
doneWhen: Partner and relationship migrations apply and partner-scoped queries pass RLS tests.

title: Add consent/version and data-subject request tables
what: Create consent records with versioning and data-subject request records.
why: Every yes, no, and 'delete my data' is recorded and honorable.
doneWhen: Consent versions and DSR records persist correctly and drive the consent/deletion queues in tests.

title: Add inventory custody event and batch print/QA evidence tables
what: Create tables for inventory custody events and batch print/QA evidence.
why: Physical sticker history — who held what, and proof it printed correctly — lives in the database, not in memory.
doneWhen: Custody and print/QA evidence migrations apply and custody events are append-only in tests.

title: Add idempotency key, state-transition log, outbox/job, and immutable audit event tables
what: Create the reliability backbone tables: idempotency keys, state-transition log, outbox/job records, and immutable audit events.
why: Retries, crashes, and reviews all resolve safely because the system remembers everything it did.
doneWhen: All four table groups exist with tests proving idempotent replay and audit immutability.

title: Add formula, obligation, approval, execution, reconciliation, and correction tables
what: Create formula/version, obligation, obligation line, approval, execution, reconciliation, and correction records using the approved public terminology.
why: Participant value has a complete financial paper trail from formula to reconciled payment.
doneWhen: All financial record migrations apply and obligation lines balance to formula totals in tests.

title: Add support case, incident, appeal, and restricted-note tables with separate access policies
what: Create support cases, incidents, appeals, and restricted notes with access policies distinct from operational data.
why: Sensitive human situations are stored behind their own locked door.
doneWhen: Migrations apply and RLS tests confirm restricted notes are invisible outside their access policy.

title: Add the claims/evidence register for public reports
what: Create a claims/evidence register backing every public claim with recorded evidence.
why: Everything Worth Keeping says publicly can be traced to proof.
doneWhen: The register exists and public report generation reads from it in tests.

title: Enforce money, timestamp, and identifier conventions
what: Store financial amounts as integer cents plus ISO currency (never floating point), timestamps in UTC rendered in user locale, UUIDs on transactional rows, created/updated metadata on mutable rows, and immutable event IDs on append-only rows.
why: Precision conventions prevent the rounding errors and timezone bugs that quietly cheat people.
doneWhen: Schema lint/tests confirm no float money columns, UTC timestamps, and required ID/metadata columns everywhere.

title: Enforce and test the ten database invariants
what: Implement the ten invariants: one active sticker per code, one active claim per unit, one effective verified redemption per claim, exactly one obligation set per verified redemption per formula version, idempotent replays, atomic non-negative caps, balanced obligation lines, executed ≤ approved value, corrections referencing sources, and default-deny cross-organization access — all tested in PostgreSQL.
why: These ten rules are the mathematical guarantee that nobody can be double-paid, shorted, or spied on.
doneWhen: PostgreSQL tests exist and pass for each of the ten invariants, including violation attempts.

title: Validate auth, authorization, schema, state, and idempotency in server-side handlers
what: Ensure all route handlers/server actions validate authentication, authorization, schema, state, and idempotency server-side, never trusting the browser.
why: No clever client can talk the platform into a transition it hasn't earned.
doneWhen: Integration tests confirm all five validation layers reject invalid requests on every mutating endpoint.

title: Keep service-role and database-owner credentials out of the browser
what: Ensure privileged credentials exist only in server environments and never ship in client bundles or responses.
why: The master keys to participant data never leave the vault.
doneWhen: Automated checks confirm no service-role key appears in any client bundle or network response.

title: Rate-limit and abuse-monitor public endpoints with non-enumerable errors
what: Apply rate limiting, abuse monitoring, and non-enumerable error responses to all public endpoints; error responses expose a correlation ID rather than stack traces or protected details.
why: Bad actors get slow, uninformative walls; real people get help via a correlation ID.
doneWhen: Tests confirm rate limits fire, errors carry correlation IDs, and valid/invalid codes are indistinguishable by response shape.

title: Apply webhook security requirements if webhooks are ever added
what: Decision checkpoint: webhooks are not in the beta; if added later, they must implement signature verification, timestamp tolerance, deduplication, replay tooling, and dead-letter handling before launch.
why: Future integrations don't get to skip the safeguards the core platform lives by.
doneWhen: A recorded decision either keeps webhooks out of scope or approves them with all five safeguards implemented.

title: Set up the organization-owned password manager with MFA for privileged accounts
what: Stand up an organization-owned password manager and enroll all privileged accounts with MFA.
why: The team's keys live in one guarded, recoverable place from day one — not in anyone's notes app.
doneWhen: All privileged credentials are in the org vault, MFA is enforced, and no secret lives in source or chat.

title: Separate local, preview, staging, and production environments and secrets
what: Establish local (synthetic fixtures), preview (synthetic/seeded), staging (synthetic plus consented test accounts), and production (minimum necessary real data) environments with distinct secrets in platform stores; no production database copy may be used in local/preview.
why: Real participants' data never leaks into a developer's laptop or a demo.
doneWhen: All four environments exist with separate secrets and a documented rule blocking production data outside production.

title: Write positive and denial RLS tests for every role/table combination
what: Implement least-privilege row-level security with an explicit positive test and denial test for every role/table pair.
why: Access rules are proven, not assumed, for every person and every record.
doneWhen: The RLS test suite covers every role/table combination with both allow and deny assertions, all passing.

title: Establish the GitHub repository security baseline
what: Create the private GitHub organization repository with protected main, pull-request workflow, required checks, dependency pinning, automated vulnerability/dependency updates, and secret scanning.
why: Every future line of code lands through a gate that catches mistakes before they reach people.
doneWhen: The org repo exists with branch protection, required checks, dependency automation, and secret scanning verified active.

title: Implement web security controls: CSP, secure cookies, CSRF, encoding, upload restrictions, rate limiting
what: Apply Content Security Policy, secure cookies, CSRF protection where applicable, input/output encoding, upload restrictions, and rate limiting across the application.
why: Standard web attacks bounce off before they ever touch a participant.
doneWhen: Security scan and tests confirm each control is active on the deployed staging application.

title: Enforce anonymous-first data minimization
what: Do not collect name, exact age, hobbies, precise location, or device fingerprint unless a documented purpose, consent/legal basis, retention rule, and access rule exists for each field.
why: The platform's default posture is to know as little about vulnerable people as possible.
doneWhen: A data inventory shows every collected sensitive field has documented purpose, basis, retention, and access rules — and undocumented fields are not collected.

title: Guarantee no sale of personal data and non-reidentifiable aggregates
what: Enforce that personal data is never sold and that aggregate campaign/business measurement cannot allow participant re-identification.
why: The promise 'we will never sell you' is engineered, not just stated.
doneWhen: Aggregate report outputs pass a re-identification review and the no-sale commitment is documented in policy and contracts.

title: Write the incident response process
what: Document incident classification, notification, containment, evidence preservation, recovery, and postmortem procedures.
why: When something goes wrong, the team protects people fast instead of improvising.
doneWhen: The written process covers all six stages and has been walked through in a tabletop exercise.

title: Build all beta-critical workflows to WCAG 2.2 AA
what: Implement keyboard navigation, visible focus, semantic landmarks, labels, error summaries, 200% zoom/reflow, sufficient contrast, reduced motion, captions/transcripts, and non-color status cues across beta-critical workflows.
why: The people this project serves are exactly the people accessibility failures exclude.
doneWhen: Automated accessibility checks pass and each listed technique is verified present in beta-critical workflows.

title: Write plain-language content and assess Austin language needs with the operating partner
what: Ship plain-language English at beta, assess Austin-language needs with the operating partner before launch, and provide partner-assisted explanation plus printable versions of critical participant materials.
why: Understanding the deal you're agreeing to should never require fluent bureaucratic English or a smartphone.
doneWhen: Plain-language review passes, the partner language assessment is documented, and printable critical materials exist.

title: Support the required browser matrix at beta launch
what: Support current and previous major versions of Safari iOS, Chrome Android, Chrome desktop, Safari desktop, Edge, and Firefox.
why: A recipient's browser choice never decides whether their sticker works.
doneWhen: Cross-browser test runs pass on all twelve browser/version combinations for beta-critical flows.

title: Establish the 99.5% monthly availability objective with checks
what: Set and monitor a 99.5% monthly availability objective for beta-critical web/API routes, excluding announced maintenance.
why: A sticker scanned at 2am on a Sunday still works.
doneWhen: Availability checks run against beta-critical routes and report monthly uptime against the 99.5% target.

title: Meet transaction and scan-page performance targets
what: Achieve p95 below 1 second for transaction APIs under expected pilot load and usable public scan-page content below 3 seconds on a representative low-end mobile/4G profile.
why: Someone standing on a sidewalk with a stranger's gift shouldn't watch a spinner.
doneWhen: Load tests show p95 < 1s on transaction APIs and the scan page hits < 3s on the low-end 4G profile.

title: Configure recovery point and recovery time objectives
what: Configure backups and recovery to meet RPO of 24 hours for general content and 15 minutes for transactional/financial records where the platform supports it, and RTO of 4 hours for beta-critical service.
why: Even in a disaster, at most minutes of anyone's earned value can be lost.
doneWhen: Backup/replication configuration is documented as meeting the stated RPO/RTO values on the chosen platform.

title: Run daily backups, pre-migration backups, and quarterly restore tests
what: Enable daily automated backups plus a backup before every migration, and run a restore test quarterly during beta preparation and before major schema changes.
why: A backup that has never been restored is a hope, not a safeguard.
doneWhen: Daily backups are verified running, pre-migration backup is in the release process, and a successful restore test is logged.

title: Set up structured logs, correlation IDs, and alerting
what: Implement structured logs, correlation IDs, error monitoring, availability checks, database health monitoring, job/queue failure alerts, and cost alerts.
why: Problems announce themselves to the team before participants have to.
doneWhen: Each of the seven observability capabilities is live and a test alert reaches the on-call channel.

title: Write unit tests for formulas, permissions, transitions, code utilities, and consent
what: Build unit test coverage for participant-value formulas, permissions, state transitions, token/code utilities, and consent logic.
why: The rules that protect money and consent are checked on every commit.
doneWhen: Unit suites for all five areas exist and pass in CI.

title: Write PostgreSQL tests for RLS, invariants, caps, idempotency, obligations, and reversals
what: Build database-level tests for RLS, invariants, atomic cap use, idempotency, ledger/obligation creation, reversals, and forbidden mutation.
why: The database itself proves it will refuse to lie, even if application code is wrong.
doneWhen: The PostgreSQL suite covers all seven areas and passes in CI.

title: Write API integration tests for every success and failure path
what: Cover every success, replay, authorization denial, invalid transition, dispute, and failure path with API integration tests.
why: The unhappy paths — where people get hurt — are tested as hard as the happy ones.
doneWhen: Integration tests cover all six path categories per endpoint and pass in CI.

title: Write end-to-end tests for all beta-critical workflows
what: Build E2E tests for recipient scan/claim, staff redemption, partner assignment, finance review, correction, reporting, and the no-smartphone support workflow.
why: The whole journey — including for someone without a phone — is proven working before anyone real depends on it.
doneWhen: E2E tests for all seven workflows pass in CI against a deployed preview.

title: Run the physical QR test matrix
what: Test printed QR codes across print proofs, glare, distance, damage, older phones, and low light, and log results.
why: A sticker that fails to scan on an old phone in bad light fails the exact person it was meant for.
doneWhen: The test matrix is completed across all six conditions with results logged and pass/fail recorded per proof.

title: Run accessibility automated checks plus keyboard and screen-reader manual review
what: Execute automated accessibility checks and a manual keyboard and screen-reader review of beta-critical workflows.
why: Real assistive-technology use catches what scanners can't.
doneWhen: Automated checks pass and the manual keyboard/screen-reader review is completed with findings resolved or accepted.

title: Complete a security review and tabletop incident/recovery exercise
what: Run a security review and a tabletop incident and recovery exercise before any real participant launch.
why: The team practices the worst day before real people are on the platform.
doneWhen: The security review report exists with findings dispositioned and the tabletop exercise is completed and documented.

title: Write operator runbooks for beta operations
what: Produce the operator runbooks required by the technical release gate.
why: Anyone on shift can handle the platform correctly without tribal knowledge.
doneWhen: Runbooks for beta-critical operations exist and a second operator can execute them in rehearsal.

title: Assign on-call ownership for the beta
what: Establish named on-call ownership for beta-critical service before production deployment.
why: When something breaks at night, a specific person is responsible for the people affected.
doneWhen: An on-call schedule with named owners and escalation is published and acknowledged.

title: Prepare migration rollback procedures
what: Document and verify a rollback path for database migrations as required by the release gate.
why: A bad migration can be undone without losing anyone's records.
doneWhen: A tested rollback procedure exists for the current migration set.

title: Pass the technical release gate with signed operational readiness
what: Verify all critical tests green, zero unresolved critical/high security findings, verified backups and restore, privacy/accessibility review complete, runbooks and on-call in place, migration rollback ready, and obtain signed operational readiness from product, engineering, partner/safety, finance, and privacy/security owners.
why: Five named humans stake their signature on the platform being safe before it touches real participants.
doneWhen: Every gate criterion is evidenced and all five owner signatures are recorded.

title: Consolidate the beta into one organization-owned Next.js application
what: Build the beta as a single Next.js application backed by Supabase/PostgreSQL, deployed through GitHub to Vercel under organization ownership.
why: One accountable codebase means one place where every promise to participants is kept.
doneWhen: The unified application exists in the org repo and deploys through GitHub to Vercel.

title: Decide the story site's fate: campaign content or migrate approved sections
what: Either preserve the story site as campaign content or migrate its approved sections into the unified application.
why: The story that earned early belief stays in service of the campaign instead of rotting in a separate repo.
doneWhen: A documented decision is made and executed: the story site is either serving campaign content or its approved sections are migrated.

title: Configure TypeScript strict mode
what: Set up TypeScript with strict mode across the application.
why: Contract drift is caught by the compiler before it becomes a bug that affects someone's value.
doneWhen: tsconfig has strict mode enabled and the build passes with no strict-mode suppressions.

title: Pin Next.js 16 App Router and React 19 to exact tested versions
what: Adopt Next.js 16 App Router with React 19 and pin exact tested versions in the lockfile/manifest.
why: The platform never breaks because a dependency updated itself overnight.
doneWhen: Lockfile and manifest show exact pinned versions matching the verified baseline.

title: Set up styling with shared design tokens
what: Use existing CSS/Tailwind 4 only where it reduces repetition, with shared design tokens and no second component system.
why: One coherent visual language keeps the brand warm and consistent everywhere a participant looks.
doneWhen: Shared design tokens exist and are consumed by application styles with no duplicate component system.

title: Add Zod validation at all server and public input boundaries
what: Apply Zod schemas to validate every server and public input boundary at runtime.
why: Malformed or malicious input is stopped at the door with typed, testable rules.
doneWhen: Every server/public input boundary has a Zod schema and invalid-input tests pass.

title: Set up managed Supabase PostgreSQL with SQL migrations and nightly logical export
what: Provision managed Supabase PostgreSQL, manage schema through SQL migrations, and schedule a nightly logical export to standard PostgreSQL as the exit path.
why: The single source of truth is managed, versioned, and always portable if Supabase ever fails us.
doneWhen: The Supabase project runs migrations from the repo and a nightly logical export lands and is verified restorable.

title: Set up Supabase Auth with MFA for privileged roles and passwordless options
what: Configure Supabase Auth integrated with RLS, MFA for privileged roles, and passwordless links/OTP where appropriate.
why: Login is easy for participants and hardened for the people holding power.
doneWhen: Auth works end-to-end with RLS, privileged MFA enforced, and passwordless flow tested.

title: Set up Supabase Storage for evidence uploads with scheduled bucket export
what: Use Supabase Storage for approved evidence/artifact uploads with access policies near application data, public assets in app/CDN, and a scheduled bucket export.
why: Evidence files inherit the same access discipline as the data they support.
doneWhen: Evidence uploads work under access policies and the scheduled bucket export runs successfully.

title: Establish the server execution model: route handlers plus bounded background jobs
what: Use Next.js route handlers/server actions for synchronous workflows and Supabase scheduled/edge jobs only for bounded background work, with a documented path to a standard Node worker if volume grows.
why: Work happens where it's authorized and observable, not scattered across ad-hoc scripts.
doneWhen: The execution model is documented, implemented, and the worker exit path is written down.

title: Set up Vercel with GitHub preview, staging, and production environments
what: Configure Vercel projects/environments for GitHub preview deployments, staging, and production, and document the container/Node hosting fallback path.
why: Every change is seen and rehearsed before it reaches the environment real people use.
doneWhen: Preview, staging, and production deployments all work from GitHub and the fallback hosting path is documented.

title: Set up Vitest, React Testing Library, Playwright, and the pgTAP/SQL harness
what: Install and configure Vitest + React Testing Library for unit/UI tests, Playwright for E2E, and pgTAP/SQL harness for database tests, runnable locally and in CI.
why: The safety net exists before the code that needs catching.
doneWhen: All three test harnesses run a passing smoke test locally and in CI.

title: Build the GitHub Actions CI pipeline gating Vercel deploys
what: Set up GitHub Actions running lint, typecheck, unit, SQL, build, and E2E smoke, with Vercel deployment only after checks pass.
why: Nothing reaches participants without passing every automated gate, with an auditable history.
doneWhen: CI runs all six check stages on every PR and deploys are blocked on failure.

title: Set up Vercel platform monitoring plus Sentry with data scrubbing
what: Enable Vercel logs/analytics for platform health and Sentry for errors/traces with data scrubbing, keeping structured logs exportable and Sentry removable without product outage.
why: The team can diagnose production pain without error tools hoovering up participant data.
doneWhen: Sentry captures a test error with scrubbing verified and Vercel analytics report platform health.

title: Build first-party, purpose-limited product analytics
what: Implement first-party event tables and aggregate reports with CSV/SQL aggregate export, instead of third-party behavioral analytics.
why: The project learns what it needs without exporting sensitive behavior to anyone else's ad machine.
doneWhen: Purpose-limited event tables populate, aggregate reports generate, and no third-party behavioral tracker is present.

title: Set up transactional email after account/processor approval
what: Configure Resend or an equivalent transactional-only email provider behind a provider adapter with exportable templates, only after account/processor approval.
why: Auth links, business notices, and support messages actually arrive — with an exit if the provider changes.
doneWhen: Transactional emails send from the approved account through the adapter and templates are exportable.

title: Set up the shared support inbox for the campaign
what: Stand up a shared support inbox to handle campaign-period inquiries.
why: Everyone who reaches out during the raise gets a human answer from a shared, accountable place.
doneWhen: The shared inbox exists, is monitored, and campaign materials point to it.

title: Build the role-based case queue for participant and transaction matters
what: Implement a role-based case queue inside the platform for participant/transaction matters with CSV/PDF case export under access controls, so sensitive cases never live only in generic SaaS tickets.
why: A distributor's dispute or a recipient's problem is handled inside the system that protects them.
doneWhen: Sensitive cases route to the in-platform queue, are access-controlled, and export works for authorized roles.

title: Select the accounting system with a qualified adviser
what: Have a qualified adviser select the small-business ledger/accounting system, with a CSV journal/export contract, importing approved aggregate transactions rather than raw profiles.
why: The books are professional-grade from the start and never ingest anyone's personal data.
doneWhen: An adviser-selected accounting system is live with the aggregate-only import rule and export contract documented.

title: Investigate Wefunder Community Round and run Investor Warmup before a live offering
what: Investigate the Wefunder Community Round as the crowdfunding vehicle and use Investor Warmup before any live offering; a reward campaign or private financing requires revised approval.
why: The community that believes in this work gets a real, compliant way to own a piece of it.
doneWhen: The Wefunder investigation is documented and Investor Warmup is live before any offering goes public.

title: Gate any Oracle campaign demo behind disclosure, rate limits, and a kill switch
what: If the Oracle AI is used as a campaign demo, deploy it only behind clear disclosure, rate limits, and a kill switch, with static curated responses as fallback; AI stays out of beta-critical workflows.
why: A charming demo never gets to become an unvetted system touching real participants.
doneWhen: Either the Oracle demo runs with all three controls verified, or a decision to use static content is recorded.

title: Record manual approved value execution in the platform
what: Keep financial execution human-reviewed in beta: record manual approved executions in a provider-neutral execution record with CSV batch support; payment-provider integration waits for legal/operating determination.
why: Real money moves only through reviewed human hands until the law and the model say otherwise.
doneWhen: A manual execution can be recorded against an approved batch with provider-neutral fields and CSV batch export.

title: Revisit deferred AI, POS, and payout-provider integrations
what: Decision checkpoint: production AI, POS integrations, and Stripe Connect/payout providers are deferred until participant-value classification, identity, and custody are approved — decide after the pilot whether to proceed.
why: Complexity and custody risk are only taken on once the pilot proves they're worth it.
doneWhen: A documented post-pilot decision exists for each deferred integration with reasoning.

title: Revisit deferred native mobile apps and custom Express API
what: Decision checkpoint: native mobile apps and a custom Express API are deferred because responsive web with partner-assisted access covers the approved beta and Next.js server routes are sufficient at pilot scale — decide after the pilot whether either is now justified.
why: Participants get the simplest platform that serves them until real evidence says more is needed.
doneWhen: A documented post-pilot decision exists for native mobile apps and for a custom API layer, each with reasoning.

title: Replace every `latest` dependency with an exact verified version
what: Pin every dependency currently on `latest` to an exact version matching the verified lockfile baseline.
why: The platform's behavior is reproducible, not at the mercy of upstream publish schedules.
doneWhen: No manifest contains `latest` and installed versions match the verified baseline.

title: Standardize on Node.js 22 LTS for development and CI
what: Use Node.js 22 LTS everywhere until a tested migration to a newer version is approved.
why: Everyone builds and tests on the same foundation the production platform runs on.
doneWhen: Local tooling, CI, and deployment all declare and use Node 22 LTS.

title: Commit one lockfile per application and protect it from casual regeneration
what: Maintain exactly one committed lockfile per application and establish the rule that it is not regenerated casually.
why: Dependency truth is versioned and deliberate, never accidental.
doneWhen: One lockfile per application is committed and the regeneration rule is documented in contributor docs.

title: Establish the security-patch and scheduled-upgrade process
what: Apply security patches through pull requests with automated tests, and schedule framework/minor upgrades rather than allowing implicit drift.
why: Staying secure and current is a routine, not an emergency.
doneWhen: The patch/upgrade process is documented and the first patch cycle has run through a tested PR.

title: Record production database extensions and CLI versions in repository docs
what: Document the production database extensions and CLI tool versions in the repository.
why: Anyone rebuilding or debugging the environment knows exactly what it runs on.
doneWhen: Repository documentation lists all production extensions and CLI versions in use.

title: Document and enforce the six core architecture decisions
what: Record as ADRs: PostgreSQL as the one transactional source of truth, anonymous-first recipient flow, server-authorized writes, append-only accountability, provider adapters for external services, and no premature distributed system (no microservices, Kafka, blockchain, or multiple databases at pilot scale).
why: The principles that keep the system honest survive team changes because they're written down.
doneWhen: All six decisions exist as committed ADRs referenced from the repository README.

title: Verify free-tier terms and budget production tiers before launch
what: Begin on free/developer tiers only where terms permit real beta use, and budget production tiers before campaign/beta launch.
why: The pilot never runs on a tier whose terms could shut it off mid-service.
doneWhen: Each service's tier terms are verified for beta use and production-tier costs are in the budget.

title: Assign ownership, ceilings, and exit routes for every paid service
what: For every paid service, assign an account owner, billing owner, backup admin, monthly ceiling, alert threshold, export route, and cancellation procedure; no campaign or beta launches on personal-only accounts without a backup administrator.
why: No single person's inbox, card, or vacation can take the platform down.
doneWhen: A service register lists all seven fields for every paid service with no personal-only accounts lacking backup admins.

title: Reassess service tiers at 50%, 75%, and 90% of included usage
what: Monitor paid-service usage during live operations and reassess tiers when any service hits 50%, 75%, and 90% of its included usage.
why: The pilot never gets throttled or surprise-billed because nobody was watching the meter.
doneWhen: Usage alerts exist at all three thresholds and each triggered alert produces a recorded tier decision.

title: Choose and lock the final product/company name
what: Run the name shortlist through domain, trademark, and company-registry searches and record the final approval.
why: Every legal, banking, and campaign step downstream depends on a name the team can actually own.
doneWhen: A written name decision exists and the matching domain is owned.

title: Convert the logo/wordmark into vector masters and small-size variants
what: Rework the current StickerPass logo direction into a vector master with small-size marks and export set.
why: A crisp, consistent mark is how supporters recognize Worth Keeping on a sticker, a page, and a pack.
doneWhen: SVG/PDF/PNG exports exist with recorded usage approval.

title: Write the brand guide
what: Document colors, typography, spacing, voice, dignity rules, sponsor attribution, co-branding, and prohibited use.
why: The dignity rules in the brand guide are what keep every future asset respectful of the people it serves.
doneWhen: An accessible PDF/web brand guide is published and approved.

title: Encode design tokens and component styles in the application
what: Translate the approved palette and typography into application design tokens and component styles.
why: Tokens make accessibility and brand consistency automatic instead of a per-screen fight.
doneWhen: Visual regression and contrast checks pass on the tokenized components.

title: Create the illustration/AI disclosure policy
what: State ownership, source, allowed use, editing rules, disclosure, and required human approval for illustration and AI-assisted art.
why: Honesty about how the art is made protects the trust the whole project runs on.
doneWhen: A rights register and approval checklist exist and are in use.

title: Finalize the pilot sticker artwork collection
what: Select approved designs and remove decorative/uncontrolled QR codes and unsupported words or numbers.
why: Recipients deserve art that is beautiful and codes that are safe — nothing on the sticker can mislead.
doneWhen: Print-ready vector masters exist for the approved pilot collection.

title: Design the front/back code layout
what: Establish the scan code placement, distributor/unit relationship, quiet zone, human-readable fallback, and test labels.
why: A code that scans reliably on a worn sticker is the difference between help delivered and help denied.
doneWhen: The device/print scan matrix passes for the layout.

title: Write the printer specification
what: Specify size, stock, adhesive, finish, durability, color, cut path, bleed, code resolution, and packaging.
why: A precise spec is how a small team gets professional stickers without expensive surprises.
doneWhen: The print partner has approved the production spec in writing.

title: Build the secure print-data export
what: Define batch ID, unique codes, checksum, status, printer handoff, and destruction/retention instructions for code data.
why: Sticker codes are the currency of the system — leaking them would let strangers claim what belongs to recipients.
doneWhen: A controlled sample export exists with an audit record of the handoff and destruction terms.

title: Run digital and press proofs with physical durability tests
what: Order digital proof, press proof, and finish/durability/adhesion test samples from the print partner.
why: A sticker that peels or fades in the field breaks a promise made to the person carrying it.
doneWhen: Signed proof acceptance is on file.

title: Design the pack and label
what: Set pack quantity, batch/pack ID, instructions, safety/support info, and approved language such as "not for resale".
why: The pack is a distributor's first instruction manual — clear packaging protects them from mistakes.
doneWhen: A packed pilot sample exists and passes review.

title: Create sticker custody forms
what: Build forms for receive, count, transfer, assign, return, loss, damage, void, and recall of stickers.
why: Custody paperwork keeps every sticker accounted for, protecting distributors from blame and recipients from loss.
doneWhen: A count reconciliation has been rehearsed using the forms.

title: Define campaign reward SKUs
what: Finalize perk contents, quantities, unit/landed cost, packaging, replacement policy, and delivery method.
why: Backers who fund Worth Keeping deserve perks that arrive as promised without eating the raise.
doneWhen: BOM, vendor quotes, and a fulfillment test are complete.

title: Build public scan and claim screens
what: Implement public scan, invalid/void/already-claimed/rate-limited, reveal, claim, expiry, redemption, correction, and help screens.
why: The scan moment is the recipient's whole experience — every state must land with clarity and dignity.
doneWhen: All listed screen states render and pass UX and accessibility review.

title: Build the optional preference profile with consent controls
what: Implement purpose-specific consent, skip, edit, withdraw, export, and delete paths for the optional profile.
why: Recipients keep full control of their information — participation never requires surrendering privacy.
doneWhen: Every consent, skip, edit, withdraw, export, and delete path works end to end.

title: Build distributor onboarding and support screens
what: Implement distributor onboarding, training, consent, inventory, activity/value statement, support, complaint, appeal, and exit screens plus printable equivalents.
why: Distributors carry the program in the field — they need tools that respect their time and their exit rights.
doneWhen: All distributor screens and printable equivalents exist and pass review.

title: Build the partner dashboard and workflows
what: Implement the partner dashboard, participant/pack workflows, restricted incident/support case views, and operating reports.
why: Community partners can only safeguard participants if they can see their own operation clearly — and nothing more.
doneWhen: Partner dashboard, workflows, restricted views, and reports pass access-control review.

title: Build business onboarding and redemption workflows
what: Implement business onboarding, approved offer templates, custom offer review, location/staff access, redemption, correction/dispute, funding/status, and reports.
why: Local businesses join because it is easy and safe to say yes — the workflow has to earn that.
doneWhen: All business workflows function end to end and pass review.

title: Build operator and back-office dashboards
what: Implement operator, finance, support, claims, fraud, inventory, audit, reconciliation, incident, and release dashboards.
why: Operators can only keep promises they can see — dashboards are the program's conscience.
doneWhen: All ten operational dashboards exist with correct role-restricted access.

title: Implement empty, error, and degraded UI states
what: Build empty, loading, offline, error, timeout, retry, unauthorized, suspended, and maintenance states across the product.
why: The system will fail sometimes; when it does, no one standing at a counter should be left confused.
doneWhen: Every listed state is implemented and reviewed across the core flows.

title: Adopt a plain-language glossary for program terms
what: Replace "micro-grant," "payout," "pledge," and "overhead" with plain, professionally-determined language across the product.
why: Words shape dignity — no recipient should need jargon translated to understand what they received.
doneWhen: The glossary is approved after professional determination and the old terms are removed from product copy.

title: Create the entity/cap-table/authority record and responsibility matrix
what: Document the legal entity, capitalization table, signing authority, and a responsibility matrix for the team.
why: Investors and partners deserve to know exactly who owns what and who can commit the company.
doneWhen: The entity, cap-table, authority record, and responsibility matrix are written and stored in the governance folder.

title: Draft the core agreement set
what: Prepare the community-partner agreement, business/sponsor agreement, data-processing terms, and artist/vendor agreement.
why: Written agreements protect partners, artists, and participants before the first sticker changes hands.
doneWhen: All four agreements are counsel-reviewed and ready for signature.

title: Prepare the print purchase order
what: Draft the purchase order for the print vendor covering proofs and production terms.
why: A real PO turns the print relationship into an accountable commitment on both sides.
doneWhen: A signed purchase order exists with the selected print vendor.

title: Write the participant policy set
what: Create participant eligibility, consent, privacy, training, safety, incident, media, complaint, suspension, appeal, and exit policies.
why: Participants are the most vulnerable people in the system — these policies are their protection in writing.
doneWhen: All eleven participant policies are written and approved.

title: Write the business policy set
what: Create business qualification, allowed/prohibited offer, age/restriction, verification, dispute, refund, expiry, and offboarding policies.
why: Clear rules keep business offers safe and appropriate for the people redeeming them.
doneWhen: All eight business policies are written and approved.

title: Write the financial SOP set
what: Create financial formula, custody, chart of accounts, approval, execution, reconciliation, correction, refund, chargeback, failure, tax-document, and retention SOPs.
why: Every dollar in this system is a promise to someone — the SOPs make sure none of them slip.
doneWhen: All financial SOPs are written, approved, and rehearsable.

title: Create the privacy and security governance pack
what: Produce the role/access matrix, data inventory/map, retention schedule, privacy notice, terms, cookie/analytics statement, security plan, incident response, backup/restore, vendor register, and access review.
why: People trust Worth Keeping with sensitive moments — governance is how that trust is kept, not just claimed.
doneWhen: All eleven governance documents exist, are approved, and the first access review is scheduled.

title: Write support scripts and escalation materials
what: Create support scripts, an escalation map, service windows, emergency language, and a printable contact card.
why: When someone in the field needs help, the person answering should never have to improvise.
doneWhen: Scripts, escalation map, service windows, emergency language, and printed contact card are approved and distributed.

title: Stand up the claims register and public-report approval workflow
what: Create a register of every public claim and a workflow requiring approval before any claim or report goes public.
why: Worth Keeping never says anything publicly that it cannot prove — this register enforces that.
doneWhen: The claims register exists and the approval workflow has been used for at least one public statement.

title: Write the beta research plan
what: Plan research covering distributors, recipients, partner staff, businesses, sponsors, and operators.
why: The pilot only teaches if every group's experience is actually studied, not assumed.
doneWhen: The research plan covering all six groups is approved.

title: Create voluntary research consent separate from program participation
what: Draft a research consent form that is fully separate from program participation and clearly revocable.
why: No one should feel their sticker depends on answering a researcher's questions.
doneWhen: A standalone, revocable research consent form is approved.

title: Write observation/interview guides and decide research compensation
what: Prepare observation and interview guides and document the participant compensation decision.
why: Good guides get honest answers; a fair compensation decision respects people's time.
doneWhen: Guides exist and the compensation decision is written down.

title: Build the metrics dictionary
what: Define every pilot metric with numerator, denominator, source, owner, exclusions, and privacy threshold.
why: Shared definitions stop the team from fooling itself — and privacy thresholds stop metrics from exposing individuals.
doneWhen: The metrics dictionary is complete for every pilot metric and approved.

title: Build the daily dashboard, weekly reconciliation, and safety review artifacts
what: Create the daily operations dashboard, weekly reconciliation report, and safety/incident review format for the pilot.
why: Daily visibility is how small problems in the field stay small.
doneWhen: Dashboard, reconciliation, and safety review templates exist and have been dry-run once.

title: Prepare the pilot close report and continue/revise/pause/stop memo
what: Write the pilot close report and the decision memo recommending continue, revise, pause, or stop.
why: The team owes itself and its investors an honest verdict on what the pilot proved.
doneWhen: Both documents are complete and the decision is recorded.

title: Create the public impact report template using aggregate evidence only
what: Design a public impact report format that uses only aggregate, privacy-safe evidence.
why: Impact should be shown without ever putting an individual recipient on display.
doneWhen: The template is approved through the claims register workflow.

title: Finalize campaign name, one-line promise, and honest proof-level statement
what: Lock the final campaign name, one-line promise, short description, and a statement of exactly what is proven so far.
why: Backers deserve to know precisely what exists today — the honesty is the pitch.
doneWhen: All four elements are written, claims-checked, and approved.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Assemble the Wefunder profile and Form C inputs
what: Prepare the company profile, investment terms, Form C inputs, capitalization/ownership, financial statements, risks, use of proceeds, minimum/maximum, deadline, oversubscription logic, and investor FAQ.
why: A complete, accurate filing protects every small investor who trusts the campaign.
doneWhen: The full investment package is counsel- and CPA-approved and loaded into Wefunder.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Write the plain-language security and total-loss risk explanation
what: Explain the selected security instrument in plain language, including explicit total-loss and illiquidity risk.
why: First-time investors deserve to understand they could lose everything before they invest a dollar.
doneWhen: The explanation is counsel-approved and published on the campaign page.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Prepare founder/team bios and disclosures
what: Write bios with portraits, roles, relevant experience, conflicts of interest, and approved contact details.
why: Investors are backing people — they should see exactly who, including the conflicts.
doneWhen: Bios, portraits, conflicts, and contact details are approved and published.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Create the campaign diagram set
what: Produce the product architecture diagram, three-party value diagram, physical/digital lifecycle, accountability flow, roadmap, budget chart, and risk/mitigation graphic.
why: Clear diagrams let a stranger understand the whole system in one sitting.
doneWhen: All seven diagrams are claims-checked, accessible, and approved.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Build the investor-perk table
what: Define perks with cost, quantity, cumulative logic, delivery, geography, tax/shipping, and limitations.
why: Perk math done honestly up front means no backer gets a broken promise later.
doneWhen: The perk table is costed, reviewed, and published with the campaign.

source.section: Crowdfunding assets — Page and investment package

title: Produce the founder campaign film
what: Shoot and edit the 60–90 second primary cut plus short social cuts.
why: The film carries the heart of Worth Keeping to people who will never read the documents.
doneWhen: Primary cut and social cuts are approved, claims-checked, and delivered.

title: Produce the product demo video
what: Show a controlled sample scan, reveal, staff verification, and the accountability workflow.
why: Showing the real workflow, not a mockup, is what makes the campaign's proof honest.
doneWhen: The demo video shows a real controlled scan end to end and is approved.

title: Shoot real physical sticker photography and scan footage
what: Photograph actual manufactured stickers and capture real scan footage.
why: Real stickers on camera prove the product exists beyond a render.
doneWhen: A photo and footage library of real stickers is delivered and rights-cleared.

title: Capture Austin establishing visuals within claims limits
what: Shoot Austin establishing footage that does not imply unapproved organizations or participant outcomes.
why: Place-setting footage must never borrow credibility from organizations that haven't said yes.
doneWhen: Establishing visuals pass the claims check and are approved for use.

title: Enforce media consent rules and cut a no-vulnerable-footage campaign version
what: Use participant/community footage only with voluntary, revocable, non-coercive consent, and produce a complete campaign version that needs no vulnerable-person footage.
why: No one's face funds this campaign without their free and revocable yes.
doneWhen: Consent records exist for all used footage and a complete consent-independent campaign cut is delivered.

title: Produce media accessibility and delivery variants
what: Create captions, transcript, alt text, audio description where necessary, thumbnail, poster frame, and mobile-safe crops.
why: The campaign story must reach people who watch, listen, or read — no one excluded.
doneWhen: Every campaign media asset has its full accessibility and format variant set.

title: Build the Investor Warmup page and email sequences
what: Create the Investor Warmup page, consented email capture, and welcome/education/nurture/launch sequences.
why: A warm, informed audience on launch day is what turns a raise from hope into momentum.
doneWhen: Warmup page is live, capture is consent-based, and all four sequences are written and loaded.

title: Assemble the press kit
what: Prepare the press release, one-page fact sheet, press FAQ, approved images, founder bio, media contact, and correction protocol.
why: When press comes calling, accurate materials keep the story true.
doneWhen: The complete press kit is claims-checked and approved.

title: Write creator/community/business outreach scripts and tracker
what: Draft outreach scripts for creators, community members, and businesses, with a tracker for contacts and responses.
why: Respectful, consistent outreach builds the coalition without burning goodwill.
doneWhen: Scripts are approved and the tracker is populated with the first outreach list.

title: Write the launch-day runbook and campaign communication library
what: Create the launch-day runbook, response library, moderation rules, investor update template, delay/scope-change/refund communications, and post-close onboarding.
why: Pre-written honesty for good days and bad days means the campaign never scrambles for words.
doneWhen: All six communication assets are written and approved before launch.

title: Build the Infinity Page infographic
what: Create the standalone responsive HTML infographic at deliverables/stickerpass-infinity-page/index.html summarizing the concept, loop, assets, work completed, maturity, PDR, pilot scope, architecture, roles, tools, subscriptions, risks, gates, campaign path, timeline, a done/next/gated/deferred legend, and links to every final planning document.
why: One page that shows the whole picture lets any teammate or adviser get oriented in minutes.
doneWhen: The page meets acceptance: no unsupported claims, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, WCAG 2.2 AA contrast, reduced motion, responsive 360px to large desktop, print stylesheet, no external runtime dependency, all links resolve, and rendering is verified.

title: Create the asset register enforcing the asset rule
what: Track every public or operational asset with its owner, source file, rights status, approver, version, accessibility variant, and claims-check status; treat "existing" material as usable input only, never automatically publishable.
why: The register is how the team knows every asset it shows the world is owned, rights-cleared, accessible, and true.
doneWhen: An asset register exists covering every asset in use, with all seven fields recorded per asset.

title: Set up the GitHub organization repository with controls
what: Create a private organization repository with an organization owner plus backup, protected main branch, and required checks; upgrade to Team only if controls require it.
why: The codebase is the company's core asset — it needs ownership and protection from day one.
doneWhen: Private org repo exists with two owners, protected main, and required checks enabled.

title: Set up Vercel hosting with environment separation
what: Create the organization project on a commercial team plan with billing owner, backup admin, and preview/staging/production separation.
why: Clean environment separation keeps experiments from ever touching real recipients.
doneWhen: Org project exists with billing owner, backup admin, and three separated environments.

title: Set up Supabase production and development projects
what: Create a Pro-class production project plus separate development/staging projects with organization owner, billing, MFA, a PITR/backup decision, and data region choice.
why: Recipient data deserves production-grade custody with backups decided before the first record exists.
doneWhen: Separate prod/dev/staging projects exist with MFA, owner, billing, region, and a recorded PITR/backup decision.

title: Set up the Wefunder account for Warmup and Community Round
what: Establish the issuer account with issuer owner, securities counsel, finance owner, and backup admin; stay in Warmup until entity and terms are ready, going public only after approval.
why: The raise platform must be governed as carefully as the money it will hold.
doneWhen: Wefunder account exists in Warmup with all four owner roles assigned and no public round before approval.

title: Register the domain in an organization-owned DNS account
what: Register the final domain in one organization-owned account with registrar lock, auto-renew, MFA, and a backup admin.
why: Losing the domain would break every link, email, and QR pointing to Worth Keeping.
doneWhen: Domain is registered under the org account with lock, auto-renew, MFA, and backup admin verified.

title: Set up organization email with role addresses
what: Create a business workspace with shared aliases/groups for support, legal, finance, and press; no personal-only campaign or finance mailbox.
why: Role addresses mean no critical message dies in one person's personal inbox.
doneWhen: Business workspace is live with shared role aliases and no campaign/finance mail routed only to a personal mailbox.

title: Set up the business password manager vault
what: Create a business/team vault with two owners, emergency recovery, and least-privilege sharing.
why: Shared secrets handled properly protect the whole system from one lost laptop.
doneWhen: Team vault exists with two owners, tested emergency recovery, and least-privilege access.

title: Set up Sentry with data scrubbing and alerts
what: Configure Sentry on the free tier with data scrubbing, environment separation, alerts, and named owners; move to paid only if volume/retention/support requires.
why: Error visibility without leaking personal data is how the team fixes problems responsibly.
doneWhen: Sentry is live with scrubbing verified, environments separated, alerts configured, and owners assigned.

title: Set up the transactional email provider
what: Stand up a development sandbox, then the approved paid tier by volume, with SPF/DKIM/DMARC, suppression handling, and templates behind a provider adapter.
why: Auth and operational notices must arrive reliably or people get locked out of their own claims.
doneWhen: Domain authentication passes, suppression handling works, templates exist, and the provider sits behind an adapter.

title: Set up the accounting system
what: Adopt the adviser-selected business plan with chart of accounts, bank controls, accountant access, and export capability.
why: Clean books from day one are the foundation of every financial promise the project makes.
doneWhen: Accounting system is live with chart of accounts, bank controls, accountant access, and verified exports.

title: Establish banking and payment execution after legal/tax/KYC determination
what: Select the custody and manual-disbursement setup only after the legal/tax/KYC determination, with dual control, limits, evidence, and reconciliation.
why: Money that will reach vulnerable people must move under dual control with a paper trail.
doneWhen: Accounts exist with dual control, limits, evidence capture, and a working reconciliation process.

title: Set up the organization-owned design workspace
what: Establish organization-owned Figma or equivalent with library owner, backup, file naming, and export/archive process.
why: Design source files owned by the org means the artwork survives any one person leaving.
doneWhen: Org-owned workspace exists with owner, backup, naming convention, and export/archive process documented.

title: Set up video/creative storage for campaign masters
what: Use existing organization cloud storage with verified local availability, folder owner, backup, and release metadata.
why: Campaign masters and consent releases must never live only on one editor's drive.
doneWhen: Storage is organized with owner, backup, release metadata, and local availability verified.

title: Establish the print vendor relationship with data protections
what: Set up a purchase-order relationship (not subscription) with NDA/data handling for codes, proof approval process, and deletion certificate.
why: The printer will hold live sticker codes — the relationship must protect them like the secrets they are.
doneWhen: Vendor is selected with signed NDA/data-handling terms, proof approval process, and deletion certificate commitment.

title: Set up perk shipping/fulfillment
what: Choose manual small-batch or a vendor after quantities are known, with address privacy, labels, tracking, and a replacement owner.
why: Backers' addresses are personal data and their perks are promises — both handled with care.
doneWhen: Fulfillment method is chosen with address privacy, tracking, and a named replacement owner.

title: Decide Vercel paid tier with spending controls
what: Before production team use, assess whether free terms/limits suffice; budget seats, bandwidth, functions, logs, and domains with a monthly ceiling and usage alerts.
why: Hosting costs stay honest when a ceiling and alerts exist before the first bill.
doneWhen: Tier decision is recorded with a monthly ceiling and usage alerts configured.

title: Activate Supabase paid tier before real beta data
what: Move to the paid project before any real beta data, budgeting base project, compute, storage, egress, and backups/PITR with 50/75/90% usage reviews.
why: Real people's data never sits on a free tier with weak guarantees.
doneWhen: Paid tier is active before beta data with usage-review thresholds set at 50/75/90%.

title: Decide GitHub paid features when controls require them
what: Upgrade seats/add-ons only when branch/security/team controls require paid features, with quarterly access reviews.
why: Paying for controls the team actually needs, reviewed quarterly, keeps access tight and spend small.
doneWhen: A tier decision is recorded and the quarterly access review is on the calendar.

title: Budget Wefunder fees from the signed listing agreement
what: Budget the published 7.9% of a successful Community Round plus annual admin fee, using the current signed listing agreement rather than the webpage estimate; legal/accounting/entity costs budgeted separately.
why: Fee math from the signed contract keeps use-of-proceeds promises accurate to the dollar.
doneWhen: The budget reflects the signed listing agreement's fee terms with separate line items for legal/accounting/entity costs.

title: Engage securities counsel and CPA with written scope
what: Engage counsel/CPA before the live offering for entity/cap table, Form C, instrument, financial statement level, filings, and annual reporting, with written scope and capped/approved fees.
why: Professional review before the raise protects every investor — and the founders — from avoidable legal harm.
doneWhen: Signed engagement letters exist with written scope and capped or approved fees.

title: Activate domain and email subscriptions immediately after the final name
what: Pay for registration, privacy, renewal, and mail seats with auto-renew and a backup card on file.
why: A lapsed renewal must never be the thing that takes Worth Keeping offline.
doneWhen: Domain and email are paid with auto-renew enabled and a backup payment card recorded.

title: Decide Sentry paid tier when limits are exceeded
what: Upgrade only when free limits or required retention/team features are exceeded, budgeting events, retention, and seats with a data-scrubbing review and budget alert.
why: Observability spend should follow real need, and every upgrade re-checks that no personal data leaks into events.
doneWhen: The tier decision is recorded with a completed data-scrubbing review and an active budget alert.

title: Activate paid transactional email before production notifications
what: Move to the paid tier before production authentication/notifications, budgeting message volume (dedicated IP only if justified) with bounce/complaint thresholds.
why: Delivery thresholds catch reputation problems before recipients stop getting critical messages.
doneWhen: Paid tier is active with bounce/complaint thresholds monitored.

title: Engage bookkeeping before receiving any campaign or business funds
what: Set up monthly bookkeeping, tax, payroll/contractor, and filings support with monthly close and budget-to-actual reporting.
why: Money entrusted to Worth Keeping is tracked professionally from the first dollar.
doneWhen: Bookkeeping engagement is active with a monthly close and budget-to-actual in place before funds arrive.

title: Secure insurance before field activity
what: Obtain adviser-selected general/professional/cyber/event/other coverage before field activity, events, staff/contractors, or inventory custody.
why: Insurance protects the people in the field and the mission behind them when something goes wrong.
doneWhen: Coverage certificates are on file with a renewal calendar before any field activity.

title: Budget print and packaging costs after proof approval
