what: Budget setup, proofs, unit tiers, packaging, freight, tax, defects, and rush costs, controlled by purchase order and acceptance sampling.
why: Knowing the true landed cost of a sticker keeps every downstream promise affordable.
doneWhen: The print/packaging budget is documented and tied to a purchase order with acceptance sampling defined.

title: Budget fulfillment and postage before physical perks are offered
what: Model pick/pack, mailer, postage, tracking, returns, replacements, and tax/customs with a landed-cost model and reserve.
why: A perk offered without a landed-cost model is a promise the campaign might not be able to keep.
doneWhen: The landed-cost model and reserve are documented before perks are offered publicly.

title: Commission accessibility and security review before real beta
what: Budget and run a fixed review or specialist hours, gating beta on findings severity and remediation.
why: Independent eyes on accessibility and security protect the people least able to absorb a failure.
doneWhen: Review findings are triaged by severity and the remediation gate is passed before real beta.

title: Apply the account setup standard to every platform account
what: For every account record legal account name, organization owner, billing owner, backup admin, MFA, recovery method, roles, data processed, processor terms, production tier, spending cap, renewal date, incident contact, export format, offboarding procedure, and deletion confirmation; no shared credential in source, chat, or personal notes.
why: A complete account register means no service can be lost, hijacked, or forgotten when it matters.
doneWhen: The account register covers every active platform with all sixteen fields and no credential stored outside the vault.

title: Run the decision checkpoint for intentionally deferred services
what: Reconsider POS, CRM/ad-network export, automated payout, SMS, AI personalization, data warehouse, native mobile distribution, international shipping, and multi-city operations — adding any only after a documented workflow, data review, budget, owner, test plan, and vendor-exit path are approved.
why: Deliberate restraint keeps the pilot focused; this checkpoint keeps the restraint deliberate rather than forgotten.
doneWhen: A written decision exists for each deferred service, and any adopted service has all six approval artifacts.

title: Hold the funding-path decision meeting
what: Answer the four decision-framework questions in order — is community financial ownership required in 2026 or acceptable in 2027, what must the money buy first, can the company accept pre-revenue debt, is six-figure compliance overhead acceptable now — and select 'Path C first' or 'sequence A then C'.
why: Every campaign, legal, and budget task downstream depends on choosing the right funding family before anyone spends money or makes a promise.
doneWhen: A dated decision record exists answering all four framework questions and naming the selected sequence.

title: Qualify ioby with one call
what: Make one qualification call to ioby to learn whether a for-profit community project can run a civic crowdfunding campaign there, or whether the structure disqualifies it.
why: If civic money is available, community dollars stretch further for the neighbors this project serves — but only a real answer settles it.
doneWhen: The call has happened and ioby's eligibility answer is logged for the decision meeting.

source.section: Part 2 / Open questions carried to the decision meeting

title: Verify Patronicity matching-grant availability in Texas
what: Contact Patronicity to confirm whether a Texas matching program exists and whether a for-profit placemaking project qualifies.
why: Where a match exists, every community dollar can be doubled — too much leverage to leave unverified.
doneWhen: Texas program availability and eligibility are confirmed in writing or ruled out, and logged for the decision meeting.

source.section: Part 2 / Open questions carried to the decision meeting

title: Decide whether the fiscal-sponsorship variant gets a counsel conversation
what: Decide whether a 501(c)(3) fiscal-sponsor structure is worth one counsel conversation or is closed on for-profit grounds, given it would constrain what funds may buy.
why: Backers deserve a settled, honest answer on deductibility before any campaign copy is written.
doneWhen: The decision (pursue one counsel conversation or close the option) is recorded in the decision-meeting notes.

source.section: Open questions carried to the decision meeting

title: Decide whether the collectible sticker is a Stage 1 reward
what: Decide whether Stage 1 offers the collectible sticker itself as the single physical reward or stays purely recognition-based.
why: The choice changes fulfillment cost, sales-tax exposure, and the promise made to every backer.
doneWhen: The reward-scope decision is recorded and reflected in the Stage 1 budget and tier definitions.

source.section: Open questions carried to the decision meeting

title: Obtain publishable letters of interest from a partner and a business
what: Secure a publishable letter of interest from at least one prospective community partner and one participating business, or prepare the explicit statement that recruitment remains in progress.
why: Real names honestly represented are what separate this campaign from vaporware — and protect the partners from misrepresentation.
doneWhen: At least one publishable partner letter and one business letter exist, or the approved recruitment-in-progress language is finalized.

title: Begin adviser and prospective partner/business outreach
what: Run the warmup Week 2 outreach: contact advisers, prospective community partners, and businesses to build the relationships the campaign and pilot will lean on.
why: Trusted advisers and early partners keep the founders honest and the pilot grounded in real relationships.
doneWhen: An outreach log shows adviser and partner/business contacts made with recorded responses.

title: Stand up the issuing entity, cap table, and data room
what: Complete the warmup Week 1 foundation: eligible issuing entity, clean cap table, and an organized data room for platform and counsel review.
why: No one should be asked to invest in an entity that isn't real, clean, and inspectable.
doneWhen: Entity documents, cap table, and data room exist and are ready for counsel and platform review.

title: Produce a manufactured sample sticker that scans reliably
what: Get a manufactured sample sticker produced and verify it scans reliably, meeting the physical-proof half of the pre-campaign proof standard.
why: No campaign in any funding family is honest until a real sticker scans in the real world.
doneWhen: A manufactured sample scans reliably and the test results are logged as campaign-citable proof.

title: Run the controlled end-to-end demonstration
what: Run a controlled demonstration from sticker assignment through offer reveal, merchant verification, and an explicitly simulated accountability entry.
why: Supporters deserve to see the actual loop work before being asked to fund it at scale.
doneWhen: The full demo runs end-to-end with the simulated accountability entry clearly labeled, and the run is recorded.

title: Confirm the Stage 1 platform (Kickstarter-first)
what: Confirm Kickstarter (Design/Art category) as the Stage 1 platform, with Indiegogo fixed-funding and GoFundMe documented as fallbacks and civic-platform qualification results folded in.
why: All-or-nothing mechanics protect backers from funding half a proof that can't be delivered.
doneWhen: The platform choice and fallback order are documented with current verified fee terms.

title: Build the Stage 1 bottom-up budget and all-or-nothing goal
what: Build a bottom-up budget sizing the Stage 1 goal (target range $25K–$60K) at the minimum that fully funds physical proof and the working demonstration after ~8–10% total fees and reward costs.
why: A goal that can't fully fund proof after fees would take neighbors' money for an outcome that can't be delivered.
doneWhen: A line-item Stage 1 budget exists and the all-or-nothing goal covers proof plus rewards after fees.

source.section: Part 4 — Recommended sequence / Part 5 — Path A execution sketch

title: Cost out and finalize the founding-member recognition ladder
what: Cost out and review the five recognition tiers (Neighbor, Storyteller, Founding Member, Street Namer, Founding Circle), confirming names, prices, plaque and event logistics, and Founding Circle capacity limits.
why: Recognition perks only stay dignified if every promised honor is actually affordable and deliverable.
doneWhen: Each tier has a confirmed name, price, fulfillment cost, and capacity limit approved for the campaign page.

title: Codify recognition and perk integrity rules across all paths
what: Write the binding rules: no participant access is ever sold, no perk implies influence over content, businesses, or funds, dedication naming passes a moderation policy, recognition is optional and revocable on request, no perk is named after an individual distributor, and no dignity-compromising perk exists anywhere.
why: These rules are what keep the people at the center of Worth Keeping from ever becoming the product being sold.
doneWhen: The written rules and the dedication moderation policy are approved and referenced by every perk and tier definition.

source.section: Part 3 recognition rules / Investor perk recommendation / Guardrails

title: Write and review Stage 1 claims language
what: Apply every v1 honest-campaign-promise constraint to the Stage 1 page, plus: never call contributions donations-for-tax-purposes, and state plainly that the company is for-profit and supporters receive recognition, not ownership.
why: Backers giving out of goodwill deserve total honesty about what their money is and isn't.
doneWhen: Stage 1 copy passes claims review with the for-profit and no-tax-deduction statements verified on the page.

source.section: Part 5 — Path A execution sketch, Claims rules

title: Adapt the campaign narrative for the Stage 1 recognition ask
what: Build the Stage 1 page using the identical hero and story spine as the Path C outline — only the ask changes from investment to recognition support.
why: The story of a neighbor who gives first shouldn't change with the funding mechanism; consistency is credibility.
doneWhen: A complete Stage 1 page draft exists following the Path C story spine with a recognition-based ask.

source.section: Part 5 — Path A execution sketch, Campaign narrative

title: Build the Stage 1 to Stage 3 opt-in consent bridge
what: Collect opt-in consent from Stage 1 backers to be contacted about a possible future investment round, while keeping all securities terms out of the rewards campaign.
why: A consented warm audience is the honest alternative to buying a cold list — and the law requires the separation.
doneWhen: The opt-in mechanism is live, consent records are stored, and the campaign contains no securities terms.

source.section: Part 5 — Path A execution sketch, Bridge to Stage 3

title: Decide on the Kiva $15K microloan add-on
what: Decide whether to pursue the Kiva US $15,000 0%-interest, no-fee microloan dedicated to the production run, and apply if approved — accepting the small but real repayment obligation.
why: Fifteen thousand zero-cost dollars could fund the physical proof by itself without touching anyone's goodwill money.
doneWhen: A go/no-go decision is recorded; if go, the Kiva application is submitted with proceeds dedicated to production.

source.section: Part 4 — Recommended sequence / Path B note

title: Hold the Stage 2 evidence checkpoint
what: Verify the Stage 2 standard before any Stage 3 planning proceeds: manufactured sticker scans, controlled demo runs, partner and business letters exist, and a consented warm audience exists.
why: The community investment round must sit on proof, not projections — this checkpoint is the promise-keeping mechanism.
doneWhen: All four evidence items are verified and the checkpoint result is recorded.

title: Make the Stage 3 go/no-go decision
what: Decide whether to run the community investment round at all — only if the beta still needs capital beyond Stage 1 proceeds plus any business sponsorship revenue, and only if community ownership is still wanted.
why: Taking on years of investor obligations when the beta is already funded would serve no one.
doneWhen: A recorded decision exists showing the capital-need math and the go/no-go outcome.

title: Re-quote all funding platforms in writing at decision time
what: Obtain current written terms from Wefunder, Republic, and StartEngine before committing to a Reg CF platform, and re-verify every third-party fee figure against each platform's own published terms.
why: Committing on stale fee data could quietly cost the community thousands of raised dollars.
doneWhen: Current written terms from all three Reg CF platforms are on file and all fee figures in the plan are re-verified.

source.section: Recommended campaign architecture / Official and platform references

title: Get counsel-approved SAFE terms
what: Adopt the Wefunder/platform-approved SAFE form with counsel-approved valuation cap, discount, MFN, and pro-rata terms.
why: The instrument's fine print is the actual promise made to every community investor.
doneWhen: Counsel has approved the SAFE form and all economic terms in writing.

title: Write the plain-language SAFE risk explainer
what: Explain in plain language what causes conversion and what does not, dilution, lack of voting/control rights, transfer restrictions, illiquidity, and total-loss risk — and never market the valuation cap as a valuation or expected return.
why: Non-accredited neighbors investing for the first time deserve to understand exactly what they could lose.
doneWhen: The plain-language explainer covering all listed risk elements is counsel-approved and on the campaign page.

title: Label the financial/value event as simulated in all campaign materials
what: Ensure the campaign clearly labels the participant financial/value event as simulated until legal classification and real pilot authorization are complete.
why: Overselling the money loop before it's legal would betray the exact people the platform exists to honor.
doneWhen: Every campaign reference to the value event carries the simulated label, verified in claims review.

title: Enforce the story-visuals-are-not-evidence rule
what: Audit campaign materials so existing story visuals illustrate the vision but never impersonate real pilot evidence.
why: The difference between a dream honestly labeled and a fake result is the whole reputation of the project.
doneWhen: A visual audit confirms every asset is labeled as illustration or backed by a real logged test.

title: Replace scenario figures with the Path C bottom-up budget
what: Replace the $100K/$250K/$500K scenario envelope with a bottom-up budget built from signed adviser scopes, staffing estimates, vendor quotes, platform/listing terms, insurance, tax/accounting treatment, and fulfillment costs — with the minimum covering all non-cancellable obligations and a viable proof outcome after fees, netting any Stage 1 raise dollar-for-dollar.
why: Publishing a number nobody has costed would ask investors to fund a guess.
doneWhen: A bottom-up budget with sourced line items replaces the scenario figures and sets published minimum, target, and maximum.

title: Recalculate the use-of-proceeds allocation from actual costs
what: Recalculate the target allocation percentages (engineering 32%, community ops 16%, physical 9%, legal 11%, campaign 12%, business 7%, contingency 13%) from actual costs before publication, with each line's release condition documented.
why: Investors are told exactly where their money goes; the numbers have to be real before they're public.
doneWhen: Published allocations derive from the bottom-up budget and each release condition is documented.

source.section: Funding model — Initial target allocation model

title: Complete the audience inventory
what: Inventory the priority audiences — personal/professional network, Austin locals, business owners, collectors, impact investors, creators/press/civic connectors, and Stage 1 opt-in backers — as the base of the warm-audience build.
why: Knowing who genuinely cares lets the campaign invite rather than intrude.
doneWhen: A documented audience inventory covering all seven priority groups exists.

title: Build a consented warm audience
what: Build a consented warm audience rather than buying an unqualified list, per the readiness targets.
why: A campaign about dignity can't start by spamming strangers.
doneWhen: The warm-audience list exists with recorded consent for every contact and no purchased names.

title: Enforce the participant-story media consent rule
what: Ensure participant stories are never used as an acquisition channel unless people independently volunteer under approved, revocable media consent.
why: The people this platform serves must never be turned into marketing material without their freely given, reversible consent.
doneWhen: The media consent policy is approved and no campaign asset uses a participant story without a consent record.

title: Write the founder narrative and landing profile
what: Complete the warmup Week 1 founder narrative and campaign landing profile.
why: People fund people; the founders owe the audience a true, well-told account of who is asking and why.
doneWhen: The founder narrative and landing profile are drafted and approved for use.

title: Draft the full campaign page
what: Draft the campaign page following the fifteen-section outline: hero, inversion, how it works, why businesses participate, the physical object, product demonstration, why Austin, business model, use of proceeds, team, safeguards and privacy, investment terms, risks, perks, and call to action.
why: The page is where every promise, proof, and risk meets the public in one place.
doneWhen: A complete draft covering all fifteen outline sections exists and is submitted for review.

source.section: Campaign narrative/page outline / Six-week warmup, Week 3

title: Build the campaign financial model
what: Complete the warmup Week 3 financial model supporting the offering, connected to the bottom-up budget and use-of-proceeds allocations.
why: The model is how the team proves to itself and investors that the plan's math closes.
doneWhen: The financial model exists, reconciles with the bottom-up budget, and is ready for CPA and counsel review.

title: Obtain reviewed financial statements
what: Engage the CPA to produce the reviewed financial statements Reg CF requires for the Form C filing.
why: Reviewed financials are the community's independent check that the company's numbers are true.
doneWhen: CPA-reviewed financial statements are delivered and accepted for the Form C.

source.section: Part 2 — Family 5 requirements / Campaign release checklist

title: Write the full risk disclosures
what: Draft the risk section covering startup, execution, regulatory, partner, adoption, print, fraud, privacy, financing, and illiquidity risks.
why: Naming every way this could fail is how the campaign respects the people deciding to risk their money.
doneWhen: All ten risk categories are drafted and counsel-approved for the page and Form C.

source.section: Campaign narrative/page outline / Six-week warmup, Week 3

title: Define the investor perk ladder
what: Define no more than five cumulative investor perks (Founding Community, Launch Collector, Numbered Founder Edition, Builder Access, Austin Launch Circle) with thresholds set only after Wefunder and fulfillment approval, kept visibly distinct from Stage 1 recognition tiers, and never represented as investment return.
why: Perks should thank investors without ever blurring into a promised return or a recycled Stage 1 offer.
doneWhen: The perk ladder is finalized with approved thresholds, distinct from Stage 1 tiers, and cleared by claims review.

title: Build the perk bill of materials
what: Complete the warmup Week 3 perk BOM costing every perk component ahead of page approval.
why: Every promised object must be priced before it is promised, or backers pay for the shortfall.
doneWhen: A complete perk BOM with unit costs exists and feeds the budget and landed-cost model.

title: Finalize perk SKUs and domestic shipping limits
what: Finalize perk SKUs and set shipping to domestic United States only before page approval, unless the fulfillment model proves international compliance and cost.
why: A shipping promise the team can't legally or affordably keep becomes a broken promise to a supporter.
doneWhen: SKU list and US-only shipping scope are finalized and reflected on the page before approval.

title: Reserve pilot inventory before allocating collectible rewards
what: Reserve the sticker inventory the pilot needs before any collectible rewards are allocated to campaign perks.
why: The pilot's participants come first; perks must never consume the inventory the actual program depends on.
doneWhen: A documented inventory reservation for the pilot exists and perk allocations draw only from the remainder.

title: Build the full landed-cost model for perks
what: Include setup, proof, unit, packaging, inbound freight, pick/pack, postage, taxes, loss, replacement, support, and contingency in the landed cost of every physical perk.
why: Hidden fulfillment costs are how funded campaigns still fail their backers.
doneWhen: Every perk has a landed cost covering all twelve components, reconciled into the budget.

title: Produce the founder video and product demo footage
what: Complete warmup Week 4 production of the founder video and product demonstration media.
why: Seeing the founder and the working loop is what turns a page of claims into something people trust.
doneWhen: Finished founder video and demo footage are delivered and queued for accessibility and claims review.

title: Run the accessibility, claims, and legal review of campaign materials
what: Run warmup Week 4's combined accessibility, claims, and legal review across the page, video, and media.
why: A campaign about dignity must be accurate and accessible to every person it invites in.
doneWhen: Review findings are logged and all materials pass accessibility, claims, and legal checks.

title: Build the email education sequence
what: Create the warmup Week 4 email sequence that educates the consented audience about the project and offering within permitted-communication rules.
why: An informed supporter makes a real choice; an ambushed one doesn't.
doneWhen: The sequence is written, claims-reviewed, and loaded for the consented list.

title: Run private previews for the warm audience
what: Hold warmup Week 5 private previews of the campaign for the consented audience.
why: Early honest feedback from friends catches problems before strangers pay for them.
doneWhen: Previews have run and feedback is logged into the objections/FAQ work.

title: Build the objections and FAQ document
what: Compile the warmup Week 5 objections list and FAQ from preview feedback and anticipated criticism.
why: Answering hard questions before launch is how the team earns the right to ask for money.
doneWhen: A reviewed FAQ addressing collected objections is published or ready for the platform Q&A.

title: Collect indications of interest to 25% of target
what: Gather investor indications of interest through approved platform mechanisms until non-binding interest equals at least 25% of the intended target, before choosing a public launch date.
why: Launching cold risks a visible failure that hurts everyone who lent the project their name.
doneWhen: Platform-recorded non-binding interest reaches 25% of target and the threshold is documented.

title: Brief press and creators under communication rules
what: Run warmup Week 5 press and creator briefings under the campaign communication rules, leading with local commerce, public art, accountability, and the giver-first exchange.
why: Local storytellers deserve accurate material, and the project deserves coverage it can stand behind.
doneWhen: Briefings are complete and every material shared came from the approved press kit.

source.section: Six-week warmup, Week 5 / Press and community outreach

title: Assemble the approved press kit
what: Build a press kit containing only approved founder/team, product, physical proof, roadmap, risk, and contact materials.
why: Journalists repeating an unapproved claim would put the campaign's honesty on the line.
doneWhen: The press kit exists with all six approved material types and nothing else.

title: Set the press claims guardrails and correction path
what: Establish that no public statement alleges named advertising companies manipulate markets without campaign counsel and evidence approval, and stand up a correction path plus direct responses to criticism about participant dignity, privacy, financial classification, and business incentives.
why: The project wins by being more honest than its critics expect, not by throwing accusations it can't prove.
doneWhen: The guardrail is documented, the correction path is live, and response owners for the four criticism areas are named.

title: Stand up the universal claims/legal review process
what: Establish the standing process by which every public number and partner claim — in any funding family, any channel — passes claims/legal review before publication.
why: One unreviewed claim can undo months of carefully earned trust with the community.
doneWhen: A named claims/legal owner and documented review workflow exist and are applied to all public materials.

source.section: Guardrails that apply to every path

title: Enforce the one-offer-type-per-campaign rule
what: Verify every campaign contains exactly one offer type — never blending a reward, a donation, and a security in the same ask — and that no tax-deduction claim appears in any family.
why: Mixing offers confuses supporters about what they're actually getting and invites legal trouble that would hurt everyone.
doneWhen: Each campaign's materials are audited and confirmed to contain a single offer type and zero tax-deduction claims.

source.section: Guardrails that apply to every path

title: Enforce pre-launch solicitation controls
what: Ensure the team does not accept funds, promise securities, or communicate unfiled offering terms outside permitted channels before launch.
why: One eager side conversation could legally poison the entire offering for every investor.
doneWhen: The team is briefed in writing on the controls and no pre-launch communication violates them.

title: Identify the first-day cohort and response owners
what: Identify the first-day investor/supporter cohort and assign named response owners before launch.
why: A strong, cared-for first day is what convinces the wider community the project is real.
doneWhen: The first-day cohort list and named response owners are documented.

title: Obtain final platform, counsel, and CPA approval
what: Complete warmup Week 6 final approvals: Wefunder accepts the issuer, security, disclosures, financials, target/max/deadline, and communications; counsel approves Form C, investment contract, risks, promoters, advertisements, perks, and material claims; CPA signs off.
why: These three approvals are the community's professional safeguards; launching without them would be launching on hope.
doneWhen: Written platform acceptance, counsel approval, and CPA sign-off are all on file.

source.section: Six-week warmup, Week 6 / Campaign release checklist

title: Confirm the entity readiness gate
what: Confirm eligible issuing entity, cap table, governance, bank, accounting, and insurance ahead of campaign release, per the release checklist.
why: The corporate foundation has to be solid before it holds other people's money.
doneWhen: All six checklist elements are verified and checked off the campaign release checklist.

title: Verify partner/business status is represented exactly
what: Confirm the campaign represents every partner and business relationship exactly as it stands — contracted, letter-of-interest, or in-recruitment.
why: Partners lent their credibility; misstating their commitment would spend it without permission.
doneWhen: Every partner/business mention on the page matches its documented status and is checked off the release checklist.

title: Approve or defer participant-value language
what: Confirm all participant-value language is professionally approved or explicitly labeled deferred/simulated before release.
why: The value promise to participants is the most sensitive claim the project makes; it must never run ahead of professional approval.
doneWhen: Every participant-value statement is either professionally approved or carries the deferred/simulated label, checked off the release checklist.

title: Document all media rights and releases
what: Document rights and releases for all art, footage, music, logos, quotes, and person releases used in campaign materials.
why: Using someone's work or likeness without documented permission would contradict everything the project stands for.
doneWhen: A rights log covers every asset on the page and in the video, checked off the release checklist.

title: Confirm perks, fulfillment, support, and contingency are costed and owned
what: Verify the release-checklist gate that every perk, the fulfillment operation, support, and contingency each have a cost and a named owner.
why: A promise with no owner and no budget is a promise waiting to be broken.
doneWhen: Each of the four areas shows a cost line and a named owner, checked off the release checklist.

title: Test page, video, mobile, captions, transcript, links, forms, and support
what: Run the release-checklist test pass across the page, video, mobile rendering, captions, transcript, links, forms, and support channels.
why: A supporter blocked by a broken link or missing caption is a supporter the campaign failed.
doneWhen: All eight test areas pass and results are logged against the release checklist.

title: Rehearse all campaign runbooks
what: Write and rehearse the launch, incident, delay, shortfall, overfunding, refund, material-change, and post-close runbooks until each passes rehearsal, alongside the Week 6 access/support/incident checks and launch rehearsal.
why: When something goes wrong mid-campaign with real money moving, the team must already know exactly what to do.
doneWhen: All eight runbooks exist, rehearsal passes are logged, and the Week 6 launch rehearsal and access/support/incident checks are complete.

source.section: Campaign release checklist / Six-week warmup, Week 6

title: Send the approved launch announcement
what: Founder sends the approved network/email announcement and posts the approved platform link at launch.
why: The people who trusted the project first deserve to hear about launch directly from the founder.
doneWhen: The approved announcement is sent and the platform link is posted, both from pre-approved copy.

title: Run first-48-hours monitoring
what: Monitor platform questions, support requests, uptime, press/creator responses, misinformation, and material changes for the first 48 hours.
why: The opening window sets the tone; unanswered questions or unchecked misinformation would erode trust fastest exactly when it matters most.
doneWhen: A monitoring log covering all six areas exists for the full first 48 hours with every item triaged.

title: Record commitments only from platform reports
what: Finance records investment commitments only from the platform's own reports, accepting no side-channel investments.
why: Off-platform money would break the legal protections every other investor is relying on.
doneWhen: Financial records reconcile exactly to platform reports and any side-channel attempt is declined and logged.

title: Claims-review every public update while live
what: The claims/legal owner reviews every public update containing numbers, partners, participant outcomes, or terms before it posts.
why: Mid-campaign excitement is exactly when an untrue number is most likely to slip out.
doneWhen: Every posted update with covered content shows a logged pre-publication review.

title: Publish the first transparent data update
what: Publish one transparent update based on verified platform data within the first 48 hours, without manufacturing urgency.
why: Showing real numbers early — good or bad — teaches supporters this campaign tells the truth.
doneWhen: The update is published, sourced entirely from platform data, and contains no manufactured-urgency language.

title: Publish weekly campaign updates while live
what: Publish a claims-reviewed campaign update every week for the duration of the live raise.
why: Consistent honest communication is the ongoing rent the campaign pays on supporters' trust.
doneWhen: An update is published for every week the campaign is live, each with a logged claims review.

title: Run material-change communication and reconfirmation
what: Communicate any material change through the platform and run investor reconfirmation when required.
why: Investors who committed under old facts have the right to reconsider under new ones.
doneWhen: Every material change during the raise shows a platform communication and, where required, a completed reconfirmation cycle.

title: Establish the post-close investor update cadence
what: Send monthly investor updates through build and beta, then quarterly thereafter unless offering obligations require more.
why: The people who funded the build deserve to watch it happen, not wonder if it did.
doneWhen: The cadence is documented and the first monthly post-close update has shipped on schedule.

title: File annual Regulation Crowdfunding reports
what: Complete the annual Reg CF reporting obligation for as long as legally required after the raise.
why: Annual reporting is the multi-year legal promise made to every community investor the day they invested.
doneWhen: Each required annual report is filed on time and confirmed with counsel.

title: Collect fulfillment addresses only after close, with retention rules
what: Collect shipping addresses only after the offering closes, only for required fulfillment, and publish the retention/deletion rules.
why: Supporters' personal data is held in trust, gathered only when needed and let go when done.
doneWhen: Address collection opens only post-close, the published retention/deletion policy is live, and no address is used beyond fulfillment.

title: Send the full sequence of fulfillment updates
what: Send production, delay, shipment, tracking, loss/replacement, and completion updates to every perk recipient.
why: A backer waiting in silence stops being a believer; steady updates keep the relationship whole.
doneWhen: Every fulfillment stage has a sent update on record, through completion or replacement for every recipient.

title: Reconsider debt and revenue-share instruments after recurring revenue
what: Hold the decision checkpoint on Honeycomb Credit, SMBX, and revenue-share/loan/convertible instruments only after reliable recurring business revenue and repayment sensitivity analysis exist.
why: Taking on scheduled repayment too early would divert money from the participants the platform exists to pay.
doneWhen: A dated reconsideration decision exists, made against actual recurring-revenue data and a repayment sensitivity analysis.

source.section: Path B note / Security/instrument decision — Deferred alternatives

title: Reconsider Patreon membership and Indiegogo InDemand after the beta
what: Hold the decision checkpoint on a Patreon 'Collector's Club' recurring membership to fund ongoing drops, and on Indiegogo InDemand for keeping the store open post-campaign — both explicitly deferred as wrong tools for the build raise.
why: Recurring supporters deserve a considered offer built on a proven program, not a membership sold before there's anything ongoing to belong to.
doneWhen: A dated decision record exists for both options, made after the beta against actual drop and campaign outcomes.

source.section: Part 2 — Donation and rewards platforms (Patreon, Indiegogo notes)

title: Confirm the local development machine tool baseline
what: Verify installed versions of Node.js, npm/npx, Git, GitHub CLI, Python, jq, ripgrep, and Apple Command Line Tools, and note which tools (Docker, Supabase CLI, Vercel CLI, Homebrew, Corepack) are absent from PATH.
why: Knowing exactly what the build machine has keeps every later setup step honest instead of assumed.
doneWhen: A dated confirmation lists each tool with its verified version and each missing tool by name.

title: Configure Git project identity and create the first reviewed baseline commit
what: Run git config --local for the approved name and approved organization email, then create the first reviewed baseline commit for the repository.
why: A clean, attributable baseline protects the team's ability to trace every change from day one.
doneWhen: git status is clean, git log -1 --oneline shows the reviewed baseline commit, and local user.name/user.email match the approved identity.

title: Move the private repository into the approved organization with branch protection
what: Verify gh auth status, create or transfer the private repository into the approved GitHub organization, and enable branch protection and required checks in repository settings.
why: Organization ownership and protected branches keep the codebase safe from single-account loss or unreviewed changes.
doneWhen: gh repo view --json nameWithOwner,visibility,defaultBranchRef shows the repo private under the approved organization with branch protection and required checks enabled.

title: Pin the Node.js 22 LTS version with .nvmrc
what: Retain Node 22 as the baseline and commit a .nvmrc containing the exact approved 22.x version; install the official macOS ARM64 LTS package on any machine that lacks it.
why: An exact pinned runtime means every contributor and CI run builds the same thing recipients will actually use.
doneWhen: node --version matches the approved 22.x version recorded in the committed .nvmrc.

title: Confirm the legal entity and organization email domain
what: Confirm the legal entity exists and the organization email domain is established before any hosted workspaces are created.
why: Every account, contract, and promise made after this rests on a real entity, not a personal login.
doneWhen: The legal entity and organization email domain are confirmed in writing and referenced by the hosted-tool setup sequence.

title: Create organization-owned hosted workspaces
what: Create organization-owned GitHub, Vercel, Supabase, password-manager, email, monitoring, and design workspaces.
why: Organization ownership means no single person's departure or account loss can strand the project's infrastructure.
doneWhen: Each of the seven workspace types exists under the organization account, not a personal account.

title: Add backup owner, require MFA, and remove unused default access
what: Add Chrix plus one backup owner to every hosted workspace, require MFA on all accounts, and remove unused default access.
why: A second owner and MFA protect the whole operation from lockout and account takeover.
doneWhen: Every workspace shows two owners, MFA enforced, and no unused default members or permissions.

title: Configure domains and email authentication
what: Configure the project domains and email authentication (sender verification) for the organization.
why: Authenticated email keeps messages to supporters and partners out of spam and hard to spoof.
doneWhen: Domains resolve correctly and email authentication records validate for the organization domain.

title: Store all secrets only in the password manager and environment stores
what: Move every credential and secret into the organization password manager and platform environment stores; no secrets in code, chat, or personal notes.
why: One disciplined home for secrets protects recipients' data and the team from a single careless leak.
doneWhen: An audit finds all known secrets in the password manager or environment stores and none anywhere else.

title: Record billing owner, caps, alerts, renewals, processors, export, and offboarding for each service
what: For every hosted service, record the billing owner, spending caps, alerts, renewal dates, payment processors, data-export path, and offboarding steps.
why: Written billing and exit paths stop surprise charges and vendor lock-in from draining a small budget.
doneWhen: A register lists all seven items for every hosted service in use.

title: Assemble the minimum accountable delivery group
what: Name accountable owners covering executive/product, technical lead, full-stack engineer, product/UX/accessibility design, community operations/safeguarding, partner management, business/sponsor, finance/reconciliation, securities/legal/accounting advisers, campaign production, print/fulfillment, QA/release, and support; people may cover multiple non-conflicting roles.
why: Every promise Worth Keeping makes needs a named human accountable for keeping it.
doneWhen: A roster maps every listed role to a named accountable person with no uncovered role.

title: Enforce separation of duties for finance and safety escalation with backups
what: Ensure financial creation, approval, execution, and reconciliation are held by explicitly separated people, safety escalation is separated likewise, and each has a named backup.
why: No one person should be able to move money or handle a safety crisis alone and unchecked.
doneWhen: The roster shows distinct named people (with backups) for each financial duty and for safety escalation, with no conflicting overlaps.

title: Adopt the training-completion standard for critical skills
what: Establish the rule that safety, consent, finance, inventory, security, accessibility, support, and release skills count as ready only after the assigned owner performs an approved scenario or checklist and a reviewer records the result.
why: Reading a guide is not readiness; the people we serve deserve owners who have actually practiced.
doneWhen: The standard is written into the training register and every critical skill row tracks a performed scenario plus a reviewer record.

title: Evidence product ownership and service design capability
what: Demonstrate product ownership and service design coverage via the approved PDR, service blueprint, backlog, and an acceptance/change-control exercise.
why: Clear product ownership keeps the service honest to the people it claims to serve.
doneWhen: The PDR, service blueprint, and backlog are approved and the acceptance/change-control exercise is recorded by a reviewer.

title: Stand up program and dependency management practice
what: Run a weekly critical-path review with a RAID log, decision register, and milestone reporting.
why: Visible risks and decisions protect the team from quiet slippage that would break promises later.
doneWhen: The weekly critical-path review is running with a maintained RAID log, decision register, and milestone reports.

title: Complete compensated lived-experience review and dignity-language approval
what: Before partner outreach and campaign claims, run a compensated lived-experience review and obtain dignity-language approval for how Worth Keeping talks about the people it serves.
why: The people whose lives this touches must shape and approve the language before anyone else hears it.
doneWhen: A compensated lived-experience review is completed and dignity-language approval is recorded.

title: Obtain written entity, tax, accounting, and benefits determinations
what: Before any funds or participant-value promise, obtain written professional determinations on entity, tax, accounting, and benefits questions, plus a staff language guide.
why: A recipient's public benefits must never be put at risk by a well-meaning but unexamined gift.
doneWhen: Written professional determinations and the staff language guide are on file and approved.

title: Establish vector and prepress capability for print masters
what: Use organization-owned Figma for source/UI and Affinity Designer, Adobe Illustrator, Inkscape, or the printer's prepress service for CMYK and cut paths; export SVG/PDF masters with packaged fonts and links per printer spec.
why: Print-ready masters are what turn the art into a physical object a recipient can actually hold.
doneWhen: The printer preflight report passes and an approved proof exists from the exported masters.

title: Set up browser and device QA coverage with a signed test matrix
what: Test with Safari and Chrome on macOS plus at least one current iPhone and one representative Android device, enabling remote inspection as needed, and sign off the device/browser/QR test matrix.
why: A sticker only counts if it scans on the phones real people actually carry.
doneWhen: A signed device/browser/QR test matrix covers the listed browsers and devices.

title: Install the QR generation library and pass a sample through the device/print matrix
what: After the code specification exists, install qrcode@1.5.4 and @types/qrcode@1.5.6 exactly, keep generation server-side/controlled, and generate a non-production sample that passes the device and print matrix.
why: A QR that fails on one common phone fails the person holding it.
doneWhen: A non-production generated sample passes the full device/print matrix and the pinned packages appear in the lockfile.

title: Evidence print, prepress, and QR QA capability before any printer order
what: Produce the printer spec, run preflight, approve a proof, complete the physical device matrix, and perform defect sampling.
why: Catching print defects before an order protects both the budget and the recipient's first impression.
doneWhen: Printer spec, preflight results, approved proof, completed physical device matrix, and defect-sampling records all exist.

title: Rehearse inventory custody and fulfillment before printing and campaign perks
what: Run a custody rehearsal, count reconciliation, and a pick/pack/replace test for physical inventory.
why: Every serialized sticker is a promise to someone; custody discipline keeps that promise trackable.
doneWhen: The custody rehearsal, count reconciliation, and pick/pack/replace test are each performed and recorded by a reviewer.

title: Evidence consent, privacy, and ethical research capability before any real data or media
what: Complete a plain-language consent role-play, a revocation/deletion drill, and a release audit before collecting any real participant data or media.
why: No one's face or story is used until they have truly understood and freely agreed.
doneWhen: The consent role-play, revocation/deletion drill, and release audit are each performed and recorded by a reviewer.

title: Obtain securities and campaign compliance review before public investment communication
what: Have qualified counsel and the platform review and approve the Form C, terms, and advertising and promoter rules before any public investment communication.
why: Compliance protects every small investor who trusts the campaign with their money.
doneWhen: Counsel/platform approval of the Form C, terms, and advertising/promoter rules is documented.

title: Evidence bookkeeping and reconciliation capability before receiving funds
what: Set up the chart of accounts and perform a dual-control payout/reconciliation rehearsal and a month-end close before any funds are received.
why: Clean books are how the team proves every dollar went where supporters were told it would.
doneWhen: The chart of accounts exists and the dual-control payout/reconciliation rehearsal and month-end close are performed and recorded.

title: Evidence UX and content design capability for product and campaign
what: Produce role flows, a usability protocol, error content, and complete a dignity/claims review of product and campaign content.
why: Words and flows must respect the dignity of recipients and never overstate what the service does.
doneWhen: Role flows, usability protocol, and error content exist and the dignity/claims review is recorded.

title: Evidence campaign strategy, narrative, and media capability
what: Produce a costed campaign calendar, approved story and claims, a filmed demo, and run a launch tabletop exercise.
why: A campaign built on an approved, honest story protects supporters and the people the story is about.
doneWhen: The costed calendar, approved story/claims, filmed demo, and launch tabletop record all exist.

title: Evidence investor relations and support capability before campaign launch
what: Establish the investor update cadence, inquiry and escalation scripts, and the material-change approval process.
why: Investors who trusted a first-time team deserve prompt, accurate, disciplined communication.
doneWhen: Update cadence, inquiry/escalation scripts, and the material-change approval process are documented and approved.

title: Enforce committed lockfile and npm ci in CI
what: Use the committed package-lock.json and run npm ci (never npm install) in CI, verifying npm ci and npm run build pass.
why: Reproducible installs mean the build that passed review is the build that ships.
doneWhen: CI runs npm ci against the committed lockfile and npm run build passes.

title: Install Supabase CLI as an exact-version project dev dependency
what: Run npm install --save-dev --save-exact supabase@2.109.1 (2026-07-18 reviewed baseline) and invoke via npx supabase; install Docker Desktop first if local services are used.
why: A pinned, project-local CLI keeps database tooling identical for everyone touching recipient data.
doneWhen: npx supabase --version reports the pinned version from the project lockfile.

title: Install Docker Desktop for isolated local database tests
what: Download the Apple Silicon DMG, drag Docker to Applications, open it, accept required permissions, and keep Kubernetes disabled unless separately needed (conditional but recommended).
why: Isolated local database tests let the team break things safely instead of near real data.
doneWhen: docker version succeeds and docker run --rm hello-world completes.

title: Install Vercel CLI and link development, staging, and production projects
what: Run npm install --save-dev --save-exact vercel@56.3.2 (2026-07-18 reviewed baseline), authenticate with the organization account, and run npx vercel link separately for development, staging, and production projects.
why: Separate linked environments keep experiments from ever touching what real users see.
doneWhen: npx vercel --version reports the pinned version and npx vercel project ls shows the three linked projects under the organization.

title: Pin TypeScript/ESLint versions and add a typecheck script
what: Replace latest ranges with exact tested versions, run npm ci, and add a typecheck script using tsc --noEmit.
why: Strict, pinned type tooling catches mistakes before they can reach a recipient's screen.
doneWhen: npm run lint and npm run typecheck both pass with exact versions in the manifest and lockfile.

title: Install Vitest and Testing Library at exact versions
what: Run npm install --save-dev --save-exact vitest@4.1.10 jsdom@29.1.1 @testing-library/react@16.3.2 @testing-library/jest-dom@6.9.1 @testing-library/user-event@14.6.1.
why: A working unit-test stack is the everyday guardrail for the code people will rely on.
doneWhen: npx vitest run executes the test suite successfully with the pinned packages in the lockfile.

title: Install Playwright with Chromium and WebKit browsers
what: Run npm install --save-dev --save-exact @playwright/test@1.61.1, then npx playwright install chromium webkit.
why: End-to-end tests in real browser engines prove the flows recipients and supporters will actually walk.
doneWhen: npx playwright test --list succeeds with the pinned package and installed browsers.

title: Install axe accessibility testing and wire it into critical E2E flows
what: Run npm install --save-dev --save-exact @axe-core/playwright@4.12.1 and include axe checks in critical E2E flows.
why: Automated accessibility checks defend access for people who are too often designed out.
doneWhen: The accessibility E2E test runs with zero serious or critical findings.

title: Install zod and create shared server-boundary validation schemas
what: Run npm install --save-exact zod@4.4.3 and create shared schemas that validate data at every server boundary.
why: Validating every input at the boundary protects recipient records from bad or malicious data.
doneWhen: npm ls zod shows the pinned version and validation unit tests pass.

title: Set up Sentry error monitoring before beta
what: Create the Sentry organization/project, run npx @sentry/wizard@latest -i nextjs, review every generated change, and configure the environment secret, data scrubbing, release tracking, and alert rules.
why: Seeing errors fast — without leaking anyone's personal data — is how a small team keeps its promises at night.
doneWhen: A staging-only test error is triggered and correlates in Sentry with no personal data present.

title: Document jq and ripgrep use in verification scripts
what: Document how the installed jq and ripgrep utilities are used in the project's verification scripts.
why: Written verification steps let any teammate check the system, not just the person who built it.
doneWhen: Verification scripts referencing jq and ripgrep are documented and both tools report versions (jq --version, rg --version).

title: Re-verify tool version compatibility and security at implementation start
what: Re-check the 2026-07-18 planning-baseline tool versions for compatibility and security when the build begins, record any approved change, and keep selected versions exact in the manifest and lockfile.
why: A months-old version pin can hide known vulnerabilities the team is accountable for.
doneWhen: Each baseline version is re-verified or updated with an approval record, and the manifest and lockfile hold exact versions.

title: Create local, preview, staging, and production environments with synthetic data outside production
what: Stand up local/preview/staging/production environments and ensure only synthetic data exists outside production.
why: Real people's data lives in exactly one place, and every experiment happens somewhere else.
doneWhen: All four environments exist and a check confirms non-production environments contain only synthetic data.

title: Run the hosted-services acceptance check before production use
what: Run the acceptance check defined in 17-platforms-subscriptions-services.md against every hosted service before any production use.
why: A final acceptance pass catches the misconfiguration that would otherwise surface in front of real users.
doneWhen: The acceptance check from 17-platforms-subscriptions-services.md passes and is recorded for each service before production use.

title: Evidence full-stack TypeScript/Next.js capability against the pinned baseline
what: Demonstrate build, test, and review capability against the pinned project baseline before the build phase proceeds.
why: The people funding the build deserve proof the team can actually ship on this stack.
doneWhen: A demonstrated build/test/review exercise against the pinned baseline is recorded by a reviewer.

title: Evidence PostgreSQL/Supabase/RLS capability with migration and restore exercises
what: Perform a migration, RLS denial and positive tests, and a restore and rollback exercise before the build proceeds.
why: Row-level security drills are how we prove one recipient can never see another's story.
doneWhen: Migration, RLS denial/positive tests, and the restore/rollback exercise are performed and recorded.

title: Evidence financial-ledger and state-machine engineering before the transaction build
what: Review and pass idempotency, invariant, reversal, concurrency, and reconciliation tests for the ledger design.
why: Money attached to vulnerable people must be impossible to double-spend, lose, or silently corrupt.
doneWhen: Idempotency, invariants, reversal, concurrency, and reconciliation test reviews are all passed and recorded.

title: Evidence application security and privacy engineering for architecture and release
what: Produce a threat model, secure-code review, data map, and incident tabletop, with all high findings closed.
why: The people on these stickers have already been failed by systems; ours must not leak or expose them.
doneWhen: Threat model, secure-code review, data map, and incident tabletop exist and all high findings are closed.

title: Evidence test and release engineering for every merge and release
what: Stand up the CI pipeline with test traceability, preview acceptance, migration rollback, and a production smoke test.
why: Disciplined releases mean a fix for one person never quietly breaks the service for everyone else.
doneWhen: CI pipeline, test traceability, preview acceptance, migration rollback, and production smoke test are all demonstrated and recorded.

title: Evidence accessibility design and QA capability for design and release
what: Complete the WCAG 2.2 AA checklist plus keyboard, screen-reader, zoom, contrast, and manual reviews.
why: Accessibility is the difference between honoring someone and locking them out of their own story.
doneWhen: The WCAG 2.2 AA checklist and keyboard/screen-reader/zoom/contrast/manual reviews are completed and recorded.

title: Evidence safeguarding and incident response before real participant onboarding
what: Adopt a partner-approved safeguarding policy, run a scenario drill including the escalation/911 boundary, and complete an incident documentation exercise.
why: Before any real participant joins, the team must have practiced protecting them when something goes wrong.
doneWhen: The partner-approved policy exists and the scenario drill and incident documentation exercise are performed and recorded.

title: Evidence business sales and onboarding capability before pilot recruitment
what: Approve the qualification criteria, offer templates, and agreement; train staff; and rehearse dispute handling.
why: Businesses joining the pilot deserve a clear, fair offer and staff ready to handle problems well.
doneWhen: Approved qualification, offer templates, and agreement exist, and staff training plus the dispute rehearsal are recorded.

title: Approve PDR, scope, tech requirements, and authority model
what: Formally approve the product definition, scope, technical requirements, and the authority model that says who can decide what.
why: Everyone downstream — partners, recipients, engineers — is protected when decisions have a named, accountable owner.
doneWhen: PDR, scope, tech requirements, and authority model documents are marked approved with approver names and dates.

title: Create authoritative Git baseline and preserve story/static artifacts as references
what: Establish one authoritative Git repository as the single source of truth and archive the story/static artifacts as read-only references.
why: One source of truth prevents the team from building on stale or conflicting versions of the work.
doneWhen: The authoritative repo exists, story/static artifacts are preserved as references, and no work happens outside it.

title: Pin dependencies and add CI build/lint/typecheck/test skeleton
what: Pin all dependency versions (no `latest`) and stand up a CI pipeline skeleton covering build, lint, typecheck, and test.
why: Reproducible builds keep the platform trustworthy for the people whose claims and payments will eventually run through it.
doneWhen: No `latest` dependencies remain, a clean checkout builds reproducibly, and CI runs build/lint/typecheck/test on every change.

title: Create organization-owned GitHub/Vercel/Supabase/password/email workspaces with owners and MFA
what: Move all production-relevant accounts into organization-owned workspaces with named owners and MFA enforced.
why: No single person's personal account should be able to take the service — or participant data — down with it.
doneWhen: GitHub, Vercel, Supabase, password manager, and email workspaces are organization-owned, each with a named owner and MFA enabled; no personal-only production account remains.

title: Open claims, risk, decision, vendor, service, and access registers
what: Create the six operating registers — claims, risk, decision, vendor, service, and access — and start recording into them.
why: Written registers keep promises honest and risks visible instead of living in someone's head.
doneWhen: All six registers exist in the repo or workspace with at least their initial entries recorded.

title: Retain entity/securities, tax/accounting, insurance, privacy, and safeguarding advisers
what: Engage professional advisers for entity/securities, tax/accounting, insurance, privacy, and safeguarding before any real-money or participant-impacting behavior.
why: Vulnerable participants and small investors deserve a project that got professional review before asking for their trust.
doneWhen: Each of the five adviser relationships is retained with an engagement record on file.

title: Pass Phase 0 exit review: control established
what: Verify the Phase 0 exit conditions: one source of truth, no personal-only production account, no `latest` dependencies, reproducible clean build, approved backlog, and named gate owners.
why: Building on an uncontrolled foundation puts every later promise to participants and investors at risk.
doneWhen: All six Phase 0 exit conditions are checked and evidenced, and each gate has a named owner.

title: Make final name/domain decision and complete brand/rights cleanup
what: Decide the final product name and domain and clear the brand and rights questions attached to them.
why: A clean name and clear rights let partners and investors say yes without a legal question mark hanging over the project.
doneWhen: The name/domain decision is recorded, the domain is secured, and brand/rights cleanup items are closed in the decision register.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Build community-partner and business outreach package
what: Produce the outreach package used to approach community partners and participating businesses.
why: Partners and businesses can only commit to something they can clearly understand and evaluate.
doneWhen: The outreach package exists and has been used in at least one partner and one business conversation.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Collect letters of interest from partners and businesses
what: Secure written letters of interest from community partners and businesses where available.
why: Real letters give the campaign honest evidence instead of hopeful claims about demand.
doneWhen: Letters of interest are on file and logged in the claims/evidence register.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Form the issuer entity and establish the cap table
what: Form the legal entity that will issue securities and establish its cap table with counsel.
why: Investors and future employees need a clean, honest ownership structure from day one.
doneWhen: The entity is formed, the cap table is documented, and counsel has reviewed both.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Produce service blueprint and role journeys
what: Map the full service blueprint and the journey for each role — distributor, recipient, business, partner, operator.
why: Every role deserves a designed experience, not an accident — especially the recipient holding the sticker.
doneWhen: The service blueprint and per-role journey documents are complete and reviewed by the product owner.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Build controlled click-through demo of the full loop
what: Create a controlled click-through/demo covering pass, scan, reveal, verification, obligation, review, and report.
why: Advisers, partners, and backers can only judge the service honestly if they can see the whole loop work.
doneWhen: The demo walks through all seven steps end to end and is clearly labeled as simulated.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Write secure sticker/code specification
what: Specify the secure sticker and code scheme — format, serialization, anti-forgery, and scan behavior.
why: The sticker is the promise in physical form; its codes have to resist forgery and duplication before anyone relies on them.
doneWhen: The specification is written, reviewed for security properties, and accepted by print and engineering owners.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Produce and verify a physical print proof
what: Get a physical print proof of the secure sticker from a print partner and verify it against the specification, including scan reliability.
why: No claim about the product is honest until a real sticker exists and scans in the real world.
doneWhen: A physical proof exists, scans reliably, matches the spec, and the result is logged as evidence.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Pass Phase 1 exit review: physical and workflow proof exists
what: Verify the Phase 1 exit: physical and workflow proof exists, campaign materials distinguish simulated from real, and advisers/platform can evaluate the offering.
why: Backers and platforms deserve materials that never blur what is demonstrated versus what is imagined.
doneWhen: Exit conditions are checked with evidence links and the simulated-vs-real distinction is verified in every campaign material.

title: Publish Wefunder Investor Warmup profile
what: Stand up the Wefunder Investor Warmup profile for the issuer entity.
why: A warm audience discovers the story early, so launch day rests on real interest instead of a cold start.
doneWhen: The Warmup profile is live on Wefunder under the issuer entity.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Build bottom-up budget
what: Construct the budget from the bottom up — actual costs per workstream, not top-down guesses.
why: An honest budget protects investors' money and prevents quiet cuts to participant and safety commitments later.
doneWhen: The bottom-up budget is complete, itemized by workstream, and reviewed by finance and the product owner.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Produce SAFE recommendation
what: Get a counsel-backed recommendation on SAFE terms for the community round.
why: Fair, well-understood terms respect the small investors the round is built for.
doneWhen: A written SAFE recommendation with proposed terms is delivered by counsel and accepted by the founders.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Draft campaign risks disclosure
what: Write the risks section for the campaign — what could go wrong, stated plainly.
why: People investing small checks deserve the same candor about risk as institutional investors get.
doneWhen: The risks document is drafted and reviewed by counsel for the campaign package.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Build perk bill of materials
what: Itemize the bill of materials and fulfillment cost for every campaign perk.
why: Perks that are costed honestly get delivered; perks that aren't become broken promises to backers.
doneWhen: Every perk has a costed BOM including fulfillment, reconciled against the budget.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Storyboard the campaign page and video
what: Create the storyboard for the campaign page and video, distinguishing simulated demonstrations from real capability.
why: The story earns trust only if what backers see is exactly what exists.
doneWhen: Page and video storyboards are complete, with every simulated element explicitly labeled, and approved by the product owner.

source.section: Phase 1 — Prove the service and campaign story

title: Assemble platform-approved issuer and Form C package
what: Complete platform issuer approval and assemble the Form C package: SAFE, terms, risks, and use of proceeds.
why: Regulatory honesty is the floor of respect owed to every person who invests.
doneWhen: The issuer is platform-approved and the Form C package with SAFE, terms, risks, and use of proceeds is filed-ready.

title: Prepare campaign financial statements
what: Prepare the financial statements required for the Form C filing with the CPA.
why: Accurate numbers let investors judge the project on the truth.
doneWhen: Financial statements meeting Form C requirements are prepared and CPA-reviewed.

title: Produce final campaign page, media, FAQ, and communications
what: Build the final campaign page, produce the media assets, write the FAQ, and prepare the communications plan.
why: Clear, honest materials let backers decide with open eyes.
doneWhen: Page, media, FAQ, and communications are complete, counsel-reviewed, and platform-accepted.

title: Run six-week audience warmup and confirm launch readiness
what: Execute the six-week audience warmup program and complete the launch readiness checklist before going live.
why: A prepared launch protects the round — and everyone counting on it — from dying quietly in week one.
doneWhen: Six weeks of warmup activity are logged and the launch readiness checklist is fully green.

title: Operate the live Wefunder round
what: Run the live round: publish approved updates, support investors, and enforce material-change controls.
why: Every investor question answered well and every update kept accurate honors the trust being extended.
doneWhen: The round runs to close with all updates approved before posting, investor support handled, and material-change controls followed without exception.

title: Run daily campaign-period health review
what: During the campaign, review support, security, safety, financial, inventory, and platform health daily.
why: Daily eyes on the round catch small problems before they become broken commitments.
doneWhen: A daily review log exists for every campaign day covering all six health areas.

title: Complete post-close funds receipt, accounting, and investor reporting
what: Receive and account for proceeds after close and stand up investor reporting.
why: The moment money arrives is the moment stewardship starts being measured.
doneWhen: Funds are received and booked, and the first investor report is delivered.

title: Pass financing gate: usable proceeds cover the approved beta scenario
what: Confirm usable proceeds after fees and reserves cover the approved beta scenario, and get finance approval of the build/pilot budget.
why: Starting a build the money can't finish would betray participants, partners, and investors alike.
doneWhen: Finance signs off that net proceeds cover the approved beta scenario and approves the build/pilot budget.

title: Execute disclosed shortfall plan if the minimum is not reached
what: If the round misses its minimum, execute the disclosed shortfall plan rather than silently reducing participant or safety commitments.
why: A hard moment handled transparently protects the people the project exists to serve.
doneWhen: Either the minimum is reached, or the disclosed shortfall plan is executed and the decision is recorded with investor communication.

title: Ship revised migrations for the full transaction domain
what: Deliver revised database migrations covering organizations, roles, consent, custody, audit, idempotency, obligation/execution/correction, support/incident, and claims evidence.
why: The schema is where consent, custody, and every dollar owed get made real and auditable.
doneWhen: Migrations for all listed domains are merged and applied cleanly in staging.

title: Write RLS and database invariant tests
what: Build automated tests for row-level security policies and database invariants.
why: Tested boundaries are what keep one person's data from ever being another person's to see.
doneWhen: RLS and invariant test suites run in CI and pass, including denial cases.

title: Build authentication/account lifecycle with MFA for privileged users
what: Implement authentication and full account lifecycle, with MFA required for privileged users.
why: Privileged access guards money and participant data; it must not hang on a single password.
doneWhen: Account lifecycle works end to end in staging and privileged logins are blocked without MFA.

title: Build operator shell and role-specific navigation
what: Deliver the operator application shell with navigation scoped to each role.
why: People see only what their role needs, which protects both them and everyone in the data.
doneWhen: The operator shell renders role-correct navigation for every defined role in staging.

title: Build sticker batch/code export, activation, assignment, custody, void/recall, and audit
what: Implement the full sticker code lifecycle: batch/code export, activation, assignment, custody tracking, void/recall, and audit trail.
why: Knowing where every sticker is and whether it's live is how the system stays honest about what's in the world.
doneWhen: Each lifecycle operation works in staging with a complete audit record for every state change.

title: Build public scan, offer reveal, claim, expiry, and help routes
what: Implement the public-facing routes: scan, offer reveal, claim, expiry handling, and help.
why: This is the moment a recipient meets the service; it has to work simply and with dignity.
doneWhen: All five public routes work in staging on real devices, including expiry and help paths.

title: Pass Phase 2 exit: synthetic end-to-end flow with security tests
what: Prove the synthetic end-to-end flow in staging with denial tests, concurrency/idempotency tests, and verification that no service-role credential reaches the browser.
why: A tested foundation is what makes it safe to ever put a real person's claim through the system.
doneWhen: The end-to-end staging flow passes with denial and concurrency/idempotency suites green and a verified absence of service-role credentials in the browser.

title: Build business qualification/onboarding with location/staff access, offer templates, and custom review
what: Implement business qualification and onboarding, location and staff access management, offer templates, and the custom-offer review path.
why: Well-vetted businesses with clear offers are what make a recipient's sticker actually worth something.
doneWhen: A business can be qualified, onboarded, granted location/staff access, and publish a templated or reviewed custom offer in staging.

title: Build authenticated no-POS redemption verification with idempotent replay and dispute/correction
what: Implement redemption verification that needs no POS integration, with authenticated staff confirmation, idempotent replay protection, and a dispute/correction path.
why: The counter moment must be fast and fair for staff and never humiliating for the person redeeming.
doneWhen: Staging demonstrates authenticated redemption without POS, replay attempts that cannot double-redeem, and a working dispute/correction flow.

title: Build transactional obligation creation under a versioned simulated/approved formula
what: Implement obligation creation as a transaction governed by a versioned formula, clearly flagged simulated versus approved.
why: What a business is owed must be computed by rules everyone can see and audit, never by hand-waving.
doneWhen: A redemption creates its obligation transactionally under a versioned formula, with the simulated/approved state explicit.

title: Build finance review, dual approval, manual execution evidence, failure/reissue, and reconciliation
what: Implement the finance workflow: obligation review, dual approval, manual execution with evidence capture, failure/reissue handling, and reconciliation.
why: Two sets of eyes and captured evidence on every payout is how businesses learn they can trust the promise.
doneWhen: The full finance workflow runs in staging: no execution without dual approval, evidence attached, failures reissuable, and reconciliation balancing.

title: Build distributor statement and sponsor aggregate report
what: Implement the distributor statement and the sponsor-facing aggregate report.
why: The people funding and handing out stickers deserve to see, truthfully, what their generosity did.
doneWhen: Both reports generate correctly from staging data and reconcile with the underlying records.

title: Build fraud/risk queue and appeal path
what: Implement the fraud/risk review queue and an appeal path for flagged cases.
why: Fraud controls protect the fund, and the appeal path protects innocent people from the controls.
doneWhen: Flagged cases enter the queue, can be resolved, and a flagged person can appeal and be reinstated in staging.

title: Pass Phase 3 exit: balanced obligations, replay-proof, history-preserving corrections, bounded roles
what: Prove with automated tests that one effective redemption creates one balanced obligation set, replay cannot duplicate, corrections preserve history, and role access is bounded.
why: These four properties are the difference between a ledger businesses can trust and one they can't.
doneWhen: Automated tests for all four properties pass in CI and are linked as evidence.

title: Build participant eligibility, status, training, consent, accommodations, support, complaint, suspension, appeal, and exit
what: Implement the full participant lifecycle: eligibility/status, training, consent, accommodations, support, complaint, suspension, appeal, and exit.
why: Every stage of a participant's journey — including leaving — should be handled with dignity and due process.
doneWhen: Each lifecycle stage works in staging, with consent recorded and suspension always paired with a working appeal.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Build partner-assisted and printable/no-smartphone flows
what: Implement flows that work through a partner's assistance and printable paths for people without smartphones.
why: The people most likely to be excluded by a phone requirement are the people this project exists for.
doneWhen: A participant can complete the core flow via partner assistance or printed materials, verified in staging.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Build incident and restricted-note controls
what: Implement incident recording and restricted-note controls limiting who can write and read sensitive notes about participants.
why: Sensitive observations about vulnerable people must never be casually visible.
doneWhen: Incident records and restricted notes enforce their access rules in staging, verified by denial tests.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Complete WCAG 2.2 AA remediation and device/low-bandwidth testing
what: Remediate the product to WCAG 2.2 AA and test on real devices and low-bandwidth connections.
why: Accessibility is not a feature here — the intended users disproportionately rely on old devices, weak connections, and assistive tech.
doneWhen: WCAG 2.2 AA audit passes and the device/low-bandwidth test matrix is completed with results logged.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Build privacy data map, retention jobs, and data-subject request workflow
what: Produce the privacy data map, implement automated retention jobs, and build the data-subject request workflow.
