why: People trusting the system with their situation deserve to know their data is mapped, expiring on schedule, and theirs to reclaim.
doneWhen: The data map is complete, retention jobs run and delete on schedule, and a data-subject request completes end to end in staging.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Ensure every shipped capability has a trained owner and backup
what: For each capability shipped during the build, confirm a named owner and backup can operate it, as the definition of done requires.
why: Tools nobody is trained on become single points of failure the moment real people depend on them.
doneWhen: Each shipped capability has a named owner and backup who can demonstrate operating it, recorded as evidence.

title: Run security/incident tabletop exercise
what: Conduct a tabletop exercise walking through security and incident scenarios with the response owners.
why: The first time the team handles a breach scenario should be a rehearsal, not the real thing.
doneWhen: The tabletop is run, findings are logged, and resulting fixes are assigned.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Rehearse inventory receipt, transfer, return, loss, and damage handling
what: Run a rehearsal of the physical inventory workflow: receipt, transfer, return, loss, and damage.
why: Physical stickers are the trust object; losing track of them means losing track of promises.
doneWhen: All five inventory scenarios are rehearsed with the system matching physical counts, and gaps are logged.

source.section: Phase 4 — Partner operations, safety, and accessibility

title: Obtain pilot readiness sign-off from all six owners
what: Collect pilot-readiness sign-off from the partner, safety, privacy/security, accessibility, support, and product owners after critical scenarios pass rehearsal.
why: Six named people saying 'ready' is the guardrail between rehearsal and real participants.
doneWhen: All six sign-offs are recorded and every critical scenario has a passing rehearsal result.

title: Stand up production environment with backups/restore, alerts, runbooks, access review, and migration/rollback
what: Provision production with tested backups and restore, alerting, runbooks, an access review, and migration/rollback procedures.
why: When real claims are live, recovery has to be a practiced procedure, not an improvisation.
doneWhen: Production exists with a passing restore test, active alerts, current runbooks, a completed access review, and documented migration/rollback.

source.section: Phase 6 — Production candidate and pilot rehearsal

title: Complete final printer run with batch acceptance sampling
what: Execute the final production printer run and accept the batch via acceptance sampling.
why: Every sticker handed to a person must scan; sampling is how that promise gets checked at scale.
doneWhen: The final run is delivered and the batch passes the defined acceptance sampling with results logged.

source.section: Phase 6 — Production candidate and pilot rehearsal

title: Run full synthetic dress rehearsal of the entire loop
what: Execute the full synthetic dress rehearsal: partner onboarding, pack issue, scan, claim, business redemption, obligation, review/execution evidence, reconciliation, report, and dispute/reversal.
why: The whole chain has to work as one motion before a single real person is asked to rely on it.
doneWhen: Every step of the rehearsal completes in sequence with evidence captured, including the dispute/reversal path.

source.section: Phase 6 — Production candidate and pilot rehearsal

title: Complete business and partner training
what: Deliver and verify completion of training for participating businesses and community partners.
why: Trained staff and partners are what make the counter moment and the handoff moment go well for real people.
doneWhen: Every participating business and partner has a recorded training completion before pilot start.

source.section: Phase 6 — Production candidate and pilot rehearsal

title: Establish support schedule and incident bridge
what: Set the pilot support coverage schedule and stand up the incident bridge for escalations.
why: Someone reachable when things go wrong is part of the promise to participants and businesses.
doneWhen: The support schedule is published with named coverage and the incident bridge is tested end to end.

source.section: Phase 6 — Production candidate and pilot rehearsal

title: Pass Phase 6 exit gate: zero critical/high issues, approvals active, smoke and restore pass
what: Verify zero critical/high unresolved release issues, all required approvals and signed agreements active, and passing production smoke and restore tests.
why: This gate is the last chance to catch a problem before it reaches a real participant.
doneWhen: The exit checklist is fully evidenced: no open critical/high issues, all approvals and agreements active, smoke and restore green.

title: Start beta with one partner, one business/location, one small pack, and a limited distributor cohort
what: Launch the Austin beta at minimum scope: one partner, one business/location, one small sticker pack, and a limited active distributor cohort.
why: Starting small means any failure hurts the fewest people and can be fixed fastest.
doneWhen: The beta is live within exactly the defined scope and the starting scope is recorded.

title: Run daily beta review of safety, fraud, support, inventory, redemption, obligations, and system health
what: Review safety, fraud, support, inventory, redemption, obligations, and system health every day of the beta.
why: Daily attention is how a small problem gets caught before it touches a vulnerable person.
doneWhen: A daily review record exists for every beta day covering all seven areas.

title: Expand toward the approved ceiling only after each cohort gate passes
what: Grow the beta cohort by cohort, expanding only when the previous cohort's gate criteria pass, and never past the approved ceiling.
why: Gated growth keeps ambition from outrunning the team's ability to care for each person in the program.
doneWhen: Every expansion step has a passing cohort-gate record, and the cohort never exceeds the approved ceiling.

title: Conduct voluntary participant research
what: Run research with beta participants on a strictly voluntary basis.
why: Participants' experiences should shape the product — but only when they freely choose to share them.
doneWhen: Research sessions are conducted with documented voluntary consent and findings are logged.

title: Run weekly financial reconciliation during beta
what: Reconcile obligations, executions, and inventory weekly throughout the beta.
why: Weekly reconciliation means no business waits long for money it's owed and no discrepancy hides.
doneWhen: A balanced reconciliation record exists for every beta week, with discrepancies investigated.

title: Publish no impact claim until evidence and privacy thresholds pass
what: Hold all public impact claims until the evidence standard and privacy thresholds are both met.
why: Overstated impact would spend the credibility the project needs to actually help people.
doneWhen: The claims register shows no published impact claim without a passing evidence and privacy threshold check.

title: Produce beta evidence report
what: Compile the beta evidence report covering loop completion, reconciliation, safety, usability, business, and partner results.
why: The decision about people's continued participation deserves to rest on evidence, not enthusiasm.
doneWhen: The evidence report is complete with linked evidence for every criterion.

title: Produce remediation backlog from beta findings
what: Convert every beta finding into a prioritized remediation backlog.
why: Problems found during beta are only worth finding if they get fixed before more people depend on the system.
doneWhen: The remediation backlog exists with every beta finding triaged and prioritized.

title: Deliver post-beta investor update
what: Send investors an honest update on beta results and the go-forward decision.
why: The people who funded the beta deserve the truth about what it proved, first.
doneWhen: The investor update is delivered through the approved channel and archived.

title: Publish public aggregate report if approved
what: Decide whether the public aggregate report meets the evidence and privacy bar, and publish it only if approved.
why: The public story must meet the same evidence and privacy standard as everything else.
doneWhen: The publish/hold decision is recorded, and if published, the report passed evidence and privacy review first.

title: Produce production operations budget
what: Build the operations budget for running the service beyond the beta.
why: A service people rely on needs a funded plan to keep existing, not just to launch.
doneWhen: The production operations budget is complete and finance-reviewed.

title: Make the continue/revise/pause/stop decision against the defined criteria
what: Decide the project's path — continue, revise, pause, or stop — strictly against the defined outcome criteria.
why: Being willing to pause or stop is the deepest protection the project offers the people it serves.
doneWhen: The decision is recorded with the criteria evaluation that produced it.

title: Decide on Austin expansion or future city readiness
what: Record the decision on expanding within Austin or preparing readiness for a future city.
why: Expansion should be a deliberate choice backed by evidence, not momentum.
doneWhen: The expansion/readiness decision is documented with its supporting evidence.

title: Establish and track the Christmas 2026 critical path with parallel workstreams
what: Set up tracking so entity/platform preparation, physical proof, partner/business outreach, and engineering run in parallel, with financing arriving by the beta-build decision, and the disclosed fallback of pilot-ready or controlled-beta status by Christmas with exit in January.
why: Holding the schedule honestly — including the rule that it never overrides safety, financial classification, securities, privacy, or partner gates — protects everyone from a deadline-driven shortcut.
doneWhen: The critical path is documented with parallel workstreams, the financing dependency, the fallback, and the gates-over-schedule rule, and is reviewed weekly.

title: Establish the weekly operating rhythm
what: Institute the weekly cadence: Monday critical path/risks/decisions/budget/dependencies, Tuesday–Thursday workstream delivery with test evidence, Friday integrated demo, claims review, release gate, and stakeholder update.
why: A steady rhythm keeps risks, claims, and decisions visible to the whole team every single week.
doneWhen: The rhythm has run for two consecutive weeks with Monday planning notes and Friday demo/claims/gate/update records.

title: Adopt the definition of done in the repository process
what: Encode the definition of done — acceptance test passes, documentation/runbook current, accessibility/security/privacy effects reviewed, owner and backup can operate it, evidence linked, merged through the approved process — into the team's working agreement and merge process.
why: A shared bar for 'done' is how quality stops depending on who happened to do the work.
doneWhen: The definition of done is documented in the repo and enforced as the merge checklist.

title: Establish one prioritized backlog of vertical, independently testable slices
what: Create the single prioritized backlog, organized as vertical slices that can each be tested independently, with every work package ending in review evidence and a release decision.
why: One honest backlog keeps the whole team pulling on the same priorities instead of parallel private plans.
doneWhen: The prioritized backlog exists, work is sliced vertically and independently testable, and each completed work package shows review evidence and a recorded release decision.

title: Enforce that no real financial or participant-impacting behavior activates before its gates pass
what: Adopt and enforce the rule that engineering may proceed in parallel, but nothing with real financial or participant impact is switched on until its professional and operational gates have passed.
why: This rule is the hard line that keeps speed from ever putting a vulnerable person or real money at risk.
doneWhen: The rule is recorded in the decision register and every activation of real-money or participant-impacting behavior links to its passed gate.

title: Review every readable concept asset three times and document findings
what: Complete a three-pass review of all locally readable and recovered concept/source assets with documented notes.
why: The whole plan is only trustworthy if it stands on what the source material actually says.
doneWhen: Every recovered concept asset has three documented review passes on file.

title: Conduct and formally close the full founder interview
what: Run the complete direction interview with Chrix and formally close it as the decision record.
why: Founder intent has to be captured once, clearly, so no one later guesses at it.
doneWhen: The interview record exists and is formally marked closed by Chrix.

title: Deliver the Product Decision Record (PDR)
what: Write and finalize the PDR capturing the approved product principles.
why: Everyone building Worth Keeping deserves one written source of what was decided and why.
doneWhen: The PDR document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Project Scope document
what: Write and finalize the project scope, including the pilot ceilings and exclusions.
why: A clear boundary protects the pilot from quietly growing past what the team can run safely.
doneWhen: The Project Scope document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Technical Requirements document
what: Write and finalize the technical requirements (TR-01 through TR-06 and related).
why: Privacy, integrity, and accessibility promises only hold if they are written down as requirements first.
doneWhen: The Technical Requirements document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Recommended Tech Stack document
what: Write and finalize the recommended stack (Next.js/TypeScript/Supabase/Vercel/GitHub) with rationale.
why: A named, boring, supportable stack keeps funded build time going to the mission instead of tooling debates.
doneWhen: The Recommended Tech Stack document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Required Assets document
what: Write and finalize the inventory of required creative, print, and campaign assets.
why: Knowing every asset up front means no one discovers a missing piece the week of launch.
doneWhen: The Required Assets document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Platforms document
what: Write and finalize the platforms document covering hosting, crowdfunding, and operational platforms.
why: Platform choices carry cost and compliance weight, so they need to be documented before money moves.
doneWhen: The Platforms document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Subscriptions and Services document
what: Write and finalize the subscriptions and services inventory with costs.
why: Every recurring dollar should be visible before the budget is promised to backers.
doneWhen: The Subscriptions and Services document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the Crowdfunding Plan, assets, and recommendations
what: Write and finalize the crowdfunding plan including assets and recommendations.
why: The community round has to start from a plan, not improvisation, because backers' trust is on the line.
doneWhen: The Crowdfunding Plan document with assets and recommendations is complete and checked off.

title: Deliver the Required Tools document with installation and setup
what: Write and finalize the tools inventory including installation and setup instructions.
why: A written setup path lets any teammate get productive without folklore.
doneWhen: The Required Tools document with installation/setup steps is complete and checked off.

title: Deliver the Required Skills document with training and evidence
what: Write and finalize the required-skills inventory with training paths and evidence criteria.
why: Naming the skills honestly shows where the team needs help before the pilot depends on it.
doneWhen: The Required Skills document with training/evidence is complete and checked off.

title: Deliver the Implementation Roadmap
what: Write and finalize the phased implementation roadmap.
why: A sequenced roadmap keeps the mission moving without skipping the gates that protect people.
doneWhen: The Implementation Roadmap document is complete and checked off in the package checklist.

title: Deliver the traceability and consistency matrix
what: Write and finalize the requirement traceability matrix, professional-gate register, and consistency rules.
why: Traceability means every promise can be tracked to its source and its proof.
doneWhen: The traceability and consistency matrix document is complete and checked off.

title: Implement the HTML Infinity Page and complete visual QA
what: Build the standalone HTML Infinity Page summarizing plan and status, with desktop/mobile/section visual QA plus browser, accessibility, print, and link QA.
why: One honest, accessible page lets anyone see where Worth Keeping actually stands.
doneWhen: The Infinity Page is implemented and desktop, mobile, and per-section visual QA plus browser/accessibility/print/link QA are logged.

source.section: Requirement traceability — VIS-01 / Package completion checklist

title: Approve the give-first three-party service blueprint
what: Approve the service blueprint/content establishing that the distributor gives first and all three parties receive clear value.
why: The dignity-first exchange is the heart of the product and had to be locked before anything else.
doneWhen: The approved service blueprint/content exists and PDR-01 is marked an approved principle.

title: Close the entity/securities professional gate
what: With securities counsel, establish the issuer entity, cap table, ownership records, chosen instrument, Form C/platform approval, and advertising/promoter compliance rules, producing the entity and market documents.
why: No investment communication or dollar of backer money is safe to touch until the legal container exists.
doneWhen: Issuer, cap table, ownership, instrument, Form C/platform approval, and advertising/promoter rules are documented and counsel confirms investment communications may begin.

title: Pin all dependencies and remove `latest` specifiers
what: Pin every dependency version in the repo, remove any `latest` specifiers, and verify a clean lockfile and CI build.
why: A reproducible build protects the small team from surprise breakage at the worst moment.
doneWhen: No `latest` specifiers remain, the lockfile is clean, and CI builds green from it.

title: Adopt the ten consistency rules as the claims-control policy
what: Formally adopt the consistency rules — placeholder terms until FIN gates close, no unpermissioned names, visuals never claimed as proof, internal-only scenario numbers, Christmas-with-January-fallback framing, counsel-approved investment terms, reducible beta ceiling, no value from scan alone, no personal-data sale, distinct Infinity Page status labels — as binding policy for all public material.
why: These rules are what keep every public word about Worth Keeping honest to backers and participants.
doneWhen: The consistency rules are adopted in the claims-control policy and every content owner has acknowledged them.

title: Produce the brand/co-brand guide for platform-owned collectible art
what: Create the brand and co-brand guide establishing platform-owned collectible art with limited attribution terms.
why: Clear art ownership and attribution rules protect both the artists and the platform before anything is printed.
doneWhen: The brand/co-brand guide is approved by the creative owner and covers ownership and limited attribution.

title: Secure a signed print proof and QR test matrix before production
what: Obtain a print proof from the print partner, run the QR code test matrix against it, and get the proof signed off before any production run.
why: A sticker that fails to scan in someone's hand breaks the entire promise at the moment it matters most.
doneWhen: A signed proof and a completed QR test matrix are on file and QA has approved production readiness.

title: Close the participant-value classification gate
what: Obtain the professional legal/tax/benefits/employment/KYC/sanctions classification of participant value, a written memo, and the approved public term to replace placeholders like "micro-grant" and "payout".
why: Getting this classification right protects vulnerable participants from tax, benefits, and legal harm.
doneWhen: A written classification memo exists and the approved public term is adopted, unblocking real obligation and disbursement language.

title: Close the funds custody and accounting gate
what: Obtain the written operating decision covering funds custody, revenue/restriction treatment, chart of accounts, approvals, refunds, reconciliation, KYC/tax documentation, and payment rails.
why: Money entrusted by backers and sponsors must have a professionally designed home before a single dollar arrives.
doneWhen: A written operating decision on custody, treatment, chart, approvals, refunds, reconciliation, and tax docs is signed, unblocking receipt and use of funds.

title: Evaluate Wefunder Community Round and decide platform and instrument
what: Complete the evaluation of the Wefunder Community Round and potential investment return, and record the platform/instrument decision with counsel.
why: Choosing the right platform and instrument determines what can honestly be promised to community investors.
doneWhen: A documented platform and instrument decision, made with counsel, is on file.

title: Execute counsel-approved SAFE terms with investor perks and no guaranteed return
what: Finalize and execute platform-approved SAFE terms including investor perks, using current signed platform documents and counsel approval, with no guaranteed return stated anywhere.
why: Investors deserve terms that are legally sound and honest about risk, not terms lifted from a plan document.
doneWhen: Executed platform-approved SAFE terms are on file, approved by counsel at launch.

title: Approve the campaign page and use-of-proceeds promising a pilot-ready beta
what: Finalize the approved campaign page and use-of-proceeds statement committing funds to a pilot-ready beta and a controlled Austin pilot, with scenario amounts sourced and approved before publication.
why: Backers should know exactly what their money builds and see only numbers the team can stand behind.
doneWhen: The campaign page and use-of-proceeds are approved with sourced amounts, matching the pilot-ready-beta promise.

source.section: Requirement traceability — CFI-03 / SCP-03 / Consistency rules #4

title: Select at most five investor perks and approve their costed BOM
what: Choose a maximum of five investor perks from the 35-option menu and get the costed perk bill of materials approved by campaign and finance.
why: Perks that are few, costed, and deliverable protect backers from broken promises and the budget from surprises.
doneWhen: No more than five perks are selected and their costed BOM has campaign and finance approval.

title: Close the print/fulfillment gate for production orders and campaign perks
what: Complete the print quote, purchase order, packaging plan, landed-cost calculation, and replacement process on top of the signed proof and code test, before any production order or physical campaign perk ships.
why: Every physical perk is a promise to a backer, and fulfillment math has to work before that promise is made.
doneWhen: Quote, proof, code test, PO, packaging, landed costs, and replacement process are all documented and approved.

title: Audit all campaign and public materials against the consistency rules
what: Before publication, audit every campaign page, video, perk description, and public document against the ten consistency rules — placeholder terms, no unpermissioned names, visuals-as-illustration, sourced numbers, date framing, counsel-approved terms.
why: This audit is the last line of defense keeping Worth Keeping's public story exactly as honest as its plan.
doneWhen: An audit record shows every public asset checked against all ten rules with violations fixed before publication.

title: Build state/ledger tests and SOP proving verified redemption is the only money-moving event
what: Implement state and ledger tests plus the operating SOP demonstrating that a verified redemption is the only candidate money-moving event in the system.
why: Participants and backers can trust the money flow only if the system provably cannot move value any other way.
doneWhen: State/ledger tests pass in CI and the SOP is approved, together evidencing PDR-02.

title: Pass the staff-verification end-to-end test with no POS integration
what: Build and pass an end-to-end test of the staff-verification redemption flow, confirming the beta works without any POS integration.
why: Small businesses can say yes to the pilot only if it asks nothing of their point-of-sale systems.
doneWhen: The staff-verification E2E test passes and is logged as acceptance evidence for SCP-02.

title: Pass consent and data-flow tests for anonymous-first public scan
what: Implement and pass tests proving public scans are anonymous by default and any profile exists only through explicit consent.
why: People experiencing homelessness should never have to trade their privacy to receive help.
doneWhen: Consent and data-flow tests pass and are recorded as acceptance evidence for TR-01.

title: Pass RLS and API denial tests for server-authorized writes
what: Implement and pass row-level-security and API denial tests proving all writes are server-authorized and unauthorized paths are rejected.
why: The ledger's integrity — and every obligation it records — depends on no one writing to it uninvited.
doneWhen: RLS and API denial tests pass in CI as acceptance evidence for TR-02.

title: Pass concurrency, replay, and invariant tests for idempotent redemption
what: Implement and pass concurrency, replay, and invariant tests proving each redemption is idempotent and creates exactly one obligation set.
why: Double-paying or double-charging anyone even once would break trust that took months to earn.
doneWhen: Concurrency/replay/invariant tests pass in CI as acceptance evidence for TR-03.

title: Pass mutation-denial and reversal tests for the append-only audit trail
what: Implement and pass tests proving audit records cannot be mutated and corrections happen only via append-only reversals.
why: An untouchable audit trail is how the team proves — to auditors and to itself — that every action was honest.
doneWhen: Mutation denial and reversal tests pass as acceptance evidence for TR-05.

title: Record the stack architecture decision and prove it with working builds
what: Record the architecture decision adopting Next.js/TypeScript/Supabase/Vercel/GitHub and demonstrate working builds on that stack.
why: A recorded decision plus a green build turns a recommendation into a foundation the team can rely on.
doneWhen: The architecture decision is documented and the application builds successfully on the chosen stack.

title: Verify no production AI dependency exists in the core beta
what: Audit the beta build to confirm production AI is excluded from core flows and no beta-critical AI dependency exists.
why: Core promises to participants must not depend on systems the team cannot fully control or explain.
doneWhen: A dependency audit confirms no beta-critical AI dependency, evidencing STK-03.

title: Close the privacy/security professional gate before handling real data
what: Produce the data map, privacy notice and terms, consent flows, retention schedule, processor list, incident duties, and threat model required before any real personal or transaction data is handled.
why: The people this project serves are exactly the people most harmed if their data is mishandled.
doneWhen: Data map, notice/terms, consent, retention, processors, incident duties, and threat model are all approved, unblocking real data.

title: Approve the pilot cohort/batch plan within the planning ceiling
what: Produce and approve the cohort and batch plan honoring the pilot ceiling of 10 distributors, 3 businesses/sponsors, and 1,000 stickers, noting gate owners may reduce it.
why: A small, deliberate cohort keeps the first pilot humane and recoverable if something goes wrong.
doneWhen: An approved cohort/batch plan exists at or below the ceiling, signed by Chrix and the partner.

title: Rehearse human-reviewed beta execution with dual control
what: Run a full approval, execution, and reconciliation rehearsal proving every beta money-adjacent action is human-reviewed under dual control.
why: Two sets of human eyes on every disbursement protects participants, sponsors, and the team alike.
doneWhen: The approval/execution/reconciliation rehearsal is completed and logged as acceptance evidence for TR-04.

title: Produce WCAG 2.2 AA and low-bandwidth/assisted-path evidence
what: Complete automated, manual, and real-device accessibility testing proving WCAG 2.2 AA conformance and working low-bandwidth and assisted paths.
why: The service must work for people on old phones, weak signal, or using assistance — that is who it is for.
doneWhen: Automated, manual, and device test evidence for WCAG 2.2 AA and low-bandwidth/assisted paths is on file for TR-06.

title: Close the partner/safeguarding gate before any participant onboarding
what: Secure the signed partner role/SLA agreement and complete eligibility criteria, consent processes, training, incident procedures, complaints, appeals, and exit paths — including the no-story-for-access policy — then rehearse them.
why: No real participant should be onboarded until the safety net around them is signed, trained, and rehearsed.
doneWhen: Signed agreement, all safeguarding policies, and a completed rehearsal are on file, unblocking participant onboarding and field activity.

title: Close the insurance and Austin local-operations gate
what: Obtain the required insurance policies including events/field coverage, and document Austin operating restrictions, before public distribution or a launch event.
why: Insurance and local compliance protect volunteers, participants, and partners when the work goes public.
doneWhen: Required policies and events/field coverage are bound and Austin operating restrictions are documented, unblocking public distribution.
